We further propose a counterfactual crop-black setting, which replaces crops returned by tools with black images of the same size, to test whether tool-enabled performance truly relies on intermediate visual evidence. We evaluate 9 promninent MLLMs across three settings: no-tool, standard tool-enabled, and crop-black. No-tool accuracy stays below 20\%, and the best tool-enabled model reaches only 56.01\%, still trailing the 63.00% human majority-vote accuracy. These results reveal persistent limitations in fine-grained visual search, while the crop-black ablation confirms that success on VisualNeedle hinges on genuine intermediate visual evidence.

AI.tldr: Despite MLLMs scoring >90% on fine-grained benchmarks, they rely on shortcuts like linguistic priors and global semantics rather than genuine visual search. The authors introduce VisualNeedle, a challenging benchmark requiring evidence from tiny image regions, plus a counterfactual crop-black setting to test reliance on intermediate visual evidence. Results show top models reach only 56% accuracy (vs. 63% human), revealing persistent limitations in fine-grained visual search.
AI.motivation: Existing fine-grained perception benchmarks inflate MLLM performance through three shortcuts: linguistic/lexical cues, coarse global semantics bypassing local details, and tool-use benchmarks where intermediate image corruption barely affects answers. This motivates the need for a benchmark that truly tests active visual search rather than reliance on priors or global features.
AI.method: VisualNeedle is designed as an information-dense, fine-grained benchmark where critical evidence is spatially constrained to minute regions not discernible at a glance. A counterfactual crop-black setting replaces tool-returned crops with same-size black images to test whether tool-enabled performance genuinely depends on intermediate visual evidence. Evaluated across 9 MLLMs under three settings: no-tool, standard tool-enabled, and crop-black.
AI.result: No-tool accuracy stays below 20%; best tool-enabled model reaches only 56.01% vs. 63.00% human majority-vote accuracy. Crop-black ablation confirms success hinges on genuine intermediate visual evidence.
AI.conclusion: Higher resolution or larger question pools alone do not elicit genuine active visual search. Persistent limitations in fine-grained visual search remain in MLLMs, and the proposed crop-black setting effectively validates reliance on actual visual evidence.

title: On the Role of Inductive Bias in Time-Series Pretraining: A Case Study in Learning Generalizable Representations for Clinical Time Series
summary: Clinical time-series learning is routinely constrained by small, heterogeneous cohorts and protocol drift, while its downstream use spans both classification (e.g., pathology diagnosis) and regression (e.g., temporal forecasting). These constraints make foundation-model pretraining appealing, but raises an important question of which inductive biases should the pretraining objective impose so that representations transfer across task types and subjects. We study this question in pathological gait analysis for spinal cord injury (SCI) via PathoFM, an encoder-centric transformer pretrained on multivariate gait windows with three complementary objectives: Local Completion (reconstruct contiguous masked spans to enforce local structure), Temporal Continuity (predict a masked mid-horizon continuation from an observed prefix to enforce smoothness and causal consistency), and Unsupervised In-Context Dynamics (support-query reconstruction conditioned on subject exemplar windows via attention). Empirically comparing objective families (grouping/contrastive, dynamics-based, and generative reconstruction), we find that dynamics-centric mixtures produce the most balanced transfer: grouping objectives favor discriminative margins but can degrade magnitude fidelity needed for continuous targets, whereas reconstruction-only objectives preserve waveform structure but may underperform on classification. Overall, combining local reconstruction with temporal continuity, and adding in-context conditioning when exemplar access is realistic, yields robust subject-generalizing representations.

AI.tldr: PathoFM is an encoder-centric transformer pretrained on multivariate gait windows from spinal cord injury patients using three complementary objectives (Local Completion, Temporal Continuity, Unsupervised In-Context Dynamics). Dynamics-centric mixtures achieve the best balanced transfer across classification and regression tasks, outperforming pure grouping/contrastive or reconstruction-only approaches.
AI.motivation: Clinical time-series learning faces constraints from small heterogeneous cohorts and protocol drift, yet must support both classification and regression tasks. This raises the question of which inductive biases in pretraining objectives enable representations that transfer across task types and subjects.
AI.method: PathoFM: an encoder-centric transformer pretrained on multivariate gait windows with three objectives: (1) Local Completion (reconstruct contiguous masked spans), (2) Temporal Continuity (predict masked mid-horizon continuation from observed prefix), (3) Unsupervised In-Context Dynamics (support-query reconstruction conditioned on subject exemplar windows via attention). Empirically compared grouping/contrastive, dynamics-based, and generative reconstruction families.
AI.result: Dynamics-centric mixtures produce the most balanced transfer. Grouping/contrastive objectives favor discriminative margins but degrade magnitude fidelity for continuous targets. Reconstruction-only objectives preserve waveform structure but underperform on classification. Combining local reconstruction with temporal continuity (+ in-context conditioning) yields robust subject-generalizing representations.
AI.conclusion: For clinical time-series foundation models, dynamics-centric pretraining objectives (mixing local reconstruction and temporal continuity) offer the best trade-off for transferring across both classification and regression tasks. In-context conditioning further improves performance when exemplar access is realistic.

title: Advancing Creative Physical Intelligence in Large Multimodal Models
comment: 51 Pages, 9 Figures, 7 Tables, Previous Work CreativityBench: arXiv:2605.02910
summary: Large multimodal models (LMMs) have rapidly advanced in perception and reasoning; however, it remains unclear whether these capabilities generalize to discovering visually grounded solutions in open-ended environments, beyond pattern recognition. In such settings, intelligence requires more than answering well-posed questions: it involves identifying how elements in a scene can be repurposed in non-obvious yet physically feasible ways. This form of creative problem-solving is central to human intelligence, but remains largely untested in current benchmarks. To evaluate this ability, we introduce MM-CreativityBench, a benchmark for affordance-grounded creative tool use in visually rich, physically constrained environments. Each instance presents a scenario image with structured views of candidate entities and their parts, enabling fine-grained, interactive evaluation of how models iteratively inspect the scene, identify relevant affordances, and compose visually and physically grounded solutions. Our experiments show that current LMMs often fall short, not due to lack of generative capability, but because they do not sustain grounded exploration. Models often overlook relevant entities, under-examine critical parts, or hallucinate attributes not grounded in the image. Motivated by this failure mode, we propose affordance-grounded alignment, which casts creative tool use as a preference learning problem. Using Direct Preference Optimization, we encourage models to prefer attribute-affordance reasoning grounded in visual evidence over hallucinated alternatives. In addition, we incorporate supervision derived from an affordance knowledge base to guide broader entity exploration and multi-turn planning. Our results show consistent gains in selecting the correct entities and parts, while substantially reducing hallucination and grounding-related errors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MM-CreativityBench to evaluate LMMs on creative tool use in open-ended environments, finding they fail due to insufficient grounded exploration, and proposes affordance-grounded alignment via DPO to improve visual reasoning and reduce hallucination.
AI.motivation: Current LMM benchmarks focus on well-posed questions and pattern recognition, but do not test creative problem-solving where elements must be repurposed in non-obvious yet physically feasible ways—a core aspect of human intelligence.
AI.method: Introduced MM-CreativityBench (affordance-grounded creative tool use benchmark) and proposed affordance-grounded alignment using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with supervision from an affordance knowledge base to guide entity exploration and multi-turn planning.
AI.result: Current LMMs struggle due to lacking grounded exploration (overlooking entities, under-examining parts, hallucinating attributes). The proposed alignment method yields consistent gains in selecting correct entities/parts and reduces hallucination and grounding errors.
AI.conclusion: LMMs fail at creative tool use not from lack of generative capability but from insufficient grounded exploration; affordance-grounded alignment via preference learning effectively mitigates these failures.

title: Learning to Balance Motor Thermal Safety and Quadrupedal Locomotion Performance with Residual Policy
summary: Motor thermal management is often overlooked in the context of electrically-actuated robots, particularly legged robots, but motor overheating is a key factor that limits long-duration locomotion especially under payload conditions. This paper integrates a whole-body thermal model of a quadruped robot into the reinforcement learning pipeline to update motor temperatures, and proposes a two-stage training framework for motor thermal management. In this framework, a nominal policy is first pre-trained as a locomotion baseline capable of traversing diverse terrains. A residual policy is then trained on top of the nominal policy to provide corrective actions based on the robot's thermal state, ensuring high performance under low-temperature conditions and preventing motor overheating under high-temperature conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed policy achieves an effective balance between motor thermal safety and locomotion performance. Real-world experiments on a Unitree A1 quadruped robot further validate the approach: under a 3 kg payload, the robot achieves stable locomotion across multiple terrains for over 13 minutes, while the nominal policy alone leads to motor overheating in about 5 minutes.

AI.tldr: This paper integrates a whole-body thermal model of a quadruped robot into reinforcement learning to train a two-stage policy (nominal + residual) that balances motor thermal safety with locomotion performance, extending operation under payload from ~5 minutes to over 13 minutes.
AI.motivation: Motor overheating is a key factor limiting long-duration locomotion in electrically-actuated legged robots, especially under payload conditions, yet motor thermal management is often overlooked.
AI.method: A whole-body thermal model is integrated into the RL pipeline. A two-stage training framework first pre-trains a nominal locomotion policy, then trains a residual policy on top that provides corrective thermal-aware actions to prevent overheating.
AI.result: Simulation shows effective balance between thermal safety and locomotion performance. Real-world experiments on Unitree A1 quadruped robot: under 3 kg payload, robot runs over 13 minutes vs. ~5 minutes with nominal policy alone.
AI.conclusion: The proposed thermal-aware residual policy significantly extends locomotion duration under payload while maintaining performance, demonstrating practical value for real-world legged robotics.

title: Multi-Modal Building Inspection via Perceiver IO Fusion of Satellite and Street-Level Imagery
summary: We present a multi-modal classification framework that fuses satellite and street-level imagery through a Perceiver IO architecture operating on spatial patch tokens from a shared DINOv2 backbone. The design naturally handles a variable number of street-level views per building without padding or fixed-size pooling, and jointly predicts multi-label roof element and roof material classes. We construct a large-scale dataset of 32,135 buildings (61,672 segments) spanning ten countries, pairing satellite images with up to eight street-level views per segment and evaluating four masking strategies for isolating the target building. We propose an RGB-M masking strategy that appends the building footprint mask as a fourth input channel, providing a soft spatial prior that outperforms hard cropping across both modalities. The Perceiver IO fusion model improves over all other fusion strategies and yields substantial per-class gains for attributes visible from street level (e.g., +11.3 AP for slate, +1.3 AP for dormers), though the satellite-only baseline retains a slight advantage in macro-averaged mAP for classes that are predominantly visible from above. These results establish a scalable, flexible architecture for multi-modal building inspection that can accommodate heterogeneous inputs and multiple output tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a multi-modal classification framework using Perceiver IO to fuse satellite and street-level imagery for building roof attribute prediction, with a novel RGB-M masking strategy and a large-scale dataset spanning ten countries.
AI.motivation: To develop a scalable, flexible multi-modal architecture for building inspection that can handle variable numbers of street-level views per building without padding or fixed-size pooling, and jointly predict roof element and material classes.
AI.method: Uses Perceiver IO architecture with shared DINOv2 backbone across satellite and street-level images. Proposes RGB-M masking strategy (adding building footprint mask as 4th input channel) and evaluates four different masking strategies. Constructs a dataset of 32,135 buildings (61,672 segments) from ten countries with up to eight street-level views per segment.
AI.result: Perceiver IO fusion model outperforms other fusion strategies, with substantial per-class gains for street-visible attributes (e.g., +11.3 AP for slate, +1.3 AP for dormers). However, satellite-only baseline retains slight advantage in macro-averaged mAP for classes predominantly visible from above. RGB-M masking outperforms hard cropping across both modalities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework establishes a scalable, flexible architecture for multi-modal building inspection that accommodates heterogeneous inputs and multiple output tasks, with RGB-M masking and Perceiver IO fusion proving effective.

title: From Privacy to Generalization: Linear Max-Information Bounds for DP-SGD
summary: Understanding the relationship between generalization and privacy remains a central challenge in modern machine learning theory, particularly for deep networks trained by variants of differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD). In this work we make progress on this persistent open problem by proving a finite-sample bound on the approximate max-information of DP-SGD that exhibits scaling properties comparable with (Dwork et al, 2015)'s classic result for $ε$-differentially private algorithms, namely at most linear in the dataset size. From our result we obtain a general-purpose PAC-Bayes generalization bound in which the necessary prior distribution can be learned by DP-SGD, as well as a generalization bound for DP-SGD-trained models themselves, with a complexity term that is fully explicit and controlled by the optimization hyperparameters.

AI.tldr: This paper proves a finite-sample bound on the approximate max-information of DP-SGD, showing at most linear scaling with dataset size, and derives PAC-Bayes generalization bounds where the prior can be learned by DP-SGD itself.
AI.motivation: Understanding the relationship between generalization and privacy in modern machine learning, particularly for deep networks trained by DP-SGD variants.
AI.method: Proves a finite-sample bound on the approximate max-information of DP-SGD with scaling comparable to (Dwork et al, 2015)'s classic result for ε-differentially private algorithms.
AI.result: Obtains a general-purpose PAC-Bayes generalization bound where the prior can be learned by DP-SGD, and a generalization bound for DP-SGD-trained models with an explicit complexity term controlled by optimization hyperparameters.
AI.conclusion: The work advances the open problem of linking generalization and privacy by providing scaling properties and explicit generalization bounds for DP-SGD.

title: From Static Context to Calibrated Interactive RL: Mitigating Distribution Shift in Multi-turn Dialogue with Aligned Simulator
summary: A long-standing goal of the research community is to develop highly interactive LLM-based dialogue agents. Recent research focuses on optimizing policies based on fixed offline logs (Static Context RL) or using a prompt-based simulator (Interactive RL). In this work, we theoretically show that both paradigms are fundamentally limited by context distribution shift--a mismatch between dialogue histories observed during training and those encountered in real conversations. This shift compounds quadratically over turns and severely degrades dialogue quality. Specifically, we attribute this shift to two distinct sources: (i) policy-induced shift, arising from training on static histories rather than self-generated trajectories; and (ii) simulator-induced shift, stemming from discrepancies between simulated and real human behaviors. To address these challenges, we propose Calibrated Interactive RL, a unified framework that couples interactive RL with simulator alignment. By aligning the simulator with human interaction patterns, our approach reduces the sim-to-real gap and mitigates compounding distribution shifts. Experiments across multiple dialogue tasks confirm our theoretical analysis: (i) Interactive RL significantly outperforms the Static Context baseline by mitigating policy distribution shift; and (ii) calibrating simulators with our alignment method further bridges the sim-to-real gap, yielding state-of-the-art downstream performance.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically identifies that both Static Context RL and Interactive RL for LLM dialogue agents suffer from context distribution shift, which compounds quadratically over turns. The authors propose Calibrated Interactive RL, which couples interactive RL with simulator alignment to reduce sim-to-real gaps, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To develop highly interactive LLM-based dialogue agents, existing methods (Static Context RL and Interactive RL) are fundamentally limited by context distribution shift—a mismatch between training and real conversation histories that degrades dialogue quality.
AI.method: Calibrated Interactive RL: a unified framework that couples interactive RL (learning from self-generated trajectories) with simulator alignment (reducing discrepancies between simulated and real human behaviors) to mitigate both policy-induced and simulator-induced distribution shifts.
AI.result: Interactive RL significantly outperforms Static Context RL by mitigating policy distribution shift; calibrating simulators via the proposed alignment method further bridges the sim-to-real gap, yielding state-of-the-art downstream performance across multiple dialogue tasks.
AI.conclusion: Context distribution shift is a fundamental bottleneck in LLM dialogue agent training, and Calibrated Interactive RL effectively addresses both sources of shift, leading to superior dialogue quality.

title: VR-DAgger: Immersive VR for Dexterous Data Collection and Uncertainty-Guided On-Policy Correction
summary: Learning from demonstrations is effective for robotic manipulation, but collecting sufficient task-specific data remains a major bottleneck. Under distribution shift, small errors compound, performance degrades, and expert time is often spent on redundant, low-value corrections instead of the few critical failure cases.

AI.tldr: Learning from demonstrations in robotics is hindered by the need for large amounts of task-specific data, as distribution shifts cause compounding errors and inefficient use of expert time on redundant corrections.
AI.motivation: To address the data collection bottleneck in robotic learning from demonstrations, particularly the inefficiency caused by distribution shift where small errors compound and expert time is wasted on low-value corrections.
AI.method: The paper likely proposes a method to reduce redundant expert corrections by focusing expert intervention on critical failure cases rather than all errors, possibly through selective data collection or error detection strategies.
AI.result: The proposed method would reduce the amount of task-specific data needed and improve learning efficiency under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: More efficient data collection strategies that prioritize critical failure cases can significantly reduce the bottleneck in learning from demonstrations for robotic manipulation.

title: Detail Consistent Stage-Wise Distillation for Efficient 3D MRI Segmentation
summary: Deploying high-performing 3D medical image segmenters (e.g., nnU-Net) is often limited by memory footprint and inference latency. Compression is therefore necessary, but compact 3D encoders tend to lose fine structural cues (small lesions and sharp boundaries) as downsampling repeats across multi-resolution stages. We propose Detail Consistent Distillation (DCD), a stage-wise distillation framework that preserves structural detail across scales by aligning teacher-student features in a wavelet-decomposed representation. At each encoder stage, DCD distills directional detail components in the wavelet domain while leaving the coarse approximation comparatively unconstrained, avoiding over-regularization of global semantics. DCD is used only during training and introduces no inference-time overhead. Experiments on the BraTS 2024 and ISLES 2022 benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance in MRI segmentation using 3D multi-modal data. Code and implementation details for DCD are publicly available at https://github.com/ClinicaAlpha/DCD-3D-MedSeg.

AI.tldr: DCD is a stage-wise distillation framework that preserves fine structural details in compressed 3D medical image segmenters by aligning teacher-student features in the wavelet domain, achieving superior MRI segmentation without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Deploying high-performing 3D medical image segmenters like nnU-Net is limited by memory and latency, but compression causes loss of fine structural cues (small lesions, sharp boundaries) due to repeated downsampling.
AI.method: DCD performs stage-wise distillation by decomposing teacher and student features into wavelet representations, distilling directional detail components while leaving coarse approximation unconstrained, avoiding over-regularization of global semantics.
AI.result: Superior performance on BraTS 2024 and ISLES 2022 benchmarks for 3D multi-modal MRI segmentation.
AI.conclusion: DCD effectively preserves structural detail in compressed 3D medical image segmenters with no inference-time overhead, offering a practical solution for deploying efficient segmentation models.

title: Provably Communication-Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Federated Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) achieve strong performance on relational data, but real-world graphs are often distributed across organizations that cannot share raw data due to privacy and policy constraints. Existing federated GNN methods either ignore cross-client links, leading to degraded accuracy, or require frequent embedding exchanges, incurring substantial communication and privacy costs. We propose CE-FedGNN, a communication-efficient and privacy-preserving federated GNN framework for learning over such coupled graphs. Our approach avoids sharing raw data or per-round embeddings by infrequently exchanging aggregated node representations. To handle cross-client dependency and staleness, we introduce a moving-average estimator that continuously tracks node representations and enables their stable reuse across rounds. To provide formal privacy guarantees for the released representations, we adopt the metric differential privacy (metric-DP) framework, which measures privacy with respect to distances in the learned embedding space rather than worst-case input perturbations. This yields meaningful guarantees at noise levels where standard differential privacy becomes overly conservative. We establish convergence to a stationary point at a rate of $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ with $O(T^{3/4})$ communication complexity. In addition, we derive $(\varepsilon,δ)$-metric-DP guarantees via Rényi differential privacy composition under a public-cohort threat model. Experiments on synthetic interbank anti-money laundering benchmarks and citation networks demonstrate that CE-FedGNN achieves strong performance while significantly reducing communication and maintaining robustness under privacy-preserving noise.

AI.tldr: CE-FedGNN is a federated GNN framework that enables learning over distributed coupled graphs without sharing raw data or frequent embeddings, using moving-average estimators and metric differential privacy to achieve communication efficiency, privacy guarantees, and strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Real-world graphs are often distributed across organizations that cannot share raw data due to privacy/policy constraints. Existing federated GNN methods either ignore cross-client links (hurting accuracy) or require frequent embedding exchanges (high communication and privacy costs).
AI.method: CE-FedGNN avoids raw data sharing and per-round embedding exchanges by infrequently exchanging aggregated node representations. It uses a moving-average estimator to track node representations for stable reuse across rounds, and adopts metric-DP (with Rényi DP composition) to provide formal privacy guarantees tailored to embedding space distances rather than worst-case perturbations.
AI.result: Theoretically: converges to a stationary point at O(1/√T) with O(T^{3/4}) communication complexity, with (ε,δ)-metric-DP guarantees. Empirically: achieves strong performance on anti-money laundering benchmarks and citation networks while significantly reducing communication and maintaining robustness under privacy-preserving noise.
AI.conclusion: CE-FedGNN effectively balances accuracy, communication efficiency, and privacy in federated graph learning across coupled subgraphs, outperforming prior methods that either ignore cross-client links or incur heavy communication overhead.

title: Reasoning, Code, or Both? How Large Language Models Handle Variations in Math Questions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve impressive accuracy on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, yet their performance drops when problems are modified with simple changes like different names or numbers. Code execution methods, which let models generate and run Python code instead of reasoning in natural language, have been proposed as a solution, but their effect on reasoning robustness (the ability to maintain accuracy across problem variations) has not been systematically tested. This study evaluates three approaches on 1,000 problems from the GSM-Symbolic dataset: pure reasoning using chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, single-shot code execution using Program-Aided Language models (PAL), and iterative code execution using Step-by-Step Coding (SBSC). All three were run on paired original and modified problems using Claude Haiku 4.5. CoT was the most robust method, with an accuracy drop of 1.3 percentage points and 1.8% of problems breaking under perturbation. PAL was the least robust at 1.7 percentage points and 3.1% broke, with SBSC falling in between. Although these differences were not statistically significant ($p = .096$), the directional trend was consistent across all measures, suggesting that code execution, whether single-shot or iterative, does not improve reasoning robustness on grade-school-level problem variations.

AI.tldr: This study tests whether code execution methods (PAL, SBSC) improve reasoning robustness over chain-of-thought (CoT) when problem variations are introduced. Using 1,000 GSM-Symbolic problems on Claude Haiku 4.5, CoT was most robust (1.3% accuracy drop, 1.8% problems broken), PAL least robust (1.7%, 3.1%), with no statistically significant differences ($p=.096$). The authors conclude code execution does not improve grade-school-level reasoning robustness.
AI.motivation: LLMs show impressive math reasoning accuracy but are brittle under simple problem variations (e.g., different names/numbers). Code execution methods have been proposed to mitigate this, but their robustness has not been systematically tested.
AI.method: Three approaches (CoT, PAL single-shot code execution, SBSC iterative code execution) were evaluated on 1,000 paired original/modified problems from GSM-Symbolic dataset using Claude Haiku 4.5, measuring accuracy drops and perturbation breakage rates.
AI.result: CoT had 1.3% accuracy drop and 1.8% problems broken; PAL had 1.7% drop and 3.1% broken; SBSC fell in between. Differences were not statistically significant ($p=.096$), but the trend was consistent across all measures.
AI.conclusion: Code execution (single-shot or iterative) does not improve reasoning robustness on grade-school-level problem variations compared to standard chain-of-thought reasoning.

title: TCBiRRT: Rapid Motion Planning for Tightly Coupled Dual-arm Space Manipulator Using Task-space Random Expansion
summary: Planning the motion path for a tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulator under closed-chain constraints is a fundamental yet challenging problem in on-orbit assembly of large-scale space structures. The closed-chain constraints significantly reduce the feasible configuration space, making it difficult for existing planners to efficiently generate collision-free motions, especially in cluttered environments. To address this issue, this paper proposes a task-space constrained bidirectional rapidly-exploring random tree algorithm, termed TCBiRRT. Unlike conventional methods that operate in the high-dimensional configuration space, the proposed approach performs random sampling and node expansion directly in the task space defined by the manipulated object pose. A task-space node expansion strategy is developed to generate candidate object motions, which are then mapped to continuous joint paths using a path inverse kinematics algorithm. The method is further integrated with a bidirectional RRT framework and a regrasp mechanism to efficiently connect two random trees. Extensive simulations are conducted in representative on-orbit assembly scenarios with varying levels of environmental complexity. The results demonstrate that TCBiRRT achieves significantly higher success rates and orders-of-magnitude improvements in planning time compared to state-of-the-art planners. The proposed method provides an efficient and robust solution for motion planning of tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes TCBiRRT, a task-space constrained bidirectional RRT algorithm that samples in the manipulated object's pose space rather than high-dimensional configuration space, enabling efficient collision-free motion planning for tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators under closed-chain constraints, with significantly improved success rates and planning speed.
AI.motivation: Motion planning for tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators under closed-chain constraints is fundamentally challenging because these constraints drastically reduce feasible configuration space, making it difficult for existing planners to generate collision-free motions efficiently, especially in cluttered on-orbit assembly environments.
AI.method: TCBiRRT performs random sampling and node expansion directly in the task space (manipulated object pose) rather than high-dimensional configuration space, uses a task-space node expansion strategy to generate candidate object motions mapped to continuous joint paths via path inverse kinematics, integrates with a bidirectional RRT framework and a regrasp mechanism to connect two random trees efficiently.
AI.result: Extensive simulations in representative on-orbit assembly scenarios show TCBiRRT achieves significantly higher success rates and orders-of-magnitude improvements in planning time compared to state-of-the-art planners.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides an efficient and robust solution for motion planning of tightly coupled dual-arm space manipulators under closed-chain constraints.

title: Zero-Shot Object Re-Identification in Egocentric Kitchen Videos via Multi-Stage SAM3 Feature Fusion
summary: Object re-identification (ReID) in egocentric kitchen videos is challenging due to rapid viewpoint changes, frequent occlusions, cluttered scenes, and large intra-class appearance variations. Objects may leave and re-enter the field of view, and the large diversity of instances with limited annotations makes supervised ReID difficult to scale, motivating zero-shot approaches. We study zero-shot object ReID on the EPIC-Kitchens benchmark, where the goal is to match active food and kitchen-tool instances across frames using only pre-trained visual features. We first evaluate five state-of-the-art feature extractors, including Vision-Language Models (VLMs) - CLIP, DINOv2, DreamSim, I-JEPA, and SAM3 - and show that zero-shot methods fail, with the best baseline achieving only 45.3% mAP. We then propose an Enhanced SAM3 ReID Pipeline, a zero-shot multi-stage method built around SAM3 segmentation as the core component. Stage 1 uses SAM3 to suppress background clutter. Stage 2 fuses embeddings from SAM3, DINOv2, and CLIP into a single L2-normalized descriptor. Stage 3 augments cosine similarity with mask-shape IoU for geometric consistency, and Stage 4 applies k-reciprocal re-ranking. The full pipeline improves performance by 7.5% mAP to 52.8%.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot object ReID in egocentric kitchen videos is challenging (best baseline 45.3% mAP). The proposed Enhanced SAM3 ReID Pipeline (SAM3 segmentation + multi-feature fusion + mask IoU + re-ranking) improves mAP to 52.8%.
AI.motivation: Object ReID in egocentric kitchen videos suffers from viewpoint changes, occlusions, clutter, and large intra-class variation. Supervised methods don't scale due to limited annotations, motivating zero-shot approaches.
AI.method: A zero-shot multi-stage pipeline: (1) SAM3 segmentation to suppress background, (2) fused embeddings from SAM3, DINOv2, and CLIP into L2-normalized descriptor, (3) mask-shape IoU for geometric consistency, (4) k-reciprocal re-ranking.
AI.result: Best zero-shot baseline (CLIP/DINOv2/etc.) achieves 45.3% mAP. The proposed pipeline achieves 52.8% mAP, a 7.5% improvement.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot ReID in egocentric videos remains challenging but the proposed multi-stage pipeline leveraging SAM3 segmentation and multi-modal feature fusion significantly outperforms single feature extractors.

title: The Bridge-Garden Dilemma in LLM Distillation: Why Mixing Hard and Soft Labels Works
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student. In language modeling, the student is trained either on tokens sampled from the teacher (hard labels) or the teacher's full next-token distribution (soft labels). Despite soft labels appear strictly richer, we find that mixing hard and soft labels consistently yields better results. Crucially, we show that this gain cannot be explained by closer teacher matching during training. Instead, it comes from reduced exposure bias, the mismatch between training and inference distributions. To explain this phenomenon, we introduce the Bridge-Garden Decomposition theory, which categorizes generation steps into two types: Bridges, where the next token must be exact, and Gardens, where it can be flexible. We show that hard-only KD excels in Bridges by avoiding risky deviations, while soft-only KD preserves diversity in Gardens. A hybrid strategy handles both cases and, as a result, reduces exposure bias across the sequence. Guided by this theory, we develop a family of Bridge-Garden hybrid supervision methods that adaptively balance hard and soft labels. Across a primary suite of seven teacher-student pairs (including Qwen, Llama, Gemma, and DeepSeek) and benchmarks in reasoning and coding, our approach outperforms divergence-based and on-policy KD baselines while reducing training cost by 9.7x, enabling efficient model compression. Code is available at https://github.com/ghwang-s/bridge_garden_hybrid_kd_release.

AI.tldr: Mixing hard and soft labels in knowledge distillation for language models reduces exposure bias and outperforms either alone, explained by the Bridge-Garden Decomposition theory—Bridges require exact tokens, Gardens allow flexibility. A hybrid strategy achieves superior results across various teacher-student pairs while reducing training cost by 9.7x.
AI.motivation: Despite soft labels being strictly richer, mixing hard and soft labels yields better results than either alone. This gain cannot be explained by closer teacher matching—instead, it stems from reduced exposure bias between training and inference distributions.
AI.method: Introduces Bridge-Garden Decomposition theory, categorizing generation steps into Bridges (exact token required) and Gardens (flexible token allowed). Proposes a family of Bridge-Garden hybrid supervision methods that adaptively balance hard and soft labels based on this theory.
AI.result: Across seven teacher-student pairs (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, DeepSeek) and reasoning/coding benchmarks, the approach outperforms divergence-based and on-policy KD baselines while reducing training cost by 9.7x.
AI.conclusion: Hard-only KD excels in Bridges by avoiding risky deviations; soft-only KD preserves diversity in Gardens. A hybrid strategy handles both cases, reducing exposure bias across the sequence and enabling efficient model compression.

title: The MiniMax-M2 Series: Mini Activations Unleashing Max Real-World Intelligence
summary: We introduce the MiniMax-M2 series, a family of Mixture-of-Experts language models built around the principle that mini activations can unleash maximum real-world intelligence. The flagship M2 contains 229.9B total parameters with only 9.8B activated per token. Designed end-to-end for agentic deployment, the M2 series rests on three components: (i) agent-driven data pipelines producing large-scale, verifiable trajectories across agentic coding and agentic cowork, each grounded in an executable workspace and an artifact-aligned reward; (ii) Forge, a scalable agent-native RL system that adapts to long-horizon agent trajectories, paired with windowed-FIFO scheduling, prefix-tree merging, inference optimization, and a clean training-inference-agent decoupling that supports both white-box and black-box agents; (iii) the latest M2.7 checkpoint takes an early step toward self-evolution -- autonomously debugging training runs and modifying its own scaffold. Across M2 through M2.7, this combination translates a mini-activation footprint into frontier-tier performance on agentic coding, deep search, office-task, and reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MiniMax-M2 is a 229.9B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts LLM (9.8B activated per token) designed for agentic tasks, featuring agent-driven data pipelines, a scalable RL system (Forge), and a self-evolving checkpoint (M2.7) that achieves frontier performance with minimal activation costs.
AI.motivation: To build a large language model that achieves maximum real-world intelligence with minimal (mini) computational activation, specifically optimized for agentic deployment scenarios.
AI.method: Three components: (1) agent-driven data pipelines with verifiable trajectories and artifact-aligned rewards; (2) Forge, an agent-native RL system with windowed-FIFO scheduling, prefix-tree merging, and clean training-inference-agent decoupling; (3) M2.7 checkpoint enabling self-evolution (autonomous debugging and scaffold modification).
AI.result: Frontier-tier performance on agentic coding, deep search, office-task, and reasoning benchmarks, achieved with only 9.8B activated parameters per token (mini-activation footprint).
AI.conclusion: The mini-activation principle combined with agent-native training infrastructure enables efficient, scalable, and self-evolving agentic LLMs that rival much larger models.

title: Towards Drone-based Mapping of Volcanic Gases using Gas Tomography
summary: Volcanoes emit large amounts of CO2, directly influencing human lives. Mapping volcanic gas emissions helps to forecast eruptions and understand the impact of volcanoes on climate and the environment. Drone-based gas sensing significantly reduces risks in volcanic monitoring but faces technical limitations when measuring gas, as rotor downwash disperses the gas plume before detection. Gas Tomography using remote gas sensing addresses this challenge. At the Salinelle dei Cappuccini mud volcanoes, we demonstrate that while drone-mounted in-situ sensors failed to detect CO2 emissions due to aerodynamic disturbance, open-path sensing successfully enabled remote gas distribution mapping. We present a novel model-based gas tomographic reconstruction approach that incorporates a Lagrangian model to compensate for wind-induced advection. The resulting gas distribution maps align with manually collected in-situ measurements, confirming that model-based gas tomography effectively overcomes downwash limitations and enables accurate mapping of volcanic emissions.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that model-based gas tomography using drone-mounted open-path sensors can overcome rotor downwash limitations and accurately map volcanic CO2 emissions, whereas traditional in-situ sensors fail due to aerodynamic disturbances.
AI.motivation: Volcanic CO2 emissions impact human lives and climate, but drone-based in-situ gas sensing faces technical limitations because rotor downwash disperses the gas plume before detection, compromising measurement accuracy.
AI.method: The authors deployed drone-mounted open-path (remote) gas sensors at Salinelle dei Cappuccini mud volcanoes and developed a model-based gas tomographic reconstruction approach incorporating a Lagrangian model to compensate for wind-induced advection effects.
AI.result: In-situ sensors failed to detect CO2 due to downwash disturbance, whereas open-path sensing successfully enabled remote gas distribution mapping. The reconstructed gas distribution maps aligned well with manually collected in-situ reference measurements.
AI.conclusion: Model-based gas tomography effectively overcomes rotor downwash limitations and enables accurate mapping of volcanic emissions, offering a safer and more reliable approach for volcanic monitoring.

title: OmniGF: A Dual-Branch Vision-Language Framework for Unified Gaze Following
summary: Understanding human gaze behavior is essential for complex scene comprehension and human-computer interaction. Traditional gaze following models are typically restricted to pure spatial localization, lacking the high-level capacity to reason about semantic targets or complex social contexts. Furthermore, these models often process individuals sequentially, requiring redundant computations over the same scene image for multi-person inference. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) offer the exceptional semantic reasoning needed to address gaze-related semantic tasks, their reliance on discrete text generation inherently limits precision in continuous spatial tasks like gaze localization. To bridge this gap, we propose OmniGF, a unified vision-language framework that adapts foundational VLMs for highly scalable multi-person gaze reasoning. The model adopts a dual-branch decoding strategy: a structured language branch generates discrete reasoning states, while a continuous spatial branch directly taps into the VLM's dense hidden states. Supervising these extracted representations with high-resolution gaze target heatmaps effectively overcomes the spatial bottleneck of text-only coordinate generation. Furthermore, to explicitly ground the model in multi-person scenes, we augment the input with head embeddings encoded from cropped head images, providing fine-grained appearance and orientation cues for all individuals simultaneously. By modeling all individuals and leveraging the strong semantic capability of VLMs, OmniGF seamlessly integrates precise spatial gaze target estimation, semantic gaze prediction, and complex social gaze reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework establishes new state-of-the-art performance across multiple standard benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/cvlab-stonybrook/omnigf.

AI.tldr: OmniGF is a unified vision-language framework that adapts foundational VLMs for scalable multi-person gaze reasoning. It uses a dual-branch decoding strategy (structured language branch for discrete reasoning + continuous spatial branch using VLM hidden states) to overcome the spatial bottleneck of text-only coordinate generation, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Traditional gaze following models are limited to pure spatial localization, lack reasoning about semantic targets or social contexts, and require redundant computations for multi-person inference. Existing VLMs offer semantic reasoning but lack precision in continuous spatial tasks due to reliance on discrete text generation.
AI.method: OmniGF adopts a dual-branch decoding strategy: (1) a structured language branch for discrete reasoning states, and (2) a continuous spatial branch that extracts dense hidden states from the VLM, supervised by high-resolution gaze target heatmaps. Input is augmented with head embeddings from cropped head images to provide fine-grained appearance and orientation cues for all individuals simultaneously.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniGF establishes new state-of-the-art performance across multiple standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By modeling all individuals and leveraging the strong semantic capability of VLMs, OmniGF seamlessly integrates precise spatial gaze target estimation, semantic gaze prediction, and complex social gaze reasoning, outperforming prior methods.

title: Unified Neural Scaling Laws
summary: We present a functional form (that we refer to as a Unified Neural Scaling Law (UNSL)) that accurately models and extrapolates the scaling behaviors of deep neural networks as multiple dimensions all vary simultaneously (i.e. how the evaluation metric of interest varies as one simultaneously varies the number of model parameters, training dataset size, number of training steps, number of inference steps, amount of compute, and various hyperparameters) for various architectures and for each of various tasks within a varied set of upstream and downstream tasks. This set includes large-scale vision, language, math, and reinforcement learning. When compared to other functional forms for neural scaling, this functional form yields extrapolations of scaling behavior that are considerably more accurate on this set.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Unified Neural Scaling Law (UNSL), a functional form that accurately models and extrapolates neural network scaling behaviors when multiple dimensions (parameters, data, steps, compute, hyperparameters) vary simultaneously across diverse tasks including vision, language, math, and RL, outperforming existing scaling law formulations.
AI.motivation: Existing neural scaling laws typically model scaling along a single dimension (e.g., model size or data size alone), but real-world scenarios involve simultaneous variation of multiple factors. The authors aim to develop a unified functional form that captures these multi-dimensional scaling behaviors accurately.
AI.method: The authors propose UNSL, a functional form designed to model scaling behavior as multiple dimensions (model parameters, dataset size, training steps, inference steps, compute, hyperparameters) vary simultaneously. They test this across diverse architectures and tasks in vision, language, math, and reinforcement learning.
AI.result: UNSL yields considerably more accurate extrapolations of scaling behavior compared to other functional forms for neural scaling, across the varied set of upstream and downstream tasks tested.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Unified Neural Scaling Law better captures multi-dimensional scaling dynamics, making it a more reliable tool for predicting model performance as multiple training and architectural factors change simultaneously.

title: Which Changes Matter? Towards Trustworthy Legal AI via Relevance-Sensitive Evaluation and Solver-Grounded Reasoning
summary: Legal reasoning requires distinguishing changes that matter from those that do not. Legal AI should remain stable under legally irrelevant perturbations, but should change when perturbations alter legally material points. We formulate this requirement as a legal-relevance-sensitive evaluation problem: LLMs should only be sensitive to the legally relevant change. We introduce a unified evaluation suite covering should-change and should-not-change evaluation across judicial fairness, robustness, and statute-confusion scenarios. Our evaluation shows that existing legal LLMs are systematically sensitive to legally irrelevant variations and often fail to distinguish related legal elements and statutory rules. To mitigate these failures, we present LexGuard, an adversarial multi-agent framework grounded in formal reasoning. LexGuard formalizes statutes into executable constraints, uses adversarial agents to extract competing fact-statute arguments, and invokes SMT solvers to verify legal satisfaction and logical consistency. Experiments show that LexGuard improves legal reasoning reliability by reducing vulnerability to manipulative framing, improving disambiguation among similar statutes, limiting the influence of legally irrelevant attributes, and increasing consistency under benign reformulations. We show that legal trustworthiness requires not only accuracy, but calibrated sensitivity to legally material changes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces LexGuard, an adversarial multi-agent framework that improves LLM legal reasoning by ensuring models are sensitive only to legally relevant changes while remaining stable under irrelevant perturbations.
AI.motivation: Legal AI models currently lack calibrated sensitivity—they fail to distinguish legally relevant changes from irrelevant ones, making them vulnerable to manipulative framing and unable to properly disambiguate similar statutes.
AI.method: The authors formulate a legal-relevance-sensitive evaluation problem, build a unified evaluation suite (should-change vs. should-not-change scenarios), and propose LexGuard—a multi-agent framework that formalizes statutes into executable constraints, uses adversarial agents for fact-statute arguments, and employs SMT solvers for verification.
AI.result: LexGuard reduces vulnerability to manipulative framing, improves disambiguation among similar statutes, limits influence of legally irrelevant attributes, and increases consistency under benign reformulations.
AI.conclusion: Legal trustworthiness requires not only accuracy but also calibrated sensitivity—models must change only when legally material points change, and remain stable otherwise.

title: FineVLA: Fine-Grained Instruction Alignment for Steerable Vision-Language-Action Policies
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly expected to not only complete robot tasks, but also follow human instructions about how those tasks should be executed. However, existing robot datasets usually pair trajectories with coarse goal-level language, leaving execution-critical details such as active arm, approach direction, and contact region unspecified. This limits steerable policy learning and robotic video understanding. We introduce FineVLA, an open framework for action-aligned fine-grained VLA supervision. The framework includes: (1) a data construction tool that unifies 972,247 trajectories across 85K tasks from 10 open-source robot datasets and builds FineVLA-Data, a human-verified dataset of 47,159 fine-grained trajectories; (2) a held-out benchmark with 500 videos, 10,816 atomic facts, and 1,030 VQA questions; (3) a robotics-specialized VLM annotator for scalable fine-grained annotation; and (4) a steerable VLA policy trained with controlled mixtures of fine-grained and raw goal-level instructions. Our experiments yield three findings. First, fine-grained supervision does not sacrifice goal-level success: FG-only improves over Raw-only by +1.4 to +8.1 success-rate points across settings. Second, fine-grained and raw instructions are complementary, following a consistent inverted-U trend peaking at FG:Raw = 1:2 to 1:1. The best mixed setting reaches 86.8%/82.5% in RoboTwin simulation and 62.7/100 in real-world dual-arm manipulation (vs. 49.9 Raw-only). Third, fine-grained supervision improves steerable control: the largest real-world gains appear on pose (+23), color (+18), and approach direction (+18)--factors where goal-level instructions provide no guidance. Overall, fine-grained language should augment goal-level instructions: specifying how to execute alongside what to achieve. Project page: https://finevla.xlang.ai/

AI.tldr: FineVLA introduces a framework for fine-grained Vision-Language-Action supervision that augments goal-level instructions with execution details (active arm, approach direction, contact region), achieving significant improvements in robot task success rates and steerable control.
AI.motivation: Existing robot datasets pair trajectories with coarse goal-level language, missing execution-critical details (active arm, approach direction, contact region), which limits steerable policy learning and robotic video understanding.
AI.method: The framework includes: (1) a data construction tool unifying 972,247 trajectories from 10 open-source datasets into FineVLA-Data (47,159 fine-grained trajectories); (2) a benchmark with 500 videos, 10,816 atomic facts, and 1,030 VQA questions; (3) a robotics-specialized VLM annotator; and (4) a steerable VLA policy trained with controlled mixtures of fine-grained and raw goal-level instructions.
AI.result: Fine-grained supervision improves goal-level success by +1.4 to +8.1 success-rate points. Best mixed setting (FG:Raw = 1:2 to 1:1) achieves 86.8%/82.5% in RoboTwin simulation and 62.7/100 in real-world dual-arm manipulation (vs. 49.9 Raw-only). Largest gains on pose (+23), color (+18), and approach direction (+18).
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained language should augment goal-level instructions, specifying how to execute alongside what to achieve.

title: The Rescue Effect: Spatio-Semantic Early Exit Bypasses Quantization Collapse in CLIP
summary: Deploying Vision-Language Models on resource-constrained hardware typically requires INT8 quantization, but in joint-embedding architectures such as CLIP this introduces a failure mode distinct from quantized CNN classifiers: activation noise accumulated across transformer blocks perturbs the direction of the multimodal embedding, eroding the cosine alignment on which zero-shot retrieval depends. We characterize this as Quantization-Induced Representation Collapse (QIRC) and quantify it on INT8 CLIP ViT-B/32, where the layer-wise noise-to-signal ratio grows from below 10% in shallow blocks to 52% at Layer 11. We propose LRA-EE (Layer-wise Representation-Aware Early Exit), which bypasses noise-saturated deep layers via Spatio-Semantic Aggregation (replacing the immature shallow [CLS] with a global patch-token average), a learned multi-feature gate (confidence, top-2 margin, spatial-activation variance), and Layer-adaptive Confidence Thresholding calibrated to each layer's Information-to-Noise Ratio. On ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification, LRA-EE reduces FLOPs by 13.4% and improves Top-1 accuracy by +2.44%p (58.72% -> 61.16%) over the INT8 baseline. A four-quadrant decomposition isolates the Rescue Effect: 9.5% of samples are correctly classified at shallow exits but lost to noise at full depth, against only 7.1% suffering the inverse.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies Quantization-Induced Representation Collapse (QIRC) in INT8-quantized CLIP models, where accumulated activation noise disrupts cosine alignment in joint embeddings. The proposed LRA-EE method bypasses noise-saturated deep layers using early exit strategies, achieving 13.4% FLOPs reduction and +2.44% accuracy improvement on ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification.
AI.motivation: INT8 quantization of Vision-Language Models like CLIP introduces a distinct failure mode—activation noise accumulates across transformer blocks, distorting the embedding direction and degrading cosine alignment critical for zero-shot retrieval, unlike quantized CNN classifiers.
AI.method: LRA-EE (Layer-wise Representation-Aware Early Exit) combines Spatio-Semantic Aggregation (global patch-token average replacing shallow [CLS]), a learned multi-feature gate (confidence, top-2 margin, spatial-activation variance), and Layer-adaptive Confidence Thresholding calibrated to each layer's Information-to-Noise Ratio.
AI.result: On ImageNet-1K zero-shot classification with INT8 CLIP ViT-B/32, LRA-EE reduces FLOPs by 13.4% and improves Top-1 accuracy by +2.44 percentage points (58.72% → 61.16%) over the INT8 baseline. A four-quadrant analysis reveals a Rescue Effect: 9.5% of samples are correctly classified at shallow exits but lost at full depth, vs. 7.1% experiencing the opposite.
AI.conclusion: Early exiting at appropriate layers can mitigate quantization-induced representation collapse in VLMs, offering both efficiency gains and accuracy improvements by avoiding noise-saturated deep layers.

title: PolyFusionAgent: A Multimodal Foundation Model and Autonomous AI Assistant for Polymer Property Prediction and Inverse Design
comment: 23 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables; Supplementary material included
summary: Polymer discovery is central to fields ranging from energy storage to biomedicine, but it is hindered by an astronomically large chemical design space and fragmented representations of structure, properties, and prior knowledge. This fragmentation leaves many AI models disconnected from physical and experimental reality, restricting their ability to support directly actionable design decisions. Here we introduce PolyFusionAgent, an interactive framework coupling a multimodal polymer foundation model (PolyFusion) with a tool-augmented, literature-grounded design agent (PolyAgent). PolyFusion aligns complementary polymer views including sequence, topology, 3D geometry, and fingerprints across millions of polymers to learn a shared latent space transferable across chemistries and data regimes, improving thermophysical property prediction and enabling property-conditioned generation of chemically valid, structurally novel polymers beyond the reference design space. PolyAgent closes the design loop by linking prediction and inverse design with evidence retrieval from the polymer literature, proposing, evaluating, and contextualizing hypotheses with explicit precedent in one workflow. Together, PolyFusionAgent enables interactive, evidence-linked polymer discovery combining large-scale representation learning, multimodal chemical knowledge, and verifiable scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: PolyFusionAgent is an interactive framework that combines a multimodal polymer foundation model (PolyFusion) with a literature-grounded design agent (PolyAgent) to enable evidence-linked polymer discovery through improved property prediction, inverse design, and scientific reasoning.
AI.motivation: Polymer discovery is hindered by an enormous chemical design space and fragmented representations of structure, properties, and prior knowledge, leaving AI models disconnected from physical/experimental reality and unable to support directly actionable design decisions.
AI.method: PolyFusionAgent couples (1) PolyFusion - a multimodal polymer foundation model that aligns sequence, topology, 3D geometry, and fingerprints across millions of polymers into a shared latent space, and (2) PolyAgent - a tool-augmented agent that links prediction and inverse design with evidence retrieval from polymer literature for proposing, evaluating, and contextualizing hypotheses.
AI.result: PolyFusion improves thermophysical property prediction and enables property-conditioned generation of chemically valid, structurally novel polymers beyond the reference design space. PolyAgent successfully closes the design loop by integrating literature evidence into the design workflow.
AI.conclusion: PolyFusionAgent enables interactive, evidence-linked polymer discovery by combining large-scale representation learning, multimodal chemical knowledge, and verifiable scientific reasoning in a unified framework.

title: Riding the Shifting Potential: When Reactive Control Suffices for Multi-Goal Behavior
summary: Reactive control is often considered insufficient for multi-objective tasks because conflicting objectives give rise to local minima. We argue this limitation is not inherent but arises from static encodings that fail to reflect how objectives currently interact. We exploit the interaction structure encoded in a graph-based world model by extending it with nullspace projections: conflicts are resolved where they arise by projecting lower-priority gradients into the nullspace of higher-priority ones, with priorities determined continuously from the current state. We demonstrate this in two domains where conflicts between objectives are central: navigation around non-convex obstacles, where static potential fields fundamentally fail, and planar pushing of non-convex objects, where our method achieves $100\%$ success across one-hundred configurations versus $0\%$ for the steepest-descent baseline and ${\sim}55\%$ for diffusion policy, without demonstrations or retraining. The same formulation transfers directly to a real robot with additional perceptual and kinematic constraints, accommodating them through the same mechanism.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that reactive control can handle multi-objective tasks by using nullspace projections in a graph-based world model to resolve conflicts dynamically, achieving high success rates in navigation and planar pushing tasks without retraining.
AI.motivation: Reactive control is often dismissed as insufficient for multi-objective tasks due to local minima from conflicting objectives. The authors argue this limitation stems from static encodings rather than reactive control itself.
AI.method: Extends a graph-based world model with nullspace projections: conflicts are resolved by projecting lower-priority gradients into the nullspace of higher-priority ones, with priorities determined continuously from the current state.
AI.result: 100% success in planar pushing across 100 configurations vs 0% for steepest-descent baseline and ~55% for diffusion policy, without demonstrations or retraining. Transfers directly to real robots with additional constraints.
AI.conclusion: Reactive control can effectively handle multi-objective tasks when objectives are dynamically prioritized, and the approach generalizes to real-world robotic systems with perceptual and kinematic constraints.

title: HydraPrompt: An Adaptive and Asymmetric Framework of Vision-Language Models for Synthetic Image Detection
summary: The rapid evolution of generative models has precipitated a proliferation of fabricated content, posing significant challenges to existing Synthetic Image Detection (SID) methods. Capitalizing on advancements in vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), recent attempts have leveraged learnable textual prompts to identify synthetic images. However, they still leverage static prompt as a fixed boundary for real and fake images, failing to adapt to the varying types of forgery that emerge during inference. To overcome this issue, we propose **HydraPrompt**, an asymmetric prompting framework that dynamically adjusts the category centers by aligning with fine-grained image cues. Specifically, we propose an Asymmetric Prompt Adapter (**APA**): (1) for authentic category, we introduce a single set of prompts to capture the consistent representative patterns, which serves as a unified anchor for real content. While (2) for fake category, we construct sample-adaptive prompts that specialize in capturing diverse cues from different samples, enabling adaptive modeling of forgery image variations. To increase pronounced discriminability within different synthetic images, we further introduce a Conditional Supervised Contrastive (**CSC**) objective, which compacts the authentic representations while capturing fine-grained forgery clues. Extensive experiments on popular SID benchmarks demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of our framework.

AI.tldr: HydraPrompt is an asymmetric prompting framework for synthetic image detection that uses a single prompt set for authentic images and sample-adaptive prompts for fake images, along with Conditional Supervised Contrastive learning, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing SID methods using vision-language models rely on static prompts as fixed boundaries between real and fake images, failing to adapt to varying types of forgery during inference.
AI.method: Proposes an Asymmetric Prompt Adapter (APA) with single prompts for authentic category (unified anchor) and sample-adaptive prompts for fake category (capturing diverse cues). Also introduces Conditional Supervised Contrastive (CSC) objective to compact authentic representations and capture fine-grained forgery clues.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on popular SID benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetric prompting approach with adaptive fake prompts and contrastive learning effectively addresses the limitation of static boundaries in synthetic image detection.

title: Scaling World-Model Reinforcement Learning Through Diffusion Policy Optimization
summary: Model-based reinforcement learning (RL) can be effectively supported at scale through the use of world models. However, in practice, scaling such approaches remains fundamentally limited. A commonly recognized challenge is model bias and error compounding, which degrade long-horizon predictions. Beyond these issues, we identify a more critical yet underexplored bottleneck: a structural misalignment between search and value learning in existing world model approaches. In particular, policy improvement often relies on value functions induced by a separate, non-search policy, resulting in training inconsistency and ultimately suboptimal learning. To address this limitation, we propose Model-Based Diffusion Policy Optimization (MBDPO) in world models, a framework that unifies search and policy optimization through diffusion policy representations, thereby unlocking the potential of world models for scalable policy learning. Instead of constructing an explicit planner over a learned world model, we reformulate policy optimization as a diffusion process over searched trajectories in latent world models. In this view, we extract an implicit energy function from the collected dataset that anchors the policy, enabling MBDPO to refine the score field for policy optimization while mitigating misalignment. We evaluate MBDPO across a wide range of settings, including multi-task offline pretraining, online learning, and offline-to-online fine-tuning. In the offline regime, we further investigate its scaling behavior by pretraining on large-scale datasets, observing consistent and monotonic performance gains with increasing model capacity.

AI.tldr: MBDPO proposes a diffusion-based policy framework to address structural misalignment between search and value learning in world model-based RL, achieving consistent scaling improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing world model-based RL suffers from model bias, error compounding, and a critical but underexplored structural misalignment between search and value learning, where policy improvement relies on value functions induced by a separate non-search policy, causing training inconsistency.
AI.method: MBDPO unifies search and policy optimization by reformulating policy optimization as a diffusion process over searched trajectories in latent world models, extracting an implicit energy function from the collected dataset to anchor the policy and refine the score field.
AI.result: Evaluated across multi-task offline pretraining, online learning, and offline-to-online fine-tuning. In offline regime, consistent and monotonic performance gains with increasing model capacity on large-scale datasets.
AI.conclusion: MBDPO effectively mitigates the misalignment between search and value learning, unlocking world models' potential for scalable policy learning.

title: MobileExplorer: Accelerating On-Device Inference for Mobile GUI Agents via Online Exploration
summary: Mobile graphical user interface (GUI) agents enable AI models to autonomously operate smartphones on behalf of users. However, most existing systems focus primarily on optimizing task accuracy and rely on cloud-hosted models for inference, which introduces privacy concerns and network-dependent latency. As a result, fully on-device deployment of mobile GUI agents remains underexplored. We propose MobileExplorer, a new framework that accelerates on-device inference for vision-based mobile GUI agents via online exploration. The key idea is to exploit the long per-step reasoning time of vision-language models (VLMs) by performing lightweight, parallel exploration of UI elements. During model inference, the agent proactively probes semantically relevant UI elements and records these exploration traces as structured memory. To ensure reliable execution in live mobile environments, we design a two-level rollback mechanism that robustly restores the initial UI state when a fast but naive backtracking strategy fails. The collected exploration traces are then summarized into concise contextual hints and injected into the prompt to enhance the subsequent reasoning step. We evaluate MobileExplorer on multiple off-the-shelf devices using the AndroidWorld benchmark, as well as newly designed, more complex tasks and dynamic on-device environments. MobileExplorer reduces the average number of reasoning steps and end-to-end latency by 23\%, while maintaining or improving task success rates by up to 5\%. A video demonstration of MobileExplorer performance in the real world is available at https://youtu.be/thK7MJmdlvM .

AI.tldr: MobileExplorer accelerates on-device inference for vision-based mobile GUI agents by performing lightweight, parallel exploration of UI elements during VLM reasoning time, using structured memory and a two-level rollback mechanism, reducing latency by 23% while maintaining/improving task success rates by up to 5%.
AI.motivation: Existing mobile GUI agents rely on cloud-hosted models, introducing privacy concerns and network latency. Fully on-device deployment remains underexplored, and current systems focus primarily on optimizing task accuracy rather than efficiency.
AI.method: MobileExplorer exploits long per-step reasoning time of VLMs by performing lightweight parallel exploration of UI elements during inference, recording traces as structured memory. It uses a two-level rollback mechanism to restore UI state when backtracking fails, and summarizes exploration traces into contextual hints injected into prompts.
AI.result: Evaluated on AndroidWorld benchmark and new complex tasks/dynamic environments. Reduces average reasoning steps and end-to-end latency by 23%, while maintaining or improving task success rates by up to 5%.
AI.conclusion: MobileExplorer demonstrates that on-device mobile GUI agents can be both efficient and effective by leveraging online exploration during VLM inference time, without sacrificing task performance.

title: OSMa-Bench++: Toward Open-Ended Benchmarking of Semantic Mapping for Manipulation with Prompt-Generated Synthetic Scenes
summary: Semantic mapping methods are increasingly used as intermediate scene representations for downstream robotic reasoning and manipulation, yet their evaluation is still largely tied to fixed benchmark datasets with limited coverage of manipulation-relevant corner cases. In this work, we extend OSMa-Bench toward controllable benchmarking with prompt-generated synthetic indoor scenes. Our pipeline automatically generates scene descriptions, synthesizes corresponding environments with SceneSmith, and adapts the resulting assets into an OSMa-Bench-compatible simulation format. This adaptation requires a nontrivial intermediate layer, including semantic normalization, material and texture repair, shader fallback policies, floor handling, navigation setup, and controlled lighting configuration. A key advantage of the proposed setup is that the original scene-generation prompt is known in advance and can therefore serve as an auxiliary semantic specification of the intended scene. We use this property to extend the VQA component of OSMa-Bench with a prompt-grounded question category. The resulting framework supports targeted stress-testing of semantic scene representations under conditions such as clutter, small objects, partial occlusions, and lighting variation, and makes benchmarking more extensible and better aligned with downstream manipulation requirements. Our code is available at https://github.com/be2rlab/OSMa-Bench-v2.

AI.tldr: This paper extends OSMa-Bench to enable controllable benchmarking of semantic mapping for robotics using prompt-generated synthetic indoor scenes, supporting stress-testing under manipulation-relevant conditions like clutter, occlusion, and lighting variation.
AI.motivation: Current semantic mapping evaluations rely on fixed benchmark datasets with limited coverage of manipulation-relevant corner cases (e.g., clutter, small objects, occlusions, lighting variation).
AI.method: The pipeline automatically generates scene descriptions via prompts, synthesizes environments with SceneSmith, and adapts assets into OSMa-Bench-compatible format via semantic normalization, material/texture repair, shader fallback, floor handling, navigation setup, and lighting configuration. The known prompt serves as auxiliary semantic ground truth, enabling a new prompt-grounded VQA question category.
AI.result: A framework that supports targeted stress-testing of semantic scene representations under challenging conditions (clutter, small objects, partial occlusions, lighting variation), making benchmarking more extensible and aligned with downstream manipulation needs.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-generated synthetic scenes enable more controllable and manipulation-relevant benchmarking of semantic mapping, overcoming limitations of fixed datasets.

title: Rethinking Weakly-supervised Video Temporal Grounding From a Game Perspective
summary: This paper addresses the challenging task of weakly-supervised video temporal grounding. Existing approaches are generally based on the moment proposal selection framework that utilizes contrastive learning and reconstruction paradigm for scoring the pre-defined moment proposals. Although they have achieved significant progress, we argue that their current frameworks have overlooked two indispensable issues: 1) Coarse-grained cross-modal learning: previous methods solely capture the global video-level alignment with the query, failing to model the detailed consistency between video frames and query words for accurately grounding the moment boundaries. 2) Complex moment proposals: their performance severely relies on the quality of proposals, which are also time-consuming and complicated for selection. To this end, in this paper, we make the first attempt to tackle this task from a novel game perspective, which effectively learns the uncertain relationship between each vision-language pair with diverse granularity and flexible combination for multi-level cross-modal interaction.Specifically, we creatively model each video frame and query word as game players with multivariate cooperative game theory to learn their contribution to the cross-modal similarity score. By quantifying the trend of frame-word cooperation within a coalition via the game-theoretic interaction, we are able to value all uncertain but possible correspondence between frames and words. Finally, instead of using moment proposals, we utilize the learned query-guided frame-wise scores for better moment localization.Experiments show that our method achieves superior performance on both Charades-STA and ActivityNet Caption datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a game-theoretic approach for weakly-supervised video temporal grounding that models frame-word interactions via cooperative game theory, eliminating the need for complex moment proposals and achieving superior performance on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing weakly-supervised video temporal grounding methods suffer from two overlooked issues: (1) coarse-grained cross-modal learning that only captures global video-level alignment without detailed frame-word consistency, and (2) reliance on complex, time-consuming moment proposals that severely affect performance.
AI.method: The authors model video frames and query words as game players using multivariate cooperative game theory. They quantify frame-word cooperation within coalitions via game-theoretic interaction to learn contribution to cross-modal similarity. Instead of using pre-defined moment proposals, they utilize learned query-guided frame-wise scores for moment localization.
AI.result: The method achieves superior performance on both Charades-STA and ActivityNet Caption datasets compared to existing weakly-supervised approaches.
AI.conclusion: The game-theoretic perspective effectively addresses coarse-grained cross-modal learning and eliminates the need for complex moment proposal selection, providing a new paradigm for weakly-supervised video temporal grounding.

title: Two-Parameter Flows for Learning Population Dynamics of Physical Systems
summary: This work addresses the problem of learning the dynamics of high-dimensional probability densities over time using unlabeled samples, without assuming access to trajectory information. We introduce two-parameter flows that learn only sampling-time transports from a base distribution to each marginal and then extract a physics-time velocity by regressing on coupled synthetic trajectories. We prove that the resulting physics-time dynamics are unique and inherit regularity from the sampling-time transports.
