AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes a fundamental flaw in the state-of-the-art algorithm for detecting commutative factors in factor graphs, where a necessary condition was mistakenly treated as sufficient, leading to incorrect results. The authors provide a corrected theorem, a fixed algorithm maintaining efficiency, and a complementary algorithm with better worst-case bounds.
AI.motivation: Lifted probabilistic inference relies on detecting commutative factors (factors invariant under input permutations) to exploit object indistinguishability. The current state-of-the-art algorithm has a theoretical flaw—its central theorem provides only a necessary condition but is mistakenly regarded as sufficient—potentially causing incorrect detection of commutative factors and thereby undermining the correctness of lifted inference.
AI.method: The authors first analyze the existing theorem to identify the sufficiency error, then prove a corrected necessary-condition theorem. They present a corrected version of the state-of-the-art algorithm that preserves its original efficiency, and additionally introduce a complementary algorithm with tighter worst-case complexity bounds.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that the original algorithm can produce incorrect results due to the flawed theorem. The corrected algorithm eliminates these errors while maintaining efficiency, and the complementary algorithm offers improved worst-case guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical foundation for detecting commutative factors in factor graphs must be corrected to ensure correctness in lifted probabilistic inference. The proposed fixes restore correctness without sacrificing efficiency, and the new complementary algorithm provides an alternative with tighter theoretical guarantees.

title: A Hybrid Vision-Language Architecture for Automated Defect Reasoning and Report Generation in Industrial Inspection
summary: Automated industrial inspection requires both precise defect localization and structured maintenance report generation; in current practice these tasks are handled separately, with linguistic interpretation left to human experts. This paper describes a decoupled, edge-deployable pipeline for wind turbine blade inspection built from three components that each handle a distinct sub-task. The Eyes a YOLO26-x-obb oriented bounding-box detector localizes defects at dataset-native resolution. The Bridge a deterministic, parameter-free encoding module maps each detected bounding box to grid-referenced spatial tokens embedded in a structured prompt. The Brain a 4-bit quantized Qwen-2.5-1.5B model adapted with Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation (QLoRA) on 947 synthetically generated maintenance reports generates a structured JSON report from that prompt. Retrieval-Augmented Fine-Tuning (RAFT) further grounds each recommendation in indexed maintenance procedures. Five ablation experiments, scored by BLEU-4, ROUGE-L, Hallucination Rate (HR), and an LLM-as-a-Judge rubric, compare the pipeline against a monolithic vision-language model (VLM) baseline and against partial configurations in which one component is removed. The complete system achieves BLEU-4 0.41, HR=4%, and Expert Score = 8.6/10 compared with 0.07, 65%, and 3.3/10 for the zero-shot VLM baseline. The QLoRA-adapted 1.5B model generates higher-quality reports than a 671B-parameter generalist API model given identical detection evidence, at 47 tokens per second on a single T4-class GPU. The results show that purpose-built decoupled architecture with a small domain-specific training corpus outperforms a generalist end-to-end model on this structured generation task.

AI.tldr: A decoupled, edge-deployable pipeline (Eyes-Bridge-Brain) for wind turbine blade inspection achieves BLEU-4 0.41, 4% hallucination rate, and 8.6/10 expert score, outperforming a zero-shot VLM baseline (0.07, 65%, 3.3/10) by using YOLO detection, spatial token encoding, and a 4-bit QLoRA-finetuned 1.5B model.
AI.motivation: Automated industrial inspection requires both precise defect localization and structured report generation, but these tasks are currently handled separately with linguistic interpretation left to human experts. The paper aims to create a unified, automated pipeline that bridges visual detection and language generation.
AI.method: Three-component decoupled pipeline: (1) Eyes — YOLO26-x-obb for oriented bounding-box defect detection; (2) Bridge — deterministic encoding module mapping detections to grid-referenced spatial tokens in a structured prompt; (3) Brain — 4-bit quantized Qwen-2.5-1.5B with QLoRA finetuned on 947 synthetic reports, plus RAFT for grounding recommendations.
AI.result: Complete system: BLEU-4 0.41, HR=4%, Expert Score=8.6/10 vs. zero-shot VLM baseline: 0.07, 65%, 3.3/10. The 1.5B QLoRA model outperformed a 671B generalist API model, achieving 47 tokens/sec on a T4 GPU.
AI.conclusion: A purpose-built decoupled architecture with a small domain-specific training corpus outperforms a generalist end-to-end VLM on structured report generation for industrial inspection, demonstrating effectiveness and efficiency for edge deployment.

title: Aperiodic and Low-Frequency Spectral Bias in Reconstruction based EEG Foundation Models
summary: EEG foundation models, pre-trained on large-scale unlabelled EEG data, have emerged as a promising direction towards learning generalizable EEG representations. Despite showing positive results in data-rich regimes, they often fail to outperform significantly smaller supervised models in low-resource settings compared to fully supervised models. We provide a mechanistic account of this shortcoming, attributing it to a fundamental mismatch between reconstruction-based pretext tasks and the idiosyncratic spectral structure of EEG signals, which decompose into distinct high-power aperiodic and low-power oscillatory components. Using controlled, synthetically-generated EEG inputs, we demonstrate that EEG foundation model embeddings are biased to capture the aperiodic components of the EEG signal while under-representing oscillatory components, particularly at higher frequencies. Additionally, linear probe evaluations on real-world BCI datasets further reveal that embeddings encode subject identity more strongly than task-relevant information, thereby reinforcing the low-frequency and aperiodic component bias in foundation model embeddings trained primarily on reconstruction based objectives. Together, these findings elucidate a failure mode in reconstruction based EEG foundation models and motivate future work to incorporate auxiliary losses explicitly targeting high-frequency oscillatory structure as a path toward more capable and generalizable EEG representations.

AI.tldr: EEG foundation models pre-trained on reconstruction tasks fail in low-resource settings because they over-represent aperiodic/low-frequency components and under-represent high-frequency oscillatory components, while also encoding subject identity over task-relevant information.
AI.motivation: To explain why EEG foundation models underperform smaller supervised models in low-resource settings, despite success in data-rich regimes.
AI.method: Mechanistic analysis using controlled synthetic EEG inputs to probe embedding biases; linear probe evaluations on real-world BCI datasets to assess what features are captured.
AI.result: Reconstruction-based pretext tasks bias embeddings toward aperiodic/low-frequency components and under-represent oscillatory components, especially at higher frequencies. Embeddings encode subject identity more strongly than task information.
AI.conclusion: Reconstruction-based objectives have a fundamental failure mode for EEG; future work should incorporate auxiliary losses targeting high-frequency oscillatory structure for better generalization.

title: TADDLE: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Detecting Deficient LLM-Generated Peer Reviews
summary: LLM-generated peer reviews are increasingly common at major venues, yet their deficiencies are hard to detect because they are uniformly fluent and well-structured. Existing work either classifies authorship without judging quality, or scores quality with features designed for human-written reviews; no prior system detects deficiencies in LLM-generated reviews at the level of individual defect types. To bridge the gap, we introduce TADDLE, a Tool-Augmented Agent for Detecting Deficient LLM-Generated Peer Reviews, together with the first expert-annotated benchmark for this task. Our benchmark comprises 1,800 reviews on 50 ICLR 2025 papers, multi-label-annotated by 18 domain experts against a taxonomy of six defect categories (plus a non-deficient label). TADDLE decomposes detection into four specialized analysis tools -- Verify, Correct, Complete, and Transform -- orchestrated by an agent; an integrator synthesizes their outputs into binary and multi-label classifications via two-stage semi-supervised learning. Extensive experiments show that TADDLE performs strongly on both binary detection and the multi-label classification task. We release the benchmark and code at https://github.com/AquariusAQ/TADDLE.

AI.tldr: TADDLE is a tool-augmented agent system that detects specific deficiency types in LLM-generated peer reviews, using a new expert-annotated benchmark of 1,800 reviews across six defect categories.
AI.motivation: LLM-generated peer reviews are increasingly common but their deficiencies are hard to detect due to fluent writing; existing work only classifies authorship or scores quality without identifying specific defect types.
AI.method: TADDLE decomposes detection into four specialized tools (Verify, Correct, Complete, Transform) orchestrated by an agent, with an integrator using two-stage semi-supervised learning for binary and multi-label classification.
AI.result: TADDLE performs strongly on both binary detection and multi-label classification tasks, validated on a benchmark of 1,800 reviews with 18 expert annotators covering six defect categories.
AI.conclusion: TADDLE bridges the gap in detecting individual defect types in LLM-generated peer reviews, providing both a system and benchmark for this task.

title: Scheduled Style Injection: Expanding the Style-Content Pareto Frontier in Training-Free Diffusion-based Style Transfer
summary: Style transfer with pre-trained diffusion models has advanced rapidly, but a core question remains underexplored: where in the model should style injection be strongest? StyleID, the leading training-free method, uses a single global parameter (gamma) uniformly across all layers and timesteps, which forces a fixed tradeoff between style quality and content preservation. We show this tradeoff is unnecessarily rigid. We systematically explore four dimensions of control: varying style injection strength across decoder layers, across denoising timesteps, and scheduling ControlNet geometric conditioning along both axes. The pattern is consistent everywhere: decreasing schedules, with stronger structural signal injection in shallower layers and earlier timesteps, reliably outperform the reverse. Beyond direction, schedule shape matters: cosine and square-root timestep schedules outperform linear. Most importantly, we find that gamma scheduling and ControlNet conditioning are nearly independent. The resulting combined configurations expand the Pareto frontier, offering superior tradeoffs between style fidelity and content preservation compared to any single baseline setting. Our best balanced configuration achieves ArtFID of 27.036 versus StyleID's 28.801 - a 6.1% relative improvement, with consistent gains across the full style-content tradeoff frontier. Results are validated across 35 configurations totaling over 28,000 stylized images using four complementary metrics. These findings generalize across SD backbones with identical rank ordering. All modifications are training-free, parameter-free, and require only a few lines of scheduling code; code is available at https://github.com/ameyskulkarni/scheduled_style_injection.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that scheduling style injection strength with decreasing schedules (stronger injection in shallower layers and earlier timesteps) using cosine/square-root patterns significantly improves style transfer quality, and shows gamma scheduling and ControlNet conditioning are nearly independent, enabling combined Pareto-optimal configurations that achieve 6.1% relative improvement over StyleID.
AI.motivation: StyleID, the current leading training-free style transfer method, uses a single global parameter (gamma) uniformly across all layers and timesteps, forcing a fixed and unnecessarily rigid tradeoff between style quality and content preservation. The authors aim to explore whether more nuanced scheduling of style injection can improve this tradeoff.
AI.method: The authors systematically explore four dimensions of control: (1) varying style injection strength across decoder layers, (2) across denoising timesteps, (3) scheduling ControlNet geometric conditioning along both axes. They test different schedule directions (decreasing vs. increasing) and shapes (linear, cosine, square-root) across 35 configurations totaling over 28,000 stylized images.
AI.result: Decreasing schedules (stronger injection in shallower layers and earlier timesteps) consistently outperform increasing schedules. Cosine and square-root timestep schedules outperform linear ones. Gamma scheduling and ControlNet conditioning are nearly independent, allowing combined configurations to expand the Pareto frontier. Best balanced configuration achieves ArtFID of 27.036 vs StyleID's 28.801 (6.1% relative improvement). Results generalize across SD backbones.
AI.conclusion: Simple, training-free scheduling of style injection strength along multiple dimensions yields consistent and substantial improvements over uniform injection. The near-independence of gamma scheduling and ControlNet conditioning enables flexible combination for superior style-content tradeoffs with minimal code changes.

title: DDGAD: Trajectory Dynamics for Diffusion-Based Graph Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify nodes or substructures whose behavior or attributes deviate significantly from the overall pattern in graph-structured data, with critical applications in financial risk control, social network analysis, and cybersecurity. However, existing GCN-based methods suffer from the fundamental problem of contamination propagation, where anomalous nodes pollute the representations of their neighbors through message passing, leading to degraded detection performance. In this paper, we propose DDGAD, a novel diffusion-based graph anomaly detection framework that leverages trajectory dynamics to distinguish normal and anomalous nodes. Our key insight is that normal nodes exhibit consistent and stable representation trajectories under the coupled effects of diffusion regularization and reliability-aware neighborhood consensus, while anomalous nodes exhibit unstable and conflicting dynamics due to the directional disagreement between the global manifold prior and locally contaminated message passing. To mitigate contamination propagation, we introduce a distributed reliability-aware consensus refinement mechanism and define three complementary anomaly signals: neighbor inconsistency, reliability weight, and dynamical conflict energy. We further provide a preliminary theoretical analysis on normal node stability under the coupled dynamics. These signals collectively characterize anomalous behaviors from the perspectives of local inconsistency, consensus reliability, and dynamical instability. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: DDGAD is a diffusion-based graph anomaly detection framework that uses trajectory dynamics to distinguish normal vs. anomalous nodes by analyzing consistency and stability under diffusion regularization and reliability-aware neighborhood consensus, outperforming existing GCN-based methods that suffer from contamination propagation.
AI.motivation: Existing GCN-based graph anomaly detection methods suffer from contamination propagation, where anomalous nodes pollute the representations of their neighbors through message passing, degrading detection performance.
AI.method: Proposes DDGAD framework leveraging trajectory dynamics with (1) diffusion regularization, (2) reliability-aware neighborhood consensus, (3) distributed reliability-aware consensus refinement, and (4) three complementary anomaly signals: neighbor inconsistency, reliability weight, and dynamical conflict energy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: DDGAD effectively mitigates contamination propagation and improves anomaly detection by exploiting the differential dynamical behaviors of normal (stable) vs. anomalous (unstable) nodes.

title: From Norms to Indicators (N2I-RAG): An Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Legal Indicator Computation
summary: Computing legal indicators from normative texts is a key task in legal monitoring and policy evaluation, but presents significant challenges due to the complexity, scale, and interpretive nature of legal language, as well as the variability in available document quality. Existing natural language processing techniques and generative models can assist in legal analysis, but often suffer from high risk of hallucinations and lack the interpretability and evidence grounding required for reliable indicator computation. This paper presents N2I-RAG (From Norms to Indicators), an agentic retrieval-augmented generation framework designed to automate the computation of legal indicators in a transparent and traceable way. We integrate adaptive retrieval, llm-based agents, and validation mechanisms in a modular pipeline, where each component performs a defined role in filtering, retrieving, and assessing evidence, and in producing binary legal outcomes linked to identifiable legal provisions. The framework emphasizes traceability by requiring explicit explanations of intermediate decisions and final indicator assignments. We evaluate N2I-RAG using an in-house constructed French marine environmental law corpus that includes both scanned and digital sources. Comparative experiments with multiple language model families demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms baseline systems, and generalizes well when tested on 2 different bans. The results indicate that agentic retrieval-augmented generation can bridge open-text legal language and standardized indicator computation, offering a foundation for transparent and scalable legal observatories.

AI.tldr: N2I-RAG is a retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses LLM agents and adaptive retrieval to compute legal indicators from normative texts transparently and traceably, outperforming baselines on French marine environmental law.
AI.motivation: Computing legal indicators from normative texts is critical for legal monitoring and policy evaluation, but existing NLP and generative models suffer from hallucinations, lack interpretability, and lack evidence grounding needed for reliable indicator computation.
AI.method: N2I-RAG is an agentic retrieval-augmented generation framework with a modular pipeline integrating adaptive retrieval, LLM-based agents, and validation mechanisms. Each component filters, retrieves, and assesses evidence, linking binary legal outcomes to identifiable legal provisions with explicit intermediate explanations.
AI.result: Evaluated on an in-house French marine environmental law corpus (scanned and digital sources). N2I-RAG consistently outperforms baseline systems across multiple LLM families and generalizes well when tested on 2 different bans.
AI.conclusion: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation can bridge open-text legal language and standardized indicator computation, providing a foundation for transparent and scalable legal observatories.

title: TrackRef3D: Multi-View Consistent Track-then-Label for Open-World Referring Segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Referring 3D Gaussian Splatting (R3DGS), which utilizes natural language for 3D object segmentation, has emerged as a crucial capability for embodied AI. However, existing methods typically rely on expensive per-scene manual annotation and per-view pseudo mask generation, which suffer from multi-view inconsistency and poor generalization to varying query specificities. To address this, we present TrackRef3D, a fully automatic pipeline that achieves open-world referring segmentation in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) without manual annotation by introducing a multi-view consistent track-then-label paradigm that fundamentally decouples object discovery from semantic grounding. Specifically, we propose a Trajectory-Aware Semantic Consensus Module (TSCM) which aggregates cross-view predictions via synonymous clustering and trajectory-aware voting to establish a canonical semantic identity, thereby ensuring multi-view consistency. Furthermore, we employ a visibility-aware description generation strategy to mitigate ambiguity and propose a Hybrid Training Strategy (HTS) that jointly optimizes coarse category semantics and fine-grained referential cues to ensure robustness under varying query specificities using a multi-positive contrastive objective. Extensive experiments on benchmarks demonstrate that TrackRef3D achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: TrackRef3D is a fully automatic pipeline for open-world referring 3D segmentation in 3DGS, eliminating manual annotation via a multi-view consistent track-then-label paradigm with trajectory-aware semantic consensus and hybrid training strategy.
AI.motivation: Existing R3DGS methods rely on expensive per-scene manual annotation and per-view pseudo mask generation, suffering from multi-view inconsistency and poor generalization to varying query specificities.
AI.method: Proposes a track-then-label paradigm with: (1) Trajectory-Aware Semantic Consensus Module (TSCM) for cross-view aggregation via synonymous clustering and trajectory-aware voting; (2) visibility-aware description generation; (3) Hybrid Training Strategy (HTS) with multi-positive contrastive objective.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TrackRef3D successfully achieves open-world referring segmentation in 3DGS without manual annotation, addressing multi-view inconsistency and varying query specificity challenges.

title: MuCon: Clipped Muon Updates for LLM Training
summary: Muon-style optimizers take a matrix-valued momentum or preconditioned update $B = U \operatorname{diag}(σ_1,\ldots,σ_r) V^\top$ and replace it with its canonical partial polar factor $\operatorname{Pol}(B) = U V^\top$. This maps every nonzero singular value to one. MuCon is the clipped-Muon variant studied here: it applies singular-value clipping to the same Muon matrix, $D^{\mathrm{MuCon}}\_τ(B) = \operatorname{MClip}\_τ(B) = U \operatorname{diag}\bigl(\min\{σ\_i,τ\}\bigr) V^\top, \qquad τ> 0$. Thus, $\operatorname{MClip}\_τ$ denotes the mathematical clipping operator, while MuCon denotes the optimizer primitive that substitutes this clipped direction for Muon's polar direction. The Muon/MuCon scaling parameterization used in this work is called $\text{SpectralP}$: it is the hidden-matrix scaling recipe under which polar Muon or clipped MuCon directions are applied. The map $\operatorname{MClip}\_τ$ is the Frobenius projection onto the spectral-norm ball $\{X : \|X\|_2 \le τ\}$: it leaves singular values at or below $τ$ unchanged and modifies only the violating singular directions. This paper asks when the MuCon clipping step can be approximated without a full dense SVD. We record two exact identities, a polar/absolute-value formula and a scalar-root formulation leading to a rational Newton filter for the clipped positive-semidefinite factor, and identify the numerical obstruction common to both: singular values near the threshold make sign decisions and rational solves ill-conditioned. Matrix-function methods are therefore useful only when paired with stable polar/square-root primitives or explicit regularization near the clipping boundary.

AI.tldr: MuCon is a Muon-style optimizer that clips singular values (instead of forcing them to 1 like polar). Two exact identities for SVD-free clipping approximations are shown, but both become ill-conditioned near the threshold—so stable primitives or regularization are needed.
AI.motivation: Muon-style optimizers replace matrix momentum with its polar factor, but this discards all singular-value information. MuCon proposes clipping singular values at a threshold τ instead, retaining smaller singular values. The paper seeks efficient (SVD-free) approximations for this clipping step, which is currently costly due to full dense SVD.
AI.method: Define MuCon's clipping operator MClip_τ(B) = U diag(min{σ_i, τ}) V^T. Two exact identities are derived: (1) a polar/absolute-value formula, (2) a scalar-root formulation that leads to a rational Newton filter for the clipped PSD factor. The numerical obstructions (ill-conditioning near threshold) are identified for both methods.
AI.result: Both exact SVD-free formulas become ill-conditioned when singular values are near the clipping threshold τ, making sign decisions and rational solves unstable. Regularization or stable polar/square-root primitives are required for practical use.
AI.conclusion: MuCon's clipping step cannot be naively approximated without SVD; matrix-function methods only work when paired with stable primitives or explicit regularization near the clipping boundary.

title: Developing a Totally Unimodular Linear Program for Optimal Conformance Checking: When and Why It Complements A*
comment: Author-accepted manuscript accepted for publication in Expert Systems with Applications. Code and experiment scripts are available at: https://github.com/Izack-Cohen/unimodular-conformance-checking. Version corresponding to the accepted paper: v1.0.0
summary:
Alignment-based conformance checking is the state-of-the-art approach for comparing observed process executions with normative process models. The standard exact solution relies on an A*-based heuristic search, which can exhibit exponential runtime in the presence of long traces or substantial deviations.
  This paper introduces a reformulation of alignment-based conformance checking as a totally unimodular linear program (LP) defined on the reachability graph of the synchronous product. By exploiting the underlying network-flow structure, the proposed formulation guarantees the existence of an integral optimal extreme-point solution through LP relaxation, thereby avoiding the combinatorial overhead associated with integer variables and branch-and-bound search.
  We conduct an extensive empirical evaluation on more than 2.1 million conformance checking instances derived from real-world and synthetic benchmark datasets. The results show that A* and the LP approach exhibit complementary performance characteristics: the former performs best on short, well-conforming traces, while the LP formulation provides substantial speedups for longer traces with deviations, precisely where conformance checking is most informative. Based on these findings, we derive simple algorithm-selection guidelines that combine both approaches, achieving average runtime savings of 38.6% with 96% selection accuracy compared to always using A*.

AI.tldr: This paper reformulates alignment-based conformance checking as a totally unimodular linear program (LP) on the reachability graph of the synchronous product, avoiding combinatorial overhead. Empirical evaluation on 2.1M instances shows LP excels on long, deviating traces while A* works best on short, well-conforming ones; combining both achieves 38.6% average runtime savings with 96% selection accuracy.
AI.motivation: Standard alignment-based conformance checking relies on A* heuristic search, which can exhibit exponential runtime for long traces or substantial deviations, creating a need for more efficient solutions.
AI.method: The authors reformulate conformance checking as a totally unimodular linear program (LP) defined on the reachability graph of the synchronous product, leveraging network-flow structure to guarantee integral optimal solutions via LP relaxation without integer variables or branch-and-bound.
AI.result: Evaluation on 2.1M instances shows A* works best on short, well-conforming traces, while the LP approach provides substantial speedups for longer traces with deviations—the most informative cases. Combined selection guidelines achieve 38.6% average runtime savings with 96% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The LP reformulation effectively complements A*, and simple algorithm-selection guidelines combining both approaches yield significant efficiency gains for conformance checking.

title: O-MARC: Omni Memory-Augmented Compression Distillation for Efficient Video Understanding
summary: Omnimodal large language models enable unified audio video understanding, but long joint token sequences make inference costly, and existing benchmarks do not fully isolate audio visual association in noisy user generated videos. We introduce UGC-AVQA, a public UGC benchmark with 1,000 videos and 4,816 QA pairs, where an audio removal test ensures that benchmark questions require both acoustic and visual evidence. To reduce inference cost, we propose OMAC, a training free plug in compression method that preserves salient visual memory and temporally grounded audio anchors. To further make compact models robust to compressed inputs, we introduce O-MARC, a compression distillation framework for learning with memory compressed multimodal contexts. On Qwen2.5-Omni-3B, O-MARC improves the average score across four benchmarks to 45.8, outperforming full token inference at 44.1 and OmniZip at 41.0. OMAC also keeps inference efficient, reducing latency by 34.6\% (1.53$\times$ speedup) and memory by 34.7\% compared with full token inference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UGC-AVQA (a benchmark for audio-visual QA in user-generated content), OMAC (a training-free compression method for multimodal models), and O-MARC (a distillation framework to make compact models robust to compressed inputs), achieving better accuracy than full-token inference while reducing latency and memory.
AI.motivation: Long joint token sequences make omnimodal LLM inference costly, and existing benchmarks do not fully isolate audio-visual association in noisy user-generated videos.
AI.method: UGC-AVQA benchmark with audio removal test; OMAC (training-free plug-in compression preserving visual memory and audio anchors); O-MARC (compression distillation framework for learning with memory-compressed multimodal contexts).
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-Omni-3B, O-MARC achieves 45.8 average score (vs. full token 44.1 and OmniZip 41.0); OMAC reduces latency by 34.6% and memory by 34.7%.
AI.conclusion: Proposed methods improve both efficiency and accuracy for omnimodal video understanding, validated on a new dedicated UGC benchmark.

title: Diffuse to Detect: Generative Diffusion Models for Unsupervised IC Anomaly Detection
summary: Latent defect screening is challenged by extremely low failure rates, high-dimensional test data, and absence of labeled anomalies. We propose the first unsupervised anomaly detection framework incorporating a Diffusion Transformer. Raw test measurements are first compressed by an autoencoder, then reshaped into a structured token sequence enriched with sinusoidal and per-device wafer-position embeddings. Anomaly scores are derived from the noise-prediction error over mid-range diffusion timesteps, enabling fast wafer-scale screening without any labeled defects or manual feature engineering. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on industrial 16nm IC test data under extreme class imbalance, offering interpretable failure localization through latent-space reconstruction residuals.

AI.tldr: First unsupervised anomaly detection framework using Diffusion Transformer for latent defect screening in semiconductor manufacturing, achieving SOTA on 16nm IC test data without labeled defects.
AI.motivation: Latent defect screening is extremely challenging due to very low failure rates, high-dimensional test data, and the lack of labeled anomaly examples in real industrial settings.
AI.method: Raw test measurements are compressed by an autoencoder, reshaped into structured token sequences with sinusoidal and wafer-position embeddings, then fed into a Diffusion Transformer. Anomaly scores are computed from noise-prediction error over mid-range diffusion timesteps.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on industrial 16nm IC test data under extreme class imbalance conditions.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables fast wafer-scale screening without labeled defects or manual feature engineering, with interpretable failure localization via latent-space reconstruction residuals.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Verification of LLM Outputs for Data-Sensitive Domains (extended preprint)
comment: Extended preprint version of accepted technical communication at KI 2026. 22 pages, 3 figures
summary: LLMs deployed in high-stakes domains face fundamental reliability challenges: hallucinations, inconsistencies, and privacy vulnerabilities introduce unacceptable risks where errors carry legal, financial, or safety consequences. This paper presents a hybrid verification architecture combining formal symbolic methods with neural semantic analysis to provide complementary guarantees for LLM-generated content. This architecture employs logical reasoning for input verification, leveraging completeness properties to provide decidable guarantees on structured requirements. For output validation, embedding-based semantic similarity detects contextual hallucinations where formal methods lack expressiveness. This separation is realized in a parallel, actor-based pipeline, addressing limitations of prompt-based self-verification approaches, which inherit the distributional biases that produce hallucinations. The proposed architecture and type-aware verification method are validated with HAIMEDA, a real-world medical device damage assessment reporting system developed through Action Design Research. Evaluation shows hallucination detection rates of over 83% for structured entities and 72% for semantic fabrications, with a 30% reduction in report creation time, demonstrating that neuro-symbolic architectures can provide principled safeguards for LLM deployment in data-sensitive domains.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a hybrid neuro-symbolic verification architecture combining formal symbolic methods (logical reasoning for input) with neural semantic analysis (embedding-based similarity for output) to detect hallucinations and inconsistencies in LLM-generated content, validated on a medical device damage assessment system (HAIMEDA) achieving 83% structured entity detection, 72% semantic fabrication detection, and 30% reduction in report creation time.
AI.motivation: LLMs deployed in high-stakes domains face fundamental reliability challenges (hallucinations, inconsistencies, privacy vulnerabilities) where errors carry legal, financial, or safety consequences, and existing prompt-based self-verification methods inherit the same distributional biases that produce hallucinations.
AI.method: A hybrid verification architecture combining (1) formal symbolic methods with logical reasoning for input verification providing decidable guarantees on structured requirements, and (2) neural semantic analysis using embedding-based similarity for output validation to detect contextual hallucinations where formal methods lack expressiveness, implemented as a parallel actor-based pipeline.
AI.result: Validated on HAIMEDA (medical device damage assessment system): 83% hallucination detection for structured entities, 72% for semantic fabrications, and 30% reduction in report creation time.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic architectures can provide principled safeguards for LLM deployment in data-sensitive domains, offering complementary guarantees that neither purely formal nor purely neural approaches can achieve alone.

title: FTibSuite: A Comprehensive Resource Suite for Tibetan Vision-Language Modeling
summary: Vision-language models have progressed rapidly, but Tibetan remains a severely underserved low-resource language due to the lack of reproducible training and evaluation infrastructure. To fill this gap, we introduce FTibSuite, a comprehensive resource suite for Tibetan vision-language research, consisting of FTibData (human-verified multimodal training corpora spanning continual pretraining, image-text alignment, and instruction tuning data), FTibBench (Tibetan adaptations of five mainstream multimodal benchmarks with a hierarchical quality-control workflow to reduce translation noise), and FTibVLM, a reproducible baseline built on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct via a three-stage adaptation pipeline. Experiments on FTibBench show FTibVLM delivers consistent performance gains across all tasks, such as improving MMBench accuracy from 42.97 to 67.78 and POPE-random accuracy from 47.53 to 80.56, while retaining the backbone's original Chinese capabilities with minimal degradation, providing the first standardized foundation for Tibetan multimodal research.

AI.tldr: FTibSuite is a comprehensive resource suite for Tibetan vision-language research, including multimodal datasets (FTibData), benchmarks (FTibBench), and a reproducible baseline VLM (FTibVLM). Experiments show significant performance gains while preserving the backbone's original Chinese capabilities.
AI.motivation: Tibetan is a severely underserved low-resource language in vision-language research, lacking reproducible training and evaluation infrastructure.
AI.method: The authors build FTibSuite with three components: FTibData (human-verified multimodal training corpora for continual pretraining, alignment, and instruction tuning), FTibBench (Tibetan adaptations of five mainstream benchmarks with a hierarchical quality-control workflow), and FTibVLM (a baseline built on Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct via three-stage adaptation).
AI.result: FTibVLM achieves consistent improvements, e.g., MMBench from 42.97→67.78 and POPE-random from 47.53→80.56, with minimal degradation of original Chinese capabilities.
AI.conclusion: FTibSuite provides the first standardized foundation for Tibetan multimodal research, enabling reproducible and effective vision-language modeling in this low-resource language.

title: Variational Inference for Evidential Deep Learning
summary: While Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) achieve remarkable performance, their tendency to produce overconfident predictions. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) mitigates this by formulating predictions as a Dirichlet distribution over class probabilities to explicitly quantify epistemic uncertainty. However, we found that the conventional EDL suffers from two fundamental limitations: a Kullback-Leibler (KL) penalty that only suppresses the evidence of negative classes, producing excessively high evidence therefore decreasing the model's ability to quantify uncertainty, and an absence in theoretical guarantee of setting Dirichlet parameter $α=e+1$. In this paper, we propose a mathematically principled framework, Variational Inference Evidential Deep Learning (VI-EDL). By reformulating evidential learning through the lens of variational inference, we derive an Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which prevents the evidence from growing excessively. Theoretically, we rigorously establish a generalization bound and reveal how the predicted uncertainty, feature and network complexity affect this bound, and why setting $\boldsymbolα = \mathbf{e} + \mathbf{1}$ can minimize it. Extensive experiments on standard visual and medical datasets demonstrate that VI-EDL achieves state-of-the-art performance, showing excellent performance in out-of-distribution detection, noise detection and autonomous driving scenario. The code is available in https://github.com/seutjw/VI-EDL.

AI.tldr: VI-EDL reformulates Evidential Deep Learning using variational inference to derive a proper ELBO, addressing the issues of overconfident evidence and lack of theoretical guarantees in conventional EDL, achieving SOTA on OOD detection, noise detection, and autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Conventional Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) suffers from (1) a KL penalty that only suppresses negative class evidence, causing excessively high evidence and poor uncertainty quantification, and (2) no theoretical guarantee for setting Dirichlet parameter α = e + 1.
AI.method: Proposes Variational Inference Evidential Deep Learning (VI-EDL), which reformulates evidential learning via variational inference to derive a proper Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) that prevents evidence from growing excessively. Also provides a theoretical generalization bound linking predicted uncertainty, feature complexity, and network complexity, explaining why α = e + 1 minimizes this bound.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard visual and medical datasets, with strong results in out-of-distribution detection, noise detection, and autonomous driving scenarios.
AI.conclusion: VI-EDL provides a mathematically principled and theoretically grounded improvement over conventional EDL, with demonstrated empirical superiority across multiple tasks.

title: LELA: An End-to-end LLM-based Entity Linking Framework with Zero-shot Domain Adaptation
summary: Entity linking is a key component of many downstream NLP systems, yet existing approaches are often tied to the specific target knowledge bases and domains, limiting their real world application. In this paper, we extend LELA, a modular and domain-agnostic LLM-based entity disambiguation method, into a practical Python library that integrates zero-shot Named Entity Recognition (NER) -thereby providing a complete end-toend pipeline for entity-linking in real-world usage. We provide experimental results validating LELA's performance and robustness across diverse entity linking settings. In our demo, users can play with the system on their own input texts.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the LELA entity disambiguation method into a complete Python library with integrated zero-shot NER, providing an end-to-end entity linking pipeline that is domain-agnostic and validated across diverse settings.
AI.motivation: Existing entity linking approaches are tightly coupled to specific knowledge bases and domains, limiting their real-world applicability. The authors aim to address this gap by creating a practical, domain-agnostic solution.
AI.method: The authors extend LELA (a modular, LLM-based entity disambiguation method) into a Python library, integrating zero-shot Named Entity Recognition (NER) to form a complete end-to-end entity linking pipeline.
AI.result: Experimental results validate LELA's performance and robustness across diverse entity linking settings. A demo system allows users to test with their own input texts.
AI.conclusion: The library enables practical, domain-agnostic entity linking for real-world applications, bridging the gap between existing approach limitations and practical deployment needs.

title: Gaussian-Voxel Duet: A Dual-Scaffolding Hybrid Representation for Fast and Accurate Monocular Surface Reconstruction
summary: While 3D Gaussian Splatting has achieved remarkable success in photorealistic novel view synthesis, its pursuit of fast and high-fidelity 3D reconstruction has long been constrained by a trade-off between geometric accuracy and optimization efficiency. Methods specialized in image rendering converge quickly at the cost of imperfect geometry caused by superfluous primitives overfitting training views, while methods integrating neural signed-distance field (SDF) for better geometry incur prohibitive training costs. In this paper, we attempt to strike a better trade-off by tethering scaffold-anchored Gaussians to a jointly optimized sparse voxel scaffold. This hybrid Gaussian-Voxel representation explicitly confines anchored Gaussians to a narrow band around surfaces defined by voxelized SDFs, which effectively improves representation efficiency and condenses floating Gaussians without sacrificing geometry quality. An implicit surface tethering loss further pulls individual Gaussian primitives closer to SDF-induced surfaces in a mutually regularized manner for improved reconstruction accuracy. Extensive experiments on diverse real-world indoor scenes from ScanNet++, ScanNetv2, and DeepBlending datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art surface reconstruction quality as well as superior novel view synthesis against leading baselines, while maintaining fast training convergence and real-time rendering. Code will be available at https://github.com/duzh11/VoxelGS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VoxelGS, a hybrid Gaussian-Voxel representation that anchors Gaussians to a sparse voxel scaffold with SDF-defined narrow surfaces, achieving both high-quality geometry reconstruction and fast training/rendering for novel view synthesis.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting methods face a trade-off: fast rendering methods produce poor geometry due to overfitting, while neural SDF-based methods achieve better geometry but are computationally expensive. The authors aim to strike a better balance between geometric accuracy and optimization efficiency.
AI.method: A hybrid Gaussian-Voxel representation where scaffold-anchored Gaussians are confined to a narrow band around surfaces defined by voxelized SDFs. An implicit surface tethering loss pulls Gaussians closer to SDF-induced surfaces for mutual regularization.
AI.result: State-of-the-art surface reconstruction quality and superior novel view synthesis on ScanNet++, ScanNetv2, and DeepBlending datasets, while maintaining fast training convergence and real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid representation effectively resolves the geometry-efficiency trade-off, offering both accurate geometry and fast optimization without sacrificing rendering quality.

title: Extra-Merge: Tracing the Rank-1 Subspace of Model Merging in Language Model Pre-Training
summary: Model merging has emerged as a lightweight paradigm for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this work, we analyze late-stage pre-training trajectories and uncover a \textbf{Rank-1 Subspace} phenomenon: while raw optimization steps oscillate violently, consecutive \emph{merged} checkpoints collapse onto a stable, approximately one-dimensional linear manifold. We theoretically ground this observation in a \emph{river-valley} landscape analysis: averaging acts as a geometric low-pass filter that dampens high-curvature noise to reveal the optimal descent direction. Capitalizing on this insight, we propose \textbf{Extra-Merge}, a training-free strategy that extrapolates along this subspace to minimize loss without additional gradient updates. Extensive experiments across GPT-2 and LLaMA families (124M to 2B) demonstrate that Extra-Merge consistently outperforms standard merging baselines. Notably, it yields consistent zero-shot accuracy gains on Pythia-12B downstream tasks and generalizes effectively to the Muon optimizer \citep{jordan2024muon}.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers a Rank-1 Subspace phenomenon in LLM pre-training trajectories, proposes Extra-Merge - a training-free method that extrapolates along this subspace to reduce loss, and demonstrates consistent improvements across GPT-2, LLaMA, and Pythia models up to 12B parameters.
AI.motivation: Model merging is a lightweight paradigm for enhancing LLMs but its underlying mechanisms are poorly understood. The authors aim to demystify what happens during model merging and leverage these insights for better performance.
AI.method: The authors identify a Rank-1 Subspace phenomenon where merged checkpoints collapse onto a stable one-dimensional linear manifold. They ground this in a river-valley landscape analysis showing averaging acts as a geometric low-pass filter. Based on this, they propose Extra-Merge, a training-free extrapolation strategy along this subspace.
AI.result: Extra-Merge consistently outperforms standard merging baselines across GPT-2 and LLaMA families (124M to 2B), yields zero-shot accuracy gains on Pythia-12B downstream tasks, and generalizes effectively to the Muon optimizer.
AI.conclusion: Model merging's effectiveness stems from the Rank-1 Subspace phenomenon where averaging acts as a low-pass filter. Extra-Merge successfully exploits this geometric insight for loss reduction without additional training.

title: Generating Robust Portfolios of Optimization Models using Large Language Models
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 LM4Plan Workshop
summary: Mathematical optimization is a powerful tool for structured decision-making across domains such as resource allocation and planning. Formulating optimization models faithful to reality, though, remains a significant bottleneck as it typically demands both domain expertise and optimization knowledge that are often scarce. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) promise to bridge this gap, enabling the generation of candidate optimization models from natural language descriptions. However, there is no guarantee that any single LLM-generated model is reliable, and existing approaches that output only one model are therefore risky. In this work, we propose a novel algorithm that generates a portfolio of optimization models, designed to be robust to the limitations of LLMs. Our method exploits the observation that a single LLM can play two distinct roles $\unicode{x2014}$ as a stochastic generator and as a reasoning evaluator $\unicode{x2014}$ and proposes a unified framework that leverages both capabilities in a complementary manner. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that, as long as either the generator or the evaluator is well-aligned with human preferences, the portfolio is guaranteed to contain high-quality candidates, enabling a principled human-in-the-loop process in which a decision-maker can review multiple candidates before committing to one. We further validate our approach empirically, demonstrating strong performance across a range of optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a portfolio-based algorithm using LLMs as both generator and evaluator to produce multiple reliable optimization models from natural language, with theoretical guarantees on quality.
AI.motivation: Formulating optimization models from natural language is difficult and requires scarce expertise; single LLM-generated models lack reliability guarantees.
AI.method: A unified framework where an LLM plays two roles: (1) a stochastic generator producing multiple candidate models, and (2) a reasoning evaluator assessing them. The portfolio approach ensures robustness with theoretical guarantees if either role is well-aligned.
AI.result: Strong empirical performance across a range of optimization modeling tasks; the portfolio reliably contains high-quality candidates.
AI.conclusion: The portfolio method enables principled human-in-the-loop decision-making by letting users review multiple candidates, with guarantees on quality under alignment conditions.

title: MedVol-R1: Reward-Driven Evidence Grounding for Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation
summary: Volumetric Reasoning Segmentation (VRS) aims to segment a target region in a 3D medical scan from a free-form clinical query, where the referent is often implicit and requires both medical knowledge and volume-grounded reasoning. Existing methods typically rely on specialized segmentation tokens to connect language with mask decoding, but this coupling collapses the decision process into opaque latent representations, limiting interpretability and generalization to diverse narrative expressions. In this paper, we present MedVol-R1, a reinforcement learning-based framework for VRS that explicitly decouples evidence grounding from volumetric delineation: the LVLM grounds clinical reasoning to a verifiable 2D evidence anchor (key axial slice and 2D bounding boxes), which is then propagated into a coherent 3D mask by a frozen MedSAM2 module. We train MedVol-R1 with cold-start supervised fine-tuning followed by GRPO, guided by a multi-component reward that encourages informative evidence selection, accurate 2D spatial grounding, and cross-slice volumetric coherence, without requiring costly chain-of-thought annotations. Experiments on CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 from the M3D-Seg benchmark demonstrate that MedVol-R1 consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance, with reinforcement learning providing clear gains over pure supervised fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: MedVol-R1 is a reinforcement learning framework for 3D medical image segmentation that decouples reasoning from mask prediction by first grounding clinical queries to a 2D evidence anchor, then propagating to 3D masks, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VRS methods couple language understanding with mask decoding into opaque latent representations, limiting interpretability and generalization to diverse narrative expressions in clinical queries.
AI.method: MedVol-R1 uses a two-stage pipeline: (1) an LVLM grounds clinical reasoning to a 2D evidence anchor (key axial slice + 2D bounding boxes), (2) a frozen MedSAM2 propagates this to a coherent 3D mask. Trained with cold-start SFT + GRPO reinforcement learning, guided by multi-component rewards for evidence selection, spatial grounding, and volumetric coherence.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines on CT-ORG, AbdomenCT-1K, and KiTS23 (M3D-Seg benchmark), achieving state-of-the-art performance. RL provides clear gains over pure supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling evidence grounding from volumetric delineation improves interpretability and generalization for VRS, and reinforcement learning effectively enhances performance beyond supervised fine-tuning alone.

title: The Stability of Singular Distribution: A Spectral Perspective on the Two-Phase Dynamics of Language Model Pre-training
summary: Large language model pre-training typically exhibits a two-phase trajectory: a fast initial loss drop followed by a prolonged slow improvement. We identify an underlying spectral phenomenon, Stability of Singular Distribution (SoSD), where the trace-normalized singular value spectrum stabilizes early, even as parameter matrices continue to evolve. We demonstrate that synchronization between SoSD and the slow-descent regime is widely observed across diverse architectures (GPT-2, LLaMA) and settings, including various schedules (Step-wise, WSD, Cosine Decay), weight decays, and optimizers (AdamW, Muon). By analyzing a simplified Transformer, we prove that growing weight norms inevitably precipitate an early SoSD threshold, after which the rate of loss decrease becomes theoretically bounded by the variation in the singular distribution. We further interpret strategies like WSD and Muon through their ability to modulate the SoSD scale, offering a spectral lens for understanding efficient pre-training dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a spectral phenomenon called Stability of Singular Distribution (SoSD) in LLM pre-training, where the singular value spectrum stabilizes early despite ongoing parameter changes, marking a transition to slow loss improvement. This is validated across architectures and training setups, with theoretical proof linking weight norms to SoSD and loss rate bounds.
AI.motivation: To understand the two-phase loss trajectory in LLM pre-training (fast drop then slow improvement) through a spectral lens, and explain why certain strategies like WSD and Muon are effective.
AI.method: Empirical observation of SoSD across GPT-2, LLaMA with various schedules/optimizers; theoretical proof using a simplified Transformer showing weight norm growth causes SoSD onset, bounding loss decrease rate thereafter.
AI.result: SoSD synchronizes with the slow-descent regime universally; weight norms drive early SoSD threshold; WSD and Muon modulate SoSD scale to improve efficiency.
AI.conclusion: SoSD provides a spectral framework for understanding and potentially improving pre-training dynamics by explaining the transition to slow learning and the benefits of certain training strategies.

title: ORCA: An End-to-End Interactive Copilot for Optimized Root Cause Analysis
summary: Causal analysis is a crucial task in many domains, including manufacturing, social science, and medicine. However, despite recent progress, the conceptual and methodological complexity of causal methods makes them largely inaccessible to domain experts. This gap prevents experts from leveraging these advances and hinders researchers who lack access to real-world data for validation. To bridge this divide, we introduce ORCA, a copilot for end-to-end causal analysis. ORCA orchestrates agents to understand the user's goals and guide them through the most appropriate causal analysis workflow, from fully automatic to highly user-guided execution. It features causal discovery, causal effect estimation, explainability and Root-Cause-Analysis (RCA). ORCA evaluates and compares performance, generates key metrics and diagrams, and generates insights through structured reports. We highlight its effectiveness across several real-world use-cases.

AI.tldr: ORCA is an AI copilot that helps domain experts perform end-to-end causal analysis through automated workflows, from discovery to reporting.
AI.motivation: Causal analysis methods are powerful but too complex for domain experts, creating a gap that prevents them from leveraging these advances and hinders researchers who lack real-world data for validation.
AI.method: ORCA orchestrates agents to understand user goals and guide them through causal analysis workflows (automatic to user-guided), featuring causal discovery, effect estimation, explainability, and root-cause analysis, with performance evaluation and structured report generation.
AI.result: Effectiveness demonstrated across several real-world use-cases through key metrics, diagrams, and structured insights.
AI.conclusion: ORCA successfully bridges the gap between causal methodology and domain experts by providing an accessible, end-to-end copilot for causal analysis.

title: MSCGC-KAN: Multi-scale Causal Graph Convolution and Kolmogorov-Arnold Feature Mapping for EEG Emotion Recognition
summary: Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based emotion recognition is an important affective computing task, and recent EEG foundation models provide useful generic representations for downstream adaptation. However, under the fine-tuning setting, three limitations remain prominent: insufficient modeling of multi-scale emotional dynamics, inadequate exploitation of inter-channel functional connectivity, and the limited expressive power of simple linear classification heads. To address these issues, this paper proposes a new EEG emotion recognition method, termed MSCGC-KAN, which introduces a structured task head composed of multi-scale causal graph convolution and Kolmogorov--Arnold feature mapping. Built on a pre-trained CBraMod backbone, MSCGC-KAN enhances downstream adaptation by jointly strengthening multi-scale temporal modeling, learnable inter-channel connectivity modeling, and nonlinear discriminative mapping within a compact task-specific head. This design preserves the representation advantage of the foundation model while making the classifier more sensitive to emotion-related spatiotemporal patterns. Extensive experiments are conducted on the public FACED and SEED-VII datasets. The proposed method achieves a balanced accuracy of 60.66\%, a Cohen's Kappa of 0.5525, and a weighted F1-score of 60.40\% on FACED, and obtains 33.27\%, 0.2223, and 33.64\%, respectively, on SEED-VII. Compared with the CBraMod+Linear baseline, the balanced accuracy is improved by 5.91 and 2.03 percentage points on the two datasets, respectively. These results indicate that structured task-head design is an effective way to improve EEG emotion recognition when fine-tuning pre-trained EEG models.

AI.tldr: MSCGC-KAN improves EEG emotion recognition by adding a structured task head (multi-scale causal graph convolution + Kolmogorov-Arnold mapping) to a pre-trained CBraMod backbone, achieving significant accuracy gains over linear fine-tuning baselines on FACED and SEED-VII datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG foundation models under fine-tuning suffer from three limitations: insufficient multi-scale emotional dynamics modeling, inadequate inter-channel functional connectivity exploitation, and limited expressiveness of linear classification heads.
AI.method: Proposes MSCGC-KAN, which augments a pre-trained CBraMod backbone with a structured task head containing multi-scale causal graph convolution (for temporal dynamics and channel connectivity) and Kolmogorov-Arnold feature mapping (for nonlinear discriminative mapping).
AI.result: On FACED: 60.66% balanced accuracy, 0.5525 Cohen's Kappa, 60.40% weighted F1. On SEED-VII: 33.27%, 0.2223, 33.64%. Outperforms CBraMod+Linear baseline by +5.91% and +2.03% balanced accuracy respectively.
AI.conclusion: Structured task-head design is an effective way to improve EEG emotion recognition when fine-tuning pre-trained EEG models, addressing key limitations of linear adaptation.

title: Beyond Pairwise Preferences: Listwise Reward-Aware Alignment for Diffusion Models
summary: Preference optimization has emerged as an efficient alternative to online reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for aligning text-to-image diffusion models. However, existing methods largely reduce supervision to binary pairwise comparisons. This pairwise reduction is limiting when training data naturally contains multiple candidate images for the same prompt, and when continuous reward scores can provide richer information than a single winner-loser label. To address these limitations, we propose Diffusion LAIR, a reward-aware listwise preference optimization method for diffusion models. For each prompt, LAIR converts reward scores across a group of candidate images into centered advantage weights, then optimizes an advantage-weighted regression objective on the implicit reward, defined as the denoising-loss improvement of the current model over a fixed reference model, with a quadratic penalty that regularizes the magnitude of the implicit reward. The resulting objective uses all candidates simultaneously rather than selecting pairs, and remains conservative by explicitly controlling the magnitude of the implicit reward. The LAIR objective admits a bounded closed-form optimum in implicit-reward space, clarifying how the regularization strength controls the magnitude of the preference update. Experiments show that Diffusion LAIR outperforms strong preference optimization baselines on SD1.5 and SDXL across text-to-image generation, compositional generation, and image editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Diffusion LAIR is a reward-aware listwise preference optimization method for diffusion models that uses centered advantage weights and advantage-weighted regression to optimize implicit rewards with quadratic penalty regularization, outperforming baselines on SD1.5 and SDXL across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing preference optimization methods reduce supervision to binary pairwise comparisons, which is limiting when training data contains multiple candidate images for the same prompt and when continuous reward scores provide richer information than a single winner-loser label.
AI.method: LAIR converts reward scores across a group of candidate images into centered advantage weights, then optimizes an advantage-weighted regression objective on the implicit reward (defined as denoising-loss improvement over a reference model) with a quadratic penalty for regularization. The objective uses all candidates simultaneously and admits a bounded closed-form optimum.
AI.result: Diffusion LAIR outperforms strong preference optimization baselines on SD1.5 and SDXL across text-to-image generation, compositional generation, and image editing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Listwise preference optimization with reward-aware advantage weights and implicit reward regularization is an effective and conservative approach for aligning diffusion models, offering improved performance over pairwise reduction methods.

title: Boosting Knowledge Graph Foundation Models via Enhanced Negative Sampling
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) have become the core backbone of numerous downstream tasks such as question answering and recommender systems. However, despite all this, KGs are often very incomplete. To perform zero-shot knowledge graph completion in unseen KGs, which have different relational vocabularies from those used for pre-training, KG foundation models (KGFMs) receive a wide range of attention. Existing KGFMs often perform training using random negative triples, which are constructed by replacing the head or tail entity of a positive triple with a random entity. However, these negative triples are often constructed with limited quality, providing weak supervision for KGFM training. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective adaptive negative sampling approach, KMAS, to enhance existing KGFMs. KMAS constructs hard negative triples through the updated relation embeddings generated from the existing KGFM's relation encoder. To further adaptively align with the evolving capability of the KGFM during the training process, KMAS adjusts the ratio of hard negative triples dynamically throughout the whole training process: after a warmup phrase, it increases the ratio linearly and then decreases linearly. Extensive experiments are conducted over 44 data sets. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed negative sampling method can enhance many SOTA KGFMs without requiring excessive additional time or memory consumption.

AI.tldr: KMAS is an adaptive negative sampling method that enhances KG foundation models for zero-shot knowledge graph completion by constructing hard negative triples using updated relation embeddings and dynamically adjusting their ratio during training.
AI.motivation: Existing KG foundation models rely on random negative triples (replacing entity with random ones) which provide weak supervision due to limited quality. The paper aims to improve negative sampling quality for better KGFM training.
AI.method: KMAS (1) constructs hard negative triples via updated relation embeddings from the KGFM's relation encoder, and (2) dynamically adjusts the ratio of hard negatives during training: linearly increasing after a warmup phase, then linearly decreasing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments over 44 datasets show KMAS enhances many state-of-the-art KGFMs without excessive additional time or memory consumption.
AI.conclusion: Simple adaptive negative sampling can significantly boost KGFM performance for zero-shot KG completion across diverse unseen knowledge graphs.

title: DelowlightSplat: Feed-Forward Gaussian Splatting for Lowlight 3D Scene Reconstruction
summary: Novel-view synthesis and 3D reconstruction from sparse posed images are central to robotics and AR/VR. Yet, feed-forward 3D Gaussian reconstruction fails under lowlight due to noise, color shifts, and unreliable correspondence. We propose DelowlightSplat, a lowlight-aware feed-forward Gaussian splatting framework for clean novel-view rendering. We build a controllable multi-view lowlight benchmark by degrading only context views while keeping target views clean. We introduce a lightweight Lowlight Adapter for residual enhancement to improve matchability, and couple it with cost-volume-based multi-view inference to directly predict clean 3D Gaussians. Experiments show that DelowlightSplat significantly outperforms previous feed-forward method and two-stage pipeline under lowlight conditions.

AI.tldr: DelowlightSplat is a feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting framework designed for clean novel-view synthesis from sparse posed images captured under low-light conditions, outperforming prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing feed-forward 3D Gaussian reconstruction methods fail under low-light conditions due to noise, color shifts, and unreliable correspondence, which limits their applicability in robotics and AR/VR.
AI.method: The authors create a controllable multi-view lowlight benchmark (degrading context views, keeping target views clean) and propose a lightweight Lowlight Adapter for residual enhancement to improve matchability, coupled with cost-volume-based multi-view inference to directly predict clean 3D Gaussians.
AI.result: DelowlightSplat significantly outperforms previous feed-forward methods and two-stage pipelines under low-light conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lowlight-aware framework effectively addresses the challenges of novel-view synthesis from sparse posed images in low-light environments.

title: Dense2MoE: Pushing the Pareto Frontier of On-Device LLMs via Unified Pruning and Upcycling
summary: The Mixture of Experts MoE architecture is highly promising for resource constrained on device deployments yet training these models from scratch incurs prohibitive costs Current methods attempt to alleviate this by upcycling dense models into MoEs however they often introduce parameter redundancy that degrades inference efficiency Alternatively standard layer pruning mitigates redundancy but inevitably compromises model accuracy To resolve this dilemma we propose Dense2MoE a novel framework that unifies pruning and upcycling through Layer Fusion UpCycling LF UC Guided by hardware Roofline theory Dense2MoE systematically overcomes the inference memory wall by pruning bandwidth heavy attention modules from redundant layers while repurposing their Multi Layer Perceptrons MLPs into MoE experts This structural innovation preserves the models core capabilities and strictly limits active parameters via selective token routing With a modest continual pre training budget Dense2MoE efficiently converts publicly available dense LLMs into on device ready MoE models Extensive experiments demonstrate that Dense2MoE significantly advances the Pareto frontier for on device inference latency versus model accuracy outperforming dense baselines state of the art compression and standard upcycling methods

AI.tldr: Dense2MoE proposes a framework that combines pruning and upcycling to convert dense LLMs into efficient MoE models for on-device deployment by fusing attention modules and repurposing MLPs as experts, outperforming existing methods in latency-accuracy tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: MoE architecture is promising for on-device deployment but training from scratch is costly, and existing upcycling methods introduce parameter redundancy while pruning degrades accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes Layer Fusion UpCycling (LF-UC) guided by Roofline theory: prune bandwidth-heavy attention modules from redundant layers and repurpose their MLPs as MoE experts, using selective token routing to limit active parameters.
AI.result: Dense2MoE significantly advances the Pareto frontier for on-device inference latency vs. model accuracy, outperforming dense baselines, SOTA compression methods, and standard upcycling approaches.
AI.conclusion: With modest continual pre-training, Dense2MoE efficiently converts publicly available dense LLMs into on-device-ready MoE models without prohibitive costs.

title: BatteryMFormer: Multi-level Learning for Battery Degradation Trajectory Forecasting
summary: Early battery degradation trajectory forecasting (BDTF), which predicts the full-life state-of-health trajectory from early operational data, is critical for battery optimization, manufacturing, and deployment. Battery degradation data exhibit two key characteristics. First, degradation data present a multi-level structure, including regularities shared within aging conditions and trajectory patterns shared across batteries. Second, degradation-related variations in voltage-current profiles are often localized to specific state-of-charge (SOC) intervals. Existing approaches often fail to explicitly model these characteristics. To bridge this gap, we propose BatteryMFormer, a multi-level Transformer for early BDTF. BatteryMFormer integrates (1) an aging-condition-aware decoder that injects aging-condition priors via aging-condition-informed queries and aging-condition-aware attention, (2) a meta degradation pattern memory that learns and retrieves trajectory prototypes to guide long-horizon forecasting, and (3) a dual-view encoder that jointly captures temporal dynamics and SOC-localized variations from voltage and current time series. Extensive experiments on four battery domains show that BatteryMFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, marking a significant step toward reliable BDTF. Our code is available at https://github.com/Ruifeng-Tan/BatteryMFormer.

AI.tldr: BatteryMFormer is a multi-level Transformer for early battery degradation trajectory forecasting that explicitly models multi-level degradation structures and SOC-localized variations, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines across four battery domains.
AI.motivation: Existing BDTF methods fail to explicitly model two key characteristics of battery degradation data: (1) the multi-level structure (shared regularities within aging conditions and trajectory patterns across batteries), and (2) degradation-related variations localized to specific SOC intervals in voltage-current profiles.
AI.method: BatteryMFormer integrates three key components: (1) an aging-condition-aware decoder with aging-condition-informed queries and attention, (2) a meta degradation pattern memory for learning and retrieving trajectory prototypes, and (3) a dual-view encoder capturing both temporal dynamics and SOC-localized variations from voltage/current time series.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four battery domains show that BatteryMFormer consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines for early BDTF.
AI.conclusion: BatteryMFormer marks a significant step toward reliable early battery degradation trajectory forecasting by explicitly modeling multi-level structures and SOC-localized variations.

title: Attenuation-Resilient Alternating Optimization for Laparoscopic Liver Landmark Detection
comment: This paper has been accepted by MICCAI 2026
summary: Liver surface landmark detection is a fundamental prerequisite for anatomical guidance in laparoscopic liver surgery. However, it remains unreliable in practice due to two pervasive challenges: illumination attenuation in underexposed regions and the structural mismatch between pixel-wise localization and continuous curvilinear geometry. To address these limitations, we propose A2ONet, an attenuation-resilient alternating optimization network for robust liver landmark detection. To mitigate illumination attenuation, A2ONet embraces an illumination field compensation (IFC) block that adaptively enhances dark regions while preserving structural consistency. Meanwhile, we introduce a lightweight frequency-orientation selective filter (FOSF) to suppress repetitive texture interference and preserve salient curvilinear cues. Building upon these resilient representations, we design an alternating seg-curve optimization (ASCO) decoder that iteratively couples dense segmentation with explicit curve modeling, enabling mutual guidance to optimize both structural continuity and endpoint localization. Extensive evaluations on L3D-2K, L3D, and P2ILF demonstrate consistent improvements over competitive methods, establishing a more reliable foundation for intraoperative anatomy guidance. Our code will be available at https://github.com/hyperiondk115/A2ONet.

AI.tldr: A2ONet is a deep learning framework for robust liver surface landmark detection that handles illumination attenuation and structural mismatches through an illumination field compensation block, frequency-orientation selective filter, and alternating seg-curve optimization decoder.
AI.motivation: Liver landmark detection is unreliable in laparoscopic surgery due to illumination attenuation in underexposed regions and structural mismatch between pixel-wise localization and continuous curvilinear geometry.
AI.method: A2ONet uses three key components: (1) Illumination Field Compensation (IFC) block to enhance dark regions, (2) Frequency-Orientation Selective Filter (FOSF) to suppress texture interference, and (3) Alternating Seg-Curve Optimization (ASCO) decoder that iteratively couples segmentation with curve modeling.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over competitive methods on L3D-2K, L3D, and P2ILF benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A2ONet establishes a more reliable foundation for intraoperative anatomy guidance in laparoscopic liver surgery.

title: PRISM: Position-encoded Regressive Inverse Spectral Model for Multilayer Thin-Film Design
summary: The inverse problem of multilayer thin-film optical coatings design represents a complex combinatorial-continuous optimization challenge. We present PRISM (Position-encoded Regressive Inverse Spectral Model), a unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer that streamlines this process by jointly predicting discrete material selection and continuous thickness regression within a single backbone. PRISM introduces two primary architectural innovations: (1) spectrum prefix conditioning, which utilizes standard prefix tokens for in-context target injection, and (2) cumulative-depth Rotary Position Embeddings, which encode continuous thickness directly into the positional representation to preserve the physical spatial relationships of the stack. Our benchmarks demonstrate that a PRISM-13M model reduces MAE by over 50\% compared to other transformer baselines while utilizing only one-fifth of the parameters. Furthermore, a 44M-parameter variant achieves state-of-the-art performance (MAE = 0.010) on our in-distribution validation benchmark and operates significantly faster than simulated annealing, offering a highly efficient alternative to classical optimization methods.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a decoder-only autoregressive transformer that jointly predicts discrete material selection and continuous thickness regression for multilayer thin-film optical coating design, achieving over 50% better MAE with far fewer parameters than existing transformer baselines.
AI.motivation: The multilayer thin-film optical coatings design problem is a complex combinatorial-continuous optimization challenge, and classical methods like simulated annealing are slow and inefficient.
AI.method: PRISM introduces (1) spectrum prefix conditioning for in-context target injection, and (2) cumulative-depth Rotary Position Embeddings to encode continuous thickness into positional representations, using a unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer backbone.
AI.result: PRISM-13M reduces MAE by over 50% compared to transformer baselines with only one-fifth of the parameters; PRISM-44M achieves state-of-the-art MAE=0.010 on in-distribution validation, operating significantly faster than simulated annealing.
AI.conclusion: PRISM offers a highly efficient alternative to classical optimization methods for thin-film optical coating design.
