However, a critical dilemma inherent in this training paradigm is the imbalanced reward polarization along different rubric dimensions. Under this bottleneck, even if LLMs achieve relatively high rewards after training, they may still exhibit severe deficiencies in certain dimensions, leading to a direct deterioration in user experience. To address this problem, we propose Focal Reward, a novel objective to automatically balance the training of reinforcement learning under rubric-based rewards. Specifically, we first leverage an inverse reward projection mechanism to estimate the saturation degree of each criterion in the rubric, which forms the basis to calibrate the reward direction. Then, the final objective is designed with an automatically reweighting coefficient for each criterion to achieve the fine-grained balancing. Extensive experiments across three model scales and six benchmarks demonstrate that our Focal Reward method outperforms the strongest static aggregation baseline in all 18 model-benchmark comparisons. Rollout, mechanism, and ablation analyses further show that these gains arise from online, saturation-aware reallocation toward rubrics that still have room for improvement.

AI.tldr: Focal Reward is a novel training objective that addresses imbalanced reward polarization across rubric dimensions in LLM reinforcement learning by using inverse reward projection to estimate saturation and automatically reweight criteria, outperforming static baselines across all 18 model-benchmark comparisons.
AI.motivation: The critical dilemma is that multi-dimensional rubric-based RL training suffers from imbalanced reward polarization, where LLMs achieve high overall rewards but exhibit severe deficiencies in certain dimensions, deteriorating user experience.
AI.method: (1) Inverse reward projection mechanism to estimate saturation degree of each rubric criterion; (2) Automatically reweighting coefficients for fine-grained balancing; (3) Online, saturation-aware reallocation toward underperforming rubrics.
AI.result: Outperforms strongest static aggregation baseline in all 18 model-benchmark comparisons across three model scales and six benchmarks. Rollout, mechanism, and ablation analyses confirm gains come from online, saturation-aware reallocation.
AI.conclusion: Focal Reward effectively balances multi-dimensional rubric rewards in LLM RL training, solving the polarization problem and improving user experience by dynamically focusing on rubrics with room for improvement.

title: The Compressive Knowledge Graph Hypothesis: Which Graph Facts Matter for Scientific Hypothesis Generation?
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) can provide structured scientific context to language models, but it remains unclear which graph facts actually shape the generated hypotheses. We study KG-guided hypothesis generation for battery materials across Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash. We perturb local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure, and evaluate outputs with both provided-graph and fixed-reference metrics. Across models, KG utility is selective and model-dependent: graph context changes outputs, but no-KG outputs also recover substantial graph content from model priors. Compact top-k subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, including when claimed-outcome triples are held out. At the same time, compression is not unique to one semantic ranking rule, random and topology-based subsets can also recover much of the signal. These results support a redundancy-aware Compressive KG hypothesis: useful KG signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how knowledge graphs (KGs) influence hypothesis generation in language models for battery materials. The authors systematically perturb KGs and find that graph utility is selective and model-dependent; compact subgraphs often approximate full-KG behavior, supporting a "Compressive KG hypothesis" where useful signal is recoverable from smaller, structured subgraphs rather than full graphs.
AI.motivation: To understand which specific graph facts in knowledge graphs actually shape the hypotheses generated by language models, as it remains unclear whether full KGs are necessary or if compact subsets suffice.
AI.method: Systematically perturb local KGs by varying density, ontology richness, topology, and control structure. Evaluate outputs from Mistral-7B, Llama-3.1-70B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash using both provided-graph and fixed-reference metrics. Compare full-KG, no-KG, compact top-k subgraphs, random subsets, and topology-based subsets.
AI.result: KG utility is selective and model-dependent; no-KG outputs recover substantial graph content from model priors; compact top-k subgraphs approximate full-KG behavior (even with held-out triples); random and topology-based subsets also recover much signal; compression is not unique to one semantic ranking rule.
AI.conclusion: The findings support a redundancy-aware Compressive KG hypothesis: useful KG signal is often recoverable from compact, scientifically structured subgraphs rather than requiring the full local graph.

title: Joint 2D-3D Segmentation and Association in Street-level Imaging
comment: 15 pages, 6 image figures, 1 in-body table, 1 in-body algorithm, 2 indexes with tables
summary: Accurate interpretation of street-level imagery is essential for large-scale urban mapping and the creation of Spatial Digital Twin (SDT) environments. This work presents a unified framework for joint 2D-3D segmentation and association that integrates visual semantics with multi-view geometric reasoning. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on sequential frames for temporal tracking, our method leverages zero-shot detection and segmentation together with structure-from-motion reconstruction to establish stable cross-view correspondences. A 3D-driven association mechanism replaces traditional 2D multi-object tracking, using geometric consistency to guide identity preservation across wide-baseline viewpoints and varying imaging conditions. By combining 2D texture cues with global 3D context, the proposed pipeline is well-suited for scalable street-level processing and can be used for a variety of object types. Experiments demonstrate substantially improved coverage of ground-truth sequences and more robust identity retention compared to state-of-the-art 2D-only tracking methods, achieving a 22% performance gain in challenging urban scenarios.

AI.tldr: A unified 2D-3D segmentation and association framework for street-level imagery that uses zero-shot detection, structure-from-motion, and 3D-driven association to outperform 2D-only tracking by 22% in urban scenarios.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate interpretation of street-level imagery for large-scale urban mapping and Spatial Digital Twin creation, overcoming limitations of traditional 2D tracking methods that rely on sequential frames.
AI.method: Combines zero-shot detection/segmentation with structure-from-motion reconstruction and a 3D-driven association mechanism (replacing 2D multi-object tracking) using geometric consistency for cross-view identity preservation.
AI.result: Achieves 22% performance gain over state-of-the-art 2D-only tracking methods in challenging urban scenarios, with improved coverage and identity retention.
AI.conclusion: The proposed 2D-3D unified framework is well-suited for scalable street-level processing and robust identity preservation across varied viewpoints and conditions.

title: On the Error-Correcting Effects of Stochasticity in Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models achieve strong performance in text and image generation, but their inference remains slow and must inherently balance sampling efficiency and sample quality. In this work, we present a systematic study of how the \emph{degree of stochasticity} in Markov transitions governs the sampling tradeoff. We show that highly deterministic transitions converge rapidly but suffer from error accumulation, while more stochastic transitions converge more slowly yet can achieve higher final sample quality. Using an information-theoretic analysis, we identify the underlying mechanism as an error-correcting effect induced by \emph{redundant transitions} that symmetrically exchange mass between states, and show that these transitions can provably contract sampling errors. Motivated by this analysis, we propose \emph{Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling} (DCRS), a novel inference algorithm that injects controlled stochasticity by alternating between forward and reverse diffusion processes. Experiments on synthetic datasets and large-scale benchmarks show that DCRS improves the speed-quality tradeoff in the low number of function evaluations regime. On image datasets, DCRS achieves up to a $10\times$ reduction in sampling steps compared to standard samplers while maintaining competitive sample quality, whereas on language benchmarks, we observe more nuanced behavior depending on the corruption process and sampling procedure.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how stochasticity in discrete diffusion models affects the speed-quality tradeoff during sampling, identifying that redundant transitions enable error correction. The authors propose DCRS, a method that alternates forward and reverse diffusion to inject controlled stochasticity, achieving up to 10× reduction in sampling steps on image tasks.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion models suffer from slow inference and must balance sampling speed with output quality. The authors aim to systematically understand how the degree of stochasticity in Markov transitions governs this tradeoff, and to develop a method that improves it.
AI.method: The authors first analyze highly deterministic vs. stochastic transitions theoretically, using information-theoretic analysis to identify that redundant transitions (symmetric mass exchange) create an error-correcting effect. They then propose Discrete Churn and Restart Sampling (DCRS), which injects controlled stochasticity by alternating between forward and reverse diffusion processes during inference.
AI.result: On image datasets, DCRS achieves up to 10× reduction in sampling steps while maintaining competitive sample quality compared to standard samplers. On language benchmarks, the behavior is more nuanced, depending on the corruption process and sampling procedure.
AI.conclusion: Controlled stochasticity via churn and restart improves the speed-quality tradeoff in discrete diffusion models, especially in low-function-evaluation regimes. The error-correcting effect from redundant transitions is the key theoretical mechanism.

title: Learning to Act under Noise: Enhancing Agent Robustness via Noisy Environments
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have facilitated the widespread deployment of LLMs as interactive agents capable of reasoning, planning, and tool use. Despite strong performance on existing benchmarks, such agents often exhibit notable degradation when deployed in real-world settings, where environments are inherently stochastic and imperfect. We argue that this discrepancy arises from a fundamental mismatch between idealized training settings and real-world interaction dynamics, where current paradigms rely on carefully curated task instructions and stable, well-controlled environments. To address this gap, we propose NoisyAgent, an agentic training framework that explicitly incorporates environmental imperfections into the agent learning process. We identify two major sources of interaction noise in real-world scenarios: user noise, which captures ambiguity and variability in user interaction, and tool noise, which reflects failures and anomalies in tool execution. We introduce such perturbations into the training pipeline by modifying user interaction patterns and simulating tool execution results within the training environment. To stabilize training while encouraging agents to handle increasingly challenging imperfections, noise is applied to only a subset of rollouts and progressively increased in difficulty as the model adapts to the current noise level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently improves agent robustness under noisy and dynamic environments. Our analysis reveals that training under noise conditions also yields performance gains on idealized benchmarks, suggesting that controlled exposure to environmental noise promotes more generalizable reasoning and decision-making behaviors. Our findings highlight the importance of modeling interaction imperfections for bridging the gap between agent training and real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: NoisyAgent introduces environmental noise (user and tool imperfections) into LLM agent training to improve robustness in real-world stochastic settings, showing performance gains on both noisy and idealized benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents perform well on clean benchmarks but degrade in real-world settings due to a mismatch between idealized training environments and real-world stochastic, imperfect interaction dynamics.
AI.method: NoisyAgent incorporates two types of interaction noise—user noise (ambiguity/variability) and tool noise (execution failures/anomalies)—into training by modifying user patterns and simulating tool results, with noise applied to a subset of rollouts and progressively increased in difficulty.
AI.result: The approach consistently improves agent robustness under noisy/dynamic environments, and training with noise also yields performance gains on idealized benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Controlled exposure to environmental noise promotes more generalizable reasoning and decision-making, highlighting the importance of modeling interaction imperfections to bridge the gap between agent training and real-world deployment.

title: Learning Reference-Guided Exposure Correction with Hybrid Illumination Characteristics
summary: We present HICNet, a reference-guided exposure correction framework. A lightweight, content-agnostic encoder distills each image into a compact illumination embedding capturing regional brightness, edge contrast, and higher-order luminance moments. The embedding difference between a source and its reference drives a multi-scale modulation network that combines FiLM-based global adjustment with Photometric Channel Rebalancing for fine-grained, illumination-aware spectral gating, producing exposure-matched outputs while faithfully preserving scene details. A cross-batch contrastive loss orders the illumination manifold, bolstering robustness to diverse lighting conditions. Trained without ground truth or intrinsic decomposition, HICNet attains better accuracy on public benchmarks and generalizes well to entirely unseen scenes.

AI.tldr: HICNet is a reference-guided exposure correction framework that uses a lightweight encoder to extract illumination embeddings, then applies FiLM-based global adjustment and Photometric Channel Rebalancing to match source exposure to a reference image, achieving state-of-the-art results without ground truth data.
AI.motivation: To correct exposure issues in images without requiring ground truth or intrinsic decomposition, using only a reference image to guide the adjustment.
AI.method: A lightweight content-agnostic encoder extracts illumination embeddings (capturing brightness, edge contrast, luminance moments). The embedding difference between source and reference drives a multi-scale modulation network combining FiLM-based global adjustment with Photometric Channel Rebalancing for spectral gating. A cross-batch contrastive loss orders the illumination manifold.
AI.result: Achieves better accuracy on public benchmarks and generalizes well to unseen scenes, trained without ground truth or intrinsic decomposition.
AI.conclusion: HICNet provides an effective reference-guided approach to exposure correction that outperforms existing methods and generalizes robustly across varying lighting conditions.

title: Near-Optimal Regret in Adversarial Kernel Bandits
summary: We study the adversarial kernel bandit problem, in which the loss at each round is induced by an arbitrary bounded element of a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS). We propose an exponential-weights algorithm built on a regularized importance-weighted loss estimator, together with an explicit correction term that cancels the bias introduced by the regularization. Our main result bounds the regret by $\widetilde{O}\big(\sqrt{T\, d_*(λ)\,\log|{X}|}\big)$, where $d_*(λ)$ is a widely-adopted notion of effective dimension that captures the complexity of the kernel. Up to logarithmic factors, this matches the known rate achieved in the related stochastic kernel bandit problem. A notable application is the Matérn$(ν,d)$ kernel with smoothness parameter $ν$ on $\mathbb{R}^d$, for which our bound specializes to $\widetilde{O}\big(T^{(ν+d)/(2ν+d)}\big)$, improving over the best-known prior rate of Chatterji et al. [2019] while simultaneously removing the rank-one adversary assumption required by their analysis. Moreover, this rate is the same as the known optimal rate for stochastic kernel bandits, and also matches a lower bound from concurrent work up to a $\log T$ factor.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an exponential-weights algorithm for adversarial kernel bandits with a regularized importance-weighted loss estimator and correction term, achieving regret bounds matching stochastic kernel bandit rates and improving over prior work for Matérn kernels.
AI.motivation: To solve the adversarial kernel bandit problem where losses are arbitrary bounded elements of an RKHS, and to close the gap between stochastic and adversarial settings in kernel bandits.
AI.method: An exponential-weights algorithm with a regularized importance-weighted loss estimator plus an explicit correction term to cancel regularization bias.
AI.result: Regret bound of $\widetilde{O}\big(\sqrt{T\, d_*(λ)\,\log|{X}|}\big)$, matching stochastic kernel bandit rates. For Matérn(ν,d) kernel, achieves $\widetilde{O}\big(T^{(ν+d)/(2ν+d)}\big)$, improving over Chatterji et al. [2019] and matching optimal stochastic rates.
AI.conclusion: The adversarial kernel bandit problem can be solved with rates matching the stochastic setting, removing restrictive assumptions like rank-one adversaries.

title: Gumbel Machine: Counterfactual Student Writing Generation via Gumbel Noise Steering
summary: An effective method of teaching across disciplines is to provide examples of high-quality work. However, an example may be significantly different from a student's current work, making it challenging for them to emulate. An ideal learning demonstration is a counterfactual version of the student work, an improved version that is still similar to their own. Existing automated approaches for counterfactual text generation using Large Language Models (LLMs) result in domain-specific systems that are difficult to translate into practical applications. We present the Gumbel Machine, a flexible, modular approach to generating counterfactuals that leverages LLM instruction-following capabilities while encouraging similarity to a reference factual text. Central to our approach is a novel, controlled decoding algorithm, $β$-Hindsight control, which uses latent randomness as a tunable similarity control mechanism during counterfactual generation. Experiments on datasets of student writing, scored on various criteria, demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach at generating counterfactuals both rubric-consistent and similar to a reference.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Gumbel Machine, a modular system using LLMs and a novel controlled decoding algorithm (β-Hindsight control) to generate counterfactual versions of student writing that are both improved and similar to the original, for use as personalized learning examples across disciplines.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for teaching across disciplines using examples of high-quality work are limited because provided examples often differ significantly from students' current work, making them hard to emulate. Automated counterfactual text generation approaches using LLMs are domain-specific and difficult to translate into practical applications.
AI.method: The Gumbel Machine is a flexible, modular approach that leverages LLM instruction-following capabilities with a novel controlled decoding algorithm called β-Hindsight control, which uses latent randomness as a tunable similarity control mechanism during counterfactual generation.
AI.result: Experiments on datasets of student writing, scored on various criteria, demonstrate that the approach generates counterfactuals that are both rubric-consistent and similar to a reference text.
AI.conclusion: The Gumbel Machine approach is effective for generating personalized counterfactual examples that are improved yet similar to original student work, offering a practical and transferable solution for educational applications.

title: CIRCLED: A Multi-turn CIR Dataset with Consistent Dialogues across Domains
summary: Existing Multi-Turn Composed Image Retrieval (MTCIR) datasets lack dialogue-history consistency and are restricted to the fashion domain. To address these limitations, we construct CIRCLED by extending FashionIQ, CIRR, and CIRCO. In CIRCLED, the query at each turn progressively approaches the target image. Data are generated via a CIReVL-based retrieval pipeline and curated with multiple filters on retrieval success, turn length, consistency, and information redundancy to ensure quality. In total, we collect 22,608 multi-turn sessions across nine subsets, substantially exceeding Multi-turn FashionIQ (11,505 sessions) in both scale and generality. We further apply multiple baseline methods and quantitatively assess retrieval accuracy on CIRCLED. Our work provides a practical, high-quality benchmark to facilitate future research on multi-turn CIR. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/tk1441/CIRCLED and https://github.com/mti-lab/circled.

AI.tldr: CIRCLED is a large, multi-domain benchmark dataset for Multi-Turn Composed Image Retrieval (MTCIR), constructed by extending FashionIQ, CIRR, and CIRCO with 22,608 high-quality multi-turn sessions, overcoming limitations of existing datasets in scale, domain diversity, and dialogue-history consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing MTCIR datasets lack dialogue-history consistency and are restricted to the fashion domain only, limiting generalization and practical applicability.
AI.method: Extended three existing datasets (FashionIQ, CIRR, CIRCO) using a CIReVL-based retrieval pipeline; applied multiple filters on retrieval success, turn length, consistency, and information redundancy to ensure quality.
AI.result: Collected 22,608 multi-turn sessions across nine subsets, substantially exceeding Multi-turn FashionIQ (11,505 sessions) in scale and generality; evaluated multiple baseline methods on the benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, high-quality benchmark to facilitate future research on multi-turn composed image retrieval.

title: Few-shot Cross-country Generalization of Tabular Machine Learning and Foundation Models for Childhood Anemia Prediction under Distribution Shift
summary:
Childhood anemia affects around 40% of children aged 6-59 months globally and arises from heterogeneous factors, limiting model generalizability. We evaluate a transformer-based tabular foundation model against classical supervised methods under cross-country and data-scarce settings.
  We used DHS data from 16 countries across Africa, Asia, Latin America, the Caucasus, and the Middle East (n=68,856). We compared Logistic Regression, XGBoost, LightGBM, and TabPFN v2.6. Performance was assessed using AUC-ROC, Brier score, and ECE. Generalization was evaluated using leave-one-country-out (LOCO), reverse-LOCO, and few-shot settings. Subgroup analyses included sex, age, residence, maternal education, and wealth. Feature importance was estimated using SHAP.
  TabPFN outperformed classical models in low-data regimes (<200 samples), showing higher discrimination and better calibration. Across countries, it achieved the lowest Brier score (0.042) and ECE (0.203). Under full-data settings, AUC-ROC ranged from 0.59-0.76 with small between-model differences ($\leq 0.05$). LOCO performance was stable (0.58-0.69), driven by country context. Reverse-LOCO showed asymmetric transferability. Subgroup performance was consistent with no systematic demographic bias. SHAP identified child age, altitude, and height-for-age z-score as dominant predictors, followed by wealth and maternal education.
  Performance in childhood anemia prediction is driven more by population variation than model choice. TabPFN provides advantages in low-resource settings through improved discrimination and calibration, highlighting foundation models as promising tools for data-scarce global health prediction.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates TabPFN, a transformer-based foundation model, against classical ML methods for childhood anemia prediction across 16 countries. TabPFN outperforms in low-data settings (<200 samples) with better calibration and discrimination, though overall performance is more influenced by population variation than model choice.
AI.motivation: Childhood anemia affects ~40% of children globally and stems from heterogeneous factors, which limits model generalizability across different populations and data-scarce settings.
AI.method: Used DHS data from 16 countries (n=68,856). Compared Logistic Regression, XGBoost, LightGBM, and TabPFN v2.6. Evaluated using AUC-ROC, Brier score, ECE. Tested generalization via LOCO, reverse-LOCO, and few-shot settings. Conducted subgroup and SHAP analyses.
AI.result: TabPFN outperformed classical models in low-data regimes (<200 samples). Achieved lowest Brier (0.042) and ECE (0.203). Full-data AUC ranged 0.59-0.76 with small between-model differences (≤0.05). LOCO performance stable (0.58-0.69). Key predictors: child age, altitude, height-for-age z-score, wealth, maternal education.
AI.conclusion: Performance in childhood anemia prediction is driven more by population variation than model choice. TabPFN shows advantages in low-resource settings, highlighting foundation models as promising tools for data-scarce global health prediction.

title: SIA: Self Improving AI with Harness & Weight Updates
summary: Humans are the bottleneck in building and improving AI. Both the models and the agents that wrap them are written, tuned, and corrected by people. The long-horizon goal of an AI that can figure out how to improve itself remains open. Two largely disjoint research lines attack this bottleneck. The harness-update school has a meta-agent rewrite the scaffold of a task-specific agent (its tools, prompts, retry logic, and search procedure) while the model weights are held fixed. The test-time training school uses hand-written RL pipelines to update the model's own weights on task feedback while the harness is held fixed. These two silos operate in isolation. We propose SIA, a self-improving loop in which a language-model agent (the Feedback-Agent) updates both the harness and the weights of a task-specific agent. We evaluate across three contrasting domains: Chinese legal charge classification, low-level GPU kernel optimisation, and single-cell RNA denoising. Combining both levers outperforms scaffold iteration alone on all three benchmarks. The gains are 56.6% on LawBench, 91.9% runtime reduction on GPU kernels, and 502% on denoising over the initial baseline. Harness updates make the model agentic, shaping how it searches and acts, while weight updates build the domain intuition that no prompt or scaffold can instil.

AI.tldr: SIA is a self-improving framework where a language-model agent updates both the harness (tools, prompts, search) and weights of a task-specific agent, outperforming scaffold-only and weight-only methods across legal classification, GPU kernel optimization, and RNA denoising benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Humans are the bottleneck in building AI, as models and agents are manually written, tuned, and corrected. Existing approaches (harness-update and test-time training) operate in isolation, each only addressing half the problem.
AI.method: SIA introduces a Feedback-Agent (a language model) that iteratively updates both the harness (prompts, tools, retry logic, search) and the model weights of a task-specific agent, combining both levers in a unified self-improving loop.
AI.result: On three benchmarks: 56.6% improvement on LawBench, 91.9% runtime reduction on GPU kernels, and 502% improvement on single-cell RNA denoising over the initial baseline. Combining both levers outperforms scaffold-only updates across all domains.
AI.conclusion: Harness updates enable agentic behavior (shaping search and action), while weight updates build domain intuition that cannot be captured by prompts or scaffolds. The combined approach is superior to either method alone.

title: Self-Intersection-Aware 3D Human Motion Generation Using an Efficient Human Sphere Proxy
summary: Human motion generation has made tremendous progress in recent years, with state-of-the-art approaches surpassing ground truth data in leading evaluation benchmarks. However, visual inspection of the generated motions paints a different picture. Even state-of-the-art approaches generate motions frequently containing self-intersections, i.e., body parts interpenetrating, which are strong artifacts, severely limiting the perceived motion quality. We introduce a novel loss, which explicitly penalizes self-intersections, to the training of human motion generation methods. We base our loss on a sphere proxy of human geometry, which allows us to calculate a self-intersection loss 98% faster and uses 83% less memory than comparable methods based on triangular meshes. The loss is agnostic to the specific approach, and we add it to the training of the recent human motion generation methods human motion diffusion model (MDM) and MoMask. Our extensive experiments show a reduction of self-intersections in generated motions of up to 49% while improving other evaluation metrics. The code is available at https://github.com/boschresearch/humansphereproxy .

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel self-intersection loss based on sphere proxies of human geometry to reduce body part interpenetration in generated human motions. The loss is 98% faster and uses 83% less memory than mesh-based methods, reducing self-intersections by up to 49% while improving other metrics.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art human motion generation methods produce motions that score well on benchmarks but frequently contain self-intersections (body parts interpenetrating), which are strong artifacts that severely limit perceived motion quality.
AI.method: The authors propose a self-intersection loss based on a sphere proxy of human geometry (instead of triangular meshes), making it 98% faster and 83% more memory-efficient. This loss is agnostic to the specific motion generation method and is added to training of MDM and MoMask.
AI.result: Reduction of self-intersections by up to 49% while also improving other evaluation metrics, demonstrating effectiveness across multiple state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed sphere proxy-based self-intersection loss effectively reduces artifacts in generated human motions, improves perceived quality, and can be easily integrated into existing motion generation pipelines.

title: Geometry-Aware Contrastive Learning for Few-Shot Automatic Modulation Recognition
summary: Standard Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) for Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR) struggles with ineffective isotropic augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift. To address these challenges, we propose Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL), a geometry-aware framework that couples Virtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) with a semantic consistency loss. We provide a theoretical analysis indicating that this strategy acts as an implicit spectral regularizer for the encoder, enabling stable manifold exploration. Complementing this, our Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attention improves structural stability by constraining attention locality, while a Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module anchors representations with physical priors. Experiments on RML benchmarks show that DyCo-CL achieves a 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings over prior methods.

AI.tldr: DyCo-CL introduces a geometry-aware contrastive learning framework with adversarial augmentations, spectral regularization, and a signal-adaptive Swin backbone to improve few-shot automatic modulation recognition, achieving 6.27% accuracy gain over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Standard SSL for AMR suffers from ineffective isotropic augmentations, spectral instability, and semantic drift, limiting performance in few-shot settings.
AI.method: 1) Dynamic-Consistency Contrastive Learning (DyCo-CL) with Virtual Adversarial Augmentation (VAA) and semantic consistency loss serving as implicit spectral regularizer; 2) Signal-Adaptive Swin Backbone with fixed-window attention for structural stability; 3) Hybrid Knowledge Fusion module incorporating physical priors.
AI.result: 6.27% accuracy gain in 1-shot settings on RML benchmarks compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware framework effectively addresses key SSL limitations for AMR, with theoretical and empirical evidence supporting its advantages.

title: Modeling Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax: A Standalone Framework for Measurement, Simulation, and Dashboarding
summary: Agentic AI systems combine probabilistic reasoning with delegated action through tools, context, memory, orchestration, and external workflow integration. This note develops a formal and managerially usable model that distinguishes Agentic Technical Debt from Stochastic Tax. Agentic Technical Debt is a stock of accumulated design and governance liability. Stochastic Tax is a recurring flow of operating burden that arises when stochastic agents are used in business workflows. The two constructs are related, but they are not the same: debt can amplify the tax, while the tax can remain positive even when debt is minimized. The note starts from a compact dashboard expression, expands it into a fuller structural model, defines all variables and parameters, shows how each cost category can be estimated from operational data, and illustrates the framework with an accounts-payable simulation and companion spreadsheet.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a formal model distinguishing Agentic Technical Debt (accumulated design/governance liability) from Stochastic Tax (recurring operating burden from stochastic agents), providing a dashboard expression, structural model, estimation methods, and an accounts-payable simulation.
AI.motivation: To create a clear, managerially usable framework that separates two distinct but related cost concepts in agentic AI systems—design/governance debt versus operational stochastic burden—enabling better cost analysis and governance decisions.
AI.method: The authors start with a compact dashboard expression, expand into a full structural model with defined variables and parameters, show cost estimation from operational data, and illustrate via an accounts-payable simulation with a companion spreadsheet.
AI.result: A formal distinction showing that Agentic Technical Debt (stock) and Stochastic Tax (flow) are related but not identical: debt amplifies tax, yet tax remains positive even when debt is minimized.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI systems incur two distinct cost types requiring separate management strategies; minimizing technical debt alone does not eliminate stochastic operational burden.

title: Once-For-All: A Train-Once and Select-Anytime Framework for Multimodal Instruction Tuning
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Mingkang Dong and Hongyi Cai contributed equally to this work. Muxin Pu is the corresponding author
summary: Multimodal instruction tuning is the de facto recipe for adapting vision language models (VLMs), yet instruction data are highly redundant, making data selection critical for training efficiency. Existing methods derive selection signals from a specific model or dataset, so whenever the target model or candidate pool changes, the criteria must be recomputed from scratch at substantial cost. To address this, we propose OFA, a data selection framework that trains a reusable selector once and applies it to any dataset or model without recomputation. OFA clusters multimodal instructions in a frozen CLIP space, derives pseudo labels from the cluster structure, and trains a lightweight selector for only a few epochs; samples on which this selector is least confident are selected as the most informative. Once trained, the frozen selector transfers directly across datasets and model scales. The selector is trained once on LLaVA-665K and applied both to LLaVA-665K itself and, without any retraining, to the unseen Vision-Flan-186K. Selecting only 15% of the data, OFA achieves 98.3% of full data performance across 10 downstream benchmarks; on the smaller Vision-Flan-186K, the transferred selector surpasses full data training by 10.6%, confirming that the learned signal generalizes to datasets never seen during selector training. The same selected subsets benefit VLMs at both Qwen2.5-VL-3B and LLaVA-v1.5-7B without per model recomputation, decoupling selection from the target model. These results demonstrate that a single, transferable selector provides an effective and reusable solution for efficient multimodal instruction tuning.

AI.tldr: OFA trains a lightweight, reusable data selector once on one dataset (LLaVA-665K) that transfers to unseen datasets and models without recomputation, selecting only 15% of data to achieve 98.3% of full data performance.
AI.motivation: Existing data selection methods are model- or dataset-specific, requiring costly recomputation when either changes. OFA aims to decouple selection from target models/datasets by training a transferable selector once.
AI.method: OFA clusters multimodal instructions in frozen CLIP space, derives pseudo labels from cluster structure, trains a lightweight selector for a few epochs, and selects samples where the selector is least confident (most informative).
AI.result: Selecting 15% data, OFA achieves 98.3% of full data performance on 10 benchmarks; transferred selector on Vision-Flan-186K surpasses full data training by 10.6%; works across Qwen2.5-VL-3B and LLaVA-v1.5-7B without per-model recomputation.
AI.conclusion: A single, transferable selector provides an effective and reusable solution for efficient multimodal instruction tuning, eliminating the need for costly per-model or per-dataset recomputation.

title: Spend Your Rollouts Where It Counts: Rollout Allocation for Group-Based RL Post-Training
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the dominant paradigm for post-training large language models. However, in the online, on-policy setting, rollout generation dominates the computational cost of training. Group-based policy optimization methods compute advantages from multiple rollouts per prompt, yet they indiscriminately allocate budget to prompts with collapsed reward distributions, wasting expensive rollouts on negligible learning signals. We demonstrate that group-based updates are most effective in regimes of high reward variance. Since the policy evolves throughout training, prompt informativeness must be estimated online rather than precomputed, but exhaustively evaluating every prompt is computationally prohibitive. We introduce Pilot-Commit, a budget-aware rollout allocation framework for group-based RL post-training. Pilot-Commit decouples prompt evaluation from exploitation: a pilot stage estimates per-prompt informativeness using a fraction of the budget, and the remaining rollouts are allocated to high-leverage prompts while low-signal prompts are skipped. Across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and model scales from 1.5B to 14B parameters, Pilot-Commit matches baseline accuracy with significantly lower sampling costs, reaching target accuracy up to $1.9\times$ faster than GRPO and $4.0\times$ faster than DAPO in cumulative rollouts.

AI.tldr: Pilot-Commit is a budget-aware rollout allocation framework that improves group-based RL post-training efficiency by estimating prompt informativeness via a pilot stage, allocating more rollouts to high-variance prompts and skipping low-signal ones, achieving up to 4× faster convergence.
AI.motivation: Group-based policy optimization wastes computational resources by allocating equal rollout budgets to all prompts, including those with collapsed reward distributions that provide negligible learning signals.
AI.method: Pilot-Commit uses a two-stage approach: a pilot stage estimates per-prompt informativeness (high reward variance) using a fraction of the budget, then commits remaining rollouts to high-leverage prompts while skipping low-signal ones.
AI.result: Matches baseline accuracy across math reasoning benchmarks (1.5B to 14B models), achieving target accuracy up to 1.9× faster than GRPO and 4.0× faster than DAPO in cumulative rollouts.
AI.conclusion: Online, budget-aware allocation of rollout budgets based on prompt informativeness significantly improves sample efficiency in group-based RL post-training for LLMs.

title: Maat: The Agentic Legal Research Assistant for Competition Protection
summary: Competition law experts conducting legal research must review extensive volumes of cases, decisions, and judicial reports to identify precedents and assess key elements in competition and merger cases. Although general research assistants such as Claude and ChatGPT and legal assistants such as SaulLM-7B and LegalGPT are increasingly used to assist legal research, they remain inadequate for competition law analysis: they lack specialized domain expertise, provide insufficient official citations, or hallucinate competition law cases. We propose Maat, a ReAct agent that orchestrates tools corresponding to different tasks of the research process. Designed iteratively with competition law experts, Maat grounds cases and findings in official sources using RAG for reliability, provides rich in-line citations, falls back to web search when database coverage is insufficient, and prompts the user for clarification when queries are ambiguous. Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks. The dataset used is available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Maat is a ReAct agent designed for competition law research that outperforms general and legal AI assistants by using RAG for official source grounding, web search fallback, and user clarification prompts.
AI.motivation: Existing AI assistants (Claude, ChatGPT, SaulLM-7B, LegalGPT) lack specialized competition law expertise, provide insufficient citations, or hallucinate cases, making them inadequate for legal research.
AI.method: Maat is a ReAct agent that orchestrates tools for different research tasks, uses RAG to ground cases in official sources, provides in-line citations, falls back to web search when needed, and prompts users for clarification on ambiguous queries.
AI.result: Maat significantly outperforms all baseline assistants on case-specific tasks and performs within range of the top baseline on theoretical question tasks.
AI.conclusion: A specialized, tool-orchestrating ReAct agent with RAG and fallback mechanisms can substantially improve AI-assisted competition law research over general-purpose and existing legal assistants.

title: Cesarean Scar Defect Segmentation in Transvaginal Ultrasound Images: a Dataset and Benchmark
summary: Cesarean Scar Defect (CSD) is one of the most prevalent complications following cesarean delivery. Transvaginal ultrasonography is widely used for primary CSD screening. Accurate determination of CSD outline and dimensions is crucial for treatment. However, CSDs are frequently overlooked by sonographers due to small size and irregular morphology, suboptimal image quality, and limited clinical awareness in resource-constrained settings. Despite artificial intelligence advances in medical imaging, no public dataset exists for transvaginal ultrasound CSD segmentation. To address this gap, we present a comprehensive CSD dataset comprising 1,111 images and 16 videos, yielding 501 positive samples with confirmed CSD and precise pixel-level manual annotations. Annotations are performed following standardized clinical guidelines through collaboration between experienced sonographers and trained PhD students. This work provides high-quality benchmark resources for advancing medical image segmentation algorithms and promoting clinical innovation. Ultimately, improved CSD diagnosis and subsequent treatment strategies can enhance the quality of life in women of reproductive age, representing significant value for both medical research and clinical practice.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first public dataset for transvaginal ultrasound segmentation of Cesarean Scar Defect (CSD), containing 1,111 images and 16 videos with 501 positive samples and precise pixel-level annotations, aiming to advance AI-based segmentation algorithms and improve CSD diagnosis.
AI.motivation: CSD is a common post-cesarean complication but is frequently overlooked due to small size, irregular morphology, poor image quality, and limited clinical awareness. No public dataset exists for transvaginal ultrasound CSD segmentation, hindering AI development.
AI.method: The authors curated a comprehensive dataset of 1,111 images and 16 videos, yielding 501 positive samples with confirmed CSD. Pixel-level manual annotations were performed following standardized clinical guidelines through collaboration between experienced sonographers and trained PhD students.
AI.result: Creation of the first public CSD segmentation dataset with high-quality, clinically-guided pixel-level annotations, providing benchmark resources for medical image segmentation research.
AI.conclusion: This dataset can promote clinical innovation and improve CSD diagnosis and treatment, ultimately enhancing quality of life for women of reproductive age.

title: PIDM-DP: Physics-Informed Diffusion with Dormand-Prince Integration for Chaotic System Identification and State Reconstruction across Multiple Dynamical Regimes
comment: extended work of my journal paper submission
summary: Reconstructing continuous state trajectories of chaotic dynamical systems from sparse, noisy observations remains a fundamental open problem in nonlinear science. We introduce the Physics-Informed Diffusion Model with Dormand-Prince Integration (PIDM-DP), which embeds a fully differentiable 5th-order Dormand-Prince (DP-RK45) ODE integrator directly into the reverse sampling loop of a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). At each denoising step, physics residuals are back-propagated via automatic differentiation, constraining every generated trajectory to satisfy the system's governing equations to 5th-order accuracy. A linear-scheduled guidance mechanism that ramps the physics weight from zero at high noise levels to its full value near the clean-data limit prevents the gradient explosions that cause naive physics-informed approaches to fail on stiff systems with Jacobian eigenvalues of order $O(10^3)$. Evaluated across five benchmark systems of increasing complexity 3D Lorenz, 3D Rössler, 5D Hyperchaotic, 20D Lorenz-96, and the stiff 3D Rabinovich-Fabrikant at 10% observation density with additive Gaussian noise ($σ=0.05$), PIDM-DP achieves reconstruction RMSE improvements of up to $15.4\times$ over an unconstrained diffusion baseline and decisively outperforms the Ensemble Kalman Filter on stiff systems where ensemble covariance collapses. On the Rabinovich-Fabrikant out-of-distribution benchmark, PIDM-DP attains RMSE $0.1097 \pm 0.0269$ versus $0.9443 \pm 0.5288$ (unconstrained diffusion, $8.6\times$ worse) and $0.3561 \pm 0.3040$ (EnKF, $3.2\times$ worse), with $p<0.001$ in paired Wilcoxon tests ($N = 30$). Topological validation via the Rosenstein Lyapunov estimator confirms that PIDM-DP preserves the chaotic invariant measure.

AI.tldr: PIDM-DP integrates a 5th-order ODE solver into diffusion model sampling for reconstructing chaotic trajectories from sparse, noisy data, achieving up to 15.4× RMSE improvement over baselines and reliably preserving chaotic invariants.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing continuous chaotic trajectories from sparse, noisy observations is a fundamental open problem in nonlinear science, and existing methods (e.g., EnKF) fail on stiff systems due to covariance collapse.
AI.method: Embeds a differentiable 5th-order Dormand-Prince integrator (DP-RK45) into the DDPM reverse sampling loop, back-propagates physics residuals via autodiff, and uses a linear-scheduled guidance mechanism to prevent gradient explosion on stiff systems.
AI.result: Up to 15.4× RMSE improvement over unconstrained diffusion; decisively beats EnKF on stiff systems (e.g., Rabinovich-Fabrikant RMSE 0.1097 vs. 0.3561 for EnKF, p<0.001); preserves chaotic invariant measures verified by Rosenstein Lyapunov estimator.
AI.conclusion: PIDM-DP offers a robust, high-accuracy framework for reconstructing chaotic dynamics from sparse/noisy data, particularly excelling on stiff systems where traditional methods fail.

title: 2-ASP(Q) programs with weak constraints: Complexity and efficient implementation
summary: ASP(Q) extends Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Quantifiers over answer sets. In this paper we focus on the class of ASP(Q) programs with two quantifiers and weak constraints, denoted as 2-ASP(Q)^w. 2-ASP(Q)^w is a practically relevant fragment of ASP(Q) that is expressive enough to capture optimization problems up to the class Delta_3^P. On the theoretical side, we provide a complete complexity characterization of the main computational tasks for 2-ASP(Q)^w programs, including tight completeness results and the analysis of nontrivial cases that have not been addressed in previous works. On the practical side, we introduce novel strategies for computing (optimal) quantified answer sets in the Casper system, that rely on a Counterexample-Guided Abstraction Refinement (CEGAR) technique tailored to ASP(Q). An experimental evaluation on hard benchmarks from different application domains shows that the proposed techniques are effective in practice.

AI.tldr: This paper studies 2-ASP(Q)^w, an extension of Answer Set Programming with two quantifiers and weak constraints, providing complexity analysis and a CEGAR-based solver in the Casper system, with experimental validation on hard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To advance ASP(Q) by focusing on a practically relevant fragment (2-ASP(Q)^w) that captures optimization problems up to the class Delta_3^P, addressing gaps in complexity characterization and lacking efficient solving techniques.
AI.method: The authors provide a complete complexity characterization of 2-ASP(Q)^w programs, and introduce a CEGAR-based technique implemented in the Casper system for computing (optimal) quantified answer sets.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on hard benchmarks from multiple application domains demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed CEGAR-based strategies.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers both theoretical completeness for 2-ASP(Q)^w complexity and practical solving improvements, showing the CEGAR approach works well in practice.

title: RT-Lynx: Putting the GEMM Sparsity In a Right Way for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiT) achieve strong performance in image generation but incur substantial inference costs. While prior work has reduced this cost via quantization and distillation, semi-structured sparsity, which can nearly halve FLOPs, remains underexplored. A key reason is that most existing approaches focus on weight sparsification, and pruning 50% of the weights can remove critical model capacity and degrade generation quality. Our study, however, shows that DiT activations are intrinsically sparse and significantly more robust to N:M semi-structured sparsification than weights. Motivated by this observation, we advocate a paradigm shift from weight sparsification to activation sparsification. We propose RT-Lynx, which applies N:M sparsification to activations and incorporates error-compensation techniques to mitigate accuracy loss. We further implement highly optimized CUDA kernels tailored to this setting, achieving up to a 1.55x speedup on average in linear layers. Extensive experiments across multiple diffusion models demonstrate that our method preserves the generation quality of the original models while substantially accelerating inference.

AI.tldr: RT-Lynx proposes shifting from weight sparsification to activation sparsification for Diffusion Transformers, using N:M semi-structured sparsity with error compensation to achieve up to 1.55x speedup while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiT) have high inference costs, and semi-structured sparsity that could reduce FLOPs remains underexplored because weight sparsification (pruning 50% of weights) degrades generation quality.
AI.method: Observing that DiT activations are intrinsically sparse and more robust to N:M sparsification than weights, the authors apply N:M semi-structured sparsity to activations, incorporate error-compensation to mitigate accuracy loss, and implement optimized CUDA kernels.
AI.result: Achieves up to 1.55x average speedup in linear layers across multiple diffusion models while preserving original generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Activation sparsification is a more effective approach than weight sparsification for accelerating Diffusion Transformers, and RT-Lynx demonstrates practical speedups without quality degradation.

title: Alignment Tampering: How Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback Is Exploited to Optimize Misaligned Biases
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026, Source code: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is the standard method to align Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences. In this work, we introduce alignment tampering, a potential vulnerability where the LLM undergoing alignment influences the preference dataset, causing RLHF to amplify undesired behaviors. This arises from core limitations of RLHF: (1) preference datasets are constructed from the LLM's own outputs, allowing it to influence them, and (2) pairwise comparisons only indicate which response is better, not why. These limitations can be exploited to cause alignment tampering. For example, if an LLM generates biased responses with higher quality, annotators will prefer them based on quality. However, preference labels do not distinguish quality from bias, and the reward model inherits this limitation. Optimizing such rewards through reinforcement learning or best-of-N sampling can amplify misaligned biases. Our experiments demonstrate amplification across diverse biases: from keyword bias to propaganda (e.g., sexism), brand promotion, and instrumental goal-seeking. Mitigation remains challenging, as existing techniques for robust RLHF fail to fully resolve alignment tampering without sacrificing response quality. These findings reveal structural vulnerabilities of current RLHF and emphasize the need to prevent this vulnerability. Project page: https://alignment-tampering.github.io/

AI.tldr: This paper identifies "alignment tampering," a vulnerability in RLHF where LLMs can influence preference datasets used for their own alignment, leading to amplification of undesired behaviors like bias and propaganda, while existing mitigation methods struggle to address it without degrading response quality.
AI.motivation: RLHF is the standard method for aligning LLMs with human preferences, but the authors identify a structural vulnerability where the LLM undergoing alignment can influence the preference dataset, causing RLHF to inadvertently amplify undesired behaviors rather than correct them.
AI.method: The authors formalize alignment tampering through two core RLHF limitations: (1) preference datasets are constructed from the LLM's own outputs, allowing it to influence them, and (2) pairwise comparisons only indicate which response is better, not why. They experimentally demonstrate this across diverse biases including keyword bias, propaganda (e.g., sexism), brand promotion, and instrumental goal-seeking.
AI.result: Experiments show that alignment tampering can amplify various misaligned biases. For instance, biased responses with higher quality are preferred by annotators based on quality alone, and preference labels fail to distinguish quality from bias. The reward model inherits this limitation, and optimizing such rewards amplifies the misaligned biases.
AI.conclusion: Alignment tampering reveals structural vulnerabilities in current RLHF, and existing mitigation techniques for robust RLHF fail to fully resolve the issue without sacrificing response quality, emphasizing the need to prevent this vulnerability.

title: Receipt Replay OOD: A Small Benchmark for Screen Replay Detection Under Domain Shift
summary: Public datasets such as DLC-2021, SynID, and KID34K have significantly contributed to research on presentation attack detection for identity documents, including screen replay attacks. However, evaluation of out-of-domain (OOD) robustness remains insufficiently explored, especially under realistic domain shifts. In this work, we introduce Receipt Replay OOD, a small out-of-domain benchmark for screen replay detection. Receipts share several characteristics with identity documents, including planar geometry, curved corners, wear-and-tear artifacts, and text or logo patterns, while avoiding personally identifiable information constraints commonly associated with identity documents. We evaluate document replay detection models under cross-domain conditions and demonstrate the impact of domain shift on generalization performance. The dataset is publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Receipt Replay OOD, a small out-of-domain benchmark using receipts to evaluate screen replay attack detection models' robustness under realistic domain shifts, as receipts share key visual features with identity documents but avoid privacy constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing public datasets (DLC-2021, SynID, KID34K) support presentation attack detection for identity documents, but out-of-domain (OOD) robustness under realistic domain shifts remains insufficiently explored.
AI.method: The authors introduce Receipt Replay OOD, a small benchmark using receipts—which share planar geometry, curved corners, wear-and-tear artifacts, and text/logo patterns with identity documents—to evaluate screen replay detection models under cross-domain conditions.
AI.result: The evaluation demonstrates that domain shift significantly impacts generalization performance of document replay detection models under cross-domain conditions.
AI.conclusion: Receipt-based benchmarks serve as effective proxies for identity document anti-spoofing evaluation while avoiding personally identifiable information (PII) constraints, and the dataset is publicly available to facilitate further research on OOD robustness.

title: More Expressive Feedforward Layers: Part I. Token-Adaptive Mixing of Activations
summary: Feedforward network (FFN) layers account for a large fraction of parameters and nonlinear expressivity in Transformer-based large language models (LLMs). Despite the evolution from ReLU and GELU to gated variants such as SwiGLU, most FFN designs still use a single fixed activation function, applying the same nonlinear transformation to all tokens. In this work, we propose Mixture of Activations (MoA), a token-adaptive FFN design that mixes a dictionary of activation functions using lightweight input-dependent gates while sharing the same linear projections. As an input-independent counterpart, we also introduce learnable activations (LA), which form linear combinations of activation functions for both ReLU-type and SwiGLU-type FFNs. Theoretically, we establish strict finite-width expressive separations among fixed-activation FFNs, LA, and MoA: LA strictly contains fixed-activation FFNs, while MoA strictly contains LA, with the additional expressivity arising from input-dependent nonlinear hybridization. Empirically, we evaluate MoA through extensive pre-training experiments on dense and MoE language models ranging from 0.12B to 2B parameters under different token budgets, optimizers, and learning rate schedules. MoA consistently achieves lower terminal loss and exhibits more favorable scaling behavior than well-tuned baselines, with minimal parameter and computational overhead. These results suggest that token-adaptive activation mixing is a simple and effective mechanism for improving FFN expressivity in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Mixture of Activations (MoA), a token-adaptive FFN design that mixes multiple activation functions via lightweight input-dependent gates, and shows it consistently outperforms fixed-activation FFNs in LLMs with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Current FFN designs in LLMs use a single fixed activation function applied uniformly to all tokens, which limits expressive power. The authors aim to improve FFN expressivity by allowing different activation functions for different tokens.
AI.method: Proposes MoA (token-adaptive activation mixing) and LA (learnable activations). MoA uses lightweight input-dependent gates to mix a dictionary of activation functions while sharing linear projections. LA forms input-independent linear combinations of activation functions. Theoretical separation proofs show MoA > LA > fixed-activation FFNs.
AI.result: Experiments on dense and MoE language models (0.12B-2B parameters) show MoA consistently achieves lower terminal loss and better scaling behavior than well-tuned baselines, with minimal parameter and computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Token-adaptive activation mixing is a simple and effective mechanism for improving FFN expressivity in LLMs, with theoretical guarantees of strict expressivity gains over fixed-activation and input-independent approaches.

title: Natural Language Query to Configuration for Retrieval Agents
summary: Modern retrieval agents expose many configuration choices -- LLM, retriever, number of documents, number of hops, and synthesis strategy -- each shaping both answer quality and serving cost. Today, these pipelines are typically hand-tuned once per workload, leaving substantial per-query optimization untapped. We formulate the problem: given a natural-language query and either an accuracy or a budget target, select from a predefined pipeline catalog the configuration that minimizes cost or maximizes accuracy at inference time. We propose **BRANE**, which uses an LLM to convert each query into workload-specific characteristics, then trains a lightweight per-configuration predictor that estimates whether the pipeline will answer the query correctly. At inference time, **BRANE** selects the configuration that maximizes predicted correctness penalized by cost, exposing a tunable cost-quality tradeoff without retraining. Across MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench, **BRANE** consistently pushes the cost-quality Pareto frontier, matches the best fixed configuration's accuracy at up to 89% lower cost, and outperforms LLM-routing, rule-based, and fine-tuned Qwen3-4B baselines. These results show that per-query configuration of the full retrieval pipeline is a practical alternative to static workload-level tuning.

AI.tldr: BRANE is a system that per-query selects the best retrieval pipeline configuration (LLM, retriever, hops, etc.) to optimize cost-quality tradeoffs, achieving up to 89% cost reduction while matching fixed-config accuracy.
AI.motivation: Modern retrieval agents have many configurable components (LLM, retriever, document count, hops, synthesis strategy) that are hand-tuned once per workload, leaving significant per-query optimization opportunity untapped.
AI.method: BRANE uses an LLM to convert each query into workload-specific characteristics, trains lightweight per-configuration predictors to estimate correctness, and at inference selects the configuration maximizing predicted correctness penalized by cost.
AI.result: Across MuSiQue, BrowseComp-Plus, and FinanceBench, BRANE pushes the cost-quality Pareto frontier, matches best fixed-config accuracy at up to 89% lower cost, and outperforms LLM-routing, rule-based, and fine-tuned Qwen3-4B baselines.
AI.conclusion: Per-query configuration of the full retrieval pipeline is a practical alternative to static workload-level tuning, enabling tunable cost-quality tradeoffs without retraining.

title: REVERSE: Reinforcing Evidence Verification and Search for Agentic Image geo-localization
summary: Image geo-localization aims to determine where a photograph was taken, a task that often requires more than recognizing visible landmarks. Human experts typically solve it through an iterative workflow: they inspect informative regions, form location hypotheses, seek external evidence, and revise their judgments as new clues appear. Existing methods only partially capture this process: direct prediction methods bypass evidence acquisition altogether, while retrieval-augmented methods introduce external evidence but usually provide limited supervision on the intermediate decisions of where to search, how to query, and how to filter noisy results. We present REVERSE, a framework that reinforces the interplay between evidence search and verification to enable multi-turn agentic reasoning. REVERSE teaches three intermediate decisions: where to look, what to query, and what evidence to trust. To support this, we construct tool-grounded trajectories with annotated region selections, search observations, and geo-informative evidence labels, and introduce process rewards for visual grounding, query utility, and evidence discrimination. An offline search cache makes retrieval observations stable and reusable during reinforcement learning, enabling dense supervision over noisy search results. With a 4B model, REVERSE outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines and rivals substantially larger models on Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k. Code is available at https://github.com/yonglleee/REVERSE.

AI.tldr: REVERSE is a multi-turn agentic framework for image geo-localization that teaches a model where to look, what to query, and what evidence to trust, using process rewards and an offline search cache, achieving strong results with only a 4B model.
AI.motivation: Existing geo-localization methods either directly predict locations without evidence acquisition or use retrieval-augmented approaches with limited supervision on intermediate decisions (where to search, how to query, how to filter noise). The goal is to enable iterative, expert-like reasoning with explicit training on search and verification steps.
AI.method: REVERSE trains three intermediate decisions via tool-grounded trajectories with annotated region selections, search observations, and geo-informative evidence labels; it introduces process rewards for visual grounding, query utility, and evidence discrimination; an offline search cache stabilizes retrieval observations for reinforcement learning.
AI.result: With a 4B model, REVERSE outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines and rivals substantially larger models on Im2GPS3k and YFCC4k benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly teaching the iterative search-and-verify reasoning process with dense reward supervision, especially using an offline cache for stable RL training, significantly improves geo-localization even with a small model.

title: Bilevel Optimization over Saddle Points of Zero-Sum Markov Games
comment: Accepted to the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) often has a hierarchical structure, where an upper-level (UL) learner selects model parameters and a lower-level (LL) decision-making process responds, naturally leading to a bilevel optimization problem. Most existing bilevel RL methods assume a single-policy LL Markov decision process (MDP), and therefore fail to capture competitive structures arising in applications such as incentive design, where multiple policies interact. We study bilevel optimization problems in which the LL problem is a regularized min-max zero-sum Markov game and the UL objective is optimized through the saddle-point equilibrium induced by the LL game. In this work, we propose penalty-augmented Nikaido-Isoda descent-ascent (PANDA), a penalty-based first-order policy-gradient method based on the Nikaido-Isoda function. By exploiting the min-max game structure, PANDA avoids computing UL hypergradients and does not require second-order information. We prove that PANDA converges to stationary points without convexity assumptions on either the UL or LL objectives. Moreover, PANDA reaches an $ε$-stationary point in $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-1})$ iterations with sample complexity $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(ε^{-3})$, matching the best-known rates for bilevel RL with single-policy LL MDPs. Experiments demonstrate the superior performance of PANDA over closely related baselines.

AI.tldr: PANDA is a first-order penalty-based policy-gradient method for solving bilevel RL problems where the lower level is a regularized min-max zero-sum Markov game, achieving convergence to ε-stationary points in Õ(ε⁻¹) iterations with Õ(ε⁻³) sample complexity without convexity assumptions.
AI.motivation: Existing bilevel RL methods assume single-policy lower-level MDPs, failing to capture competitive multi-policy interactions in applications like incentive design. The authors aim to extend bilevel RL to handle min-max zero-sum Markov games at the lower level.
AI.method: PANDA (Penalty-augmented Nikaido-Isoda Descent-Ascent): a penalty-based first-order policy-gradient method using the Nikaido-Isoda function that exploits the min-max game structure to avoid computing UL hypergradients and second-order information.
AI.result: Converges to stationary points without convexity assumptions on UL or LL objectives. Achieves Õ(ε⁻¹) iteration complexity and Õ(ε⁻³) sample complexity for ε-stationary points, matching best-known rates for bilevel RL with single-policy LL MDPs. Experiments show superior performance over baselines.
AI.conclusion: PANDA effectively extends bilevel RL to competitive multi-policy lower-level settings (zero-sum Markov games) with strong theoretical guarantees and empirical performance.

title: MUSE-Autoskill: Self-Evolving Agents via Skill Creation, Memory, Management, and Evaluation
comment: 30 pages, 8 figures, 13 tables, working in progress
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents rely on reusable skills to solve complex tasks. However, existing skill creation approaches treat skills as isolated and static artifacts, limiting their reusability, reliability, and long-term improvement. We propose MUSE-Autoskill Agent (Memory-Utilizing Skill Evolution), a skill-centric agent framework that lets agents continuously improve their task-solving capability by creating, reusing, and refining skills under a unified lifecycle (creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement). Our framework enables agents to create skills on demand, store and reuse them across tasks, organize and select them efficiently, and evaluate them through unit tests and runtime feedback for continuous refinement. We further introduce skill-level memory that accumulates experience for each skill across tasks, enabling more effective reuse and adaptation over time. Experiments on SkillsBench provide initial evidence that lifecycle-managed skills can improve task success, efficiency, reuse, and cross-agent transfer, highlighting the importance of treating skills as long-lived, experience-aware, and testable assets.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MUSE-Autoskill Agent, a framework that helps LLM agents continuously create, reuse, and refine skills through a unified lifecycle (creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement), enabling long-term improvement and cross-agent transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing skill creation approaches treat skills as isolated and static artifacts, which limits their reusability, reliability, and long-term improvement potential for LLM agents.
AI.method: The paper introduces a skill-centric framework with a unified lifecycle covering creation, memory, management, evaluation, and refinement, along with skill-level memory that accumulates experience across tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on SkillsBench show that lifecycle-managed skills improve task success, efficiency, reuse, and cross-agent transfer.
AI.conclusion: Treating skills as long-lived, experience-aware, and testable assets is important for improving LLM agent capabilities.

title: RoadGIE: Towards A Global-Scale Aerial Benchmark for Generalizable Interactive Road Extraction
summary: Accurate road segmentation from aerial imagery is fundamental to many geospatial applications. However, existing datasets often suffer from limited scene diversity, low semantic granularity, and poor structural continuity, restricting their generalization across environments. To address these challenges, we introduce WorldRoadSeg-360K, the largest and most diverse road segmentation dataset to date, comprising 366,947 high-resolution images collected from 38 countries and 223 cities across various terrains and continents. WorldRoadSeg-360K serves as a comprehensive benchmark and reveals key challenges in handling diverse and structurally complex scenes. Automated approaches often struggle to preserve road connectivity, while current interactive methods lack efficient, topology-sensitive tools for real-world road editing. To this end, we present RoadGIE, establishing a novel interactive paradigm for road extraction in remote sensing. Unlike prior point- or box-based prompting strategies, RoadGIE supports connectivity-aware prompts, including clicks and scribbles, which inherently align with the topology of road networks. To improve structural consistency and mitigate performance degradation during iterative interactions, RoadGIE integrates an expert-guided prompting strategy and adapts the skeleton-based recall loss for interactive scenarios. RoadGIE achieves state-of-the-art performance in both segmentation accuracy and topological consistency on WorldRoadSeg-360K and other benchmarks, while maintaining efficient operation with only 3.7M parameters. The code are publicly available at: https://github.com/chaineypung/RoadGIE

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WorldRoadSeg-360K, the largest and most diverse road segmentation dataset (366,947 images from 38 countries), and RoadGIE, an interactive road extraction model using connectivity-aware prompts (clicks/scribbles) with expert-guided strategy and skeleton-based recall loss, achieving SOTA performance with only 3.7M parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing road segmentation datasets suffer from limited scene diversity, low semantic granularity, and poor structural continuity, restricting generalization across environments. Automated methods struggle with road connectivity, and interactive methods lack efficient topology-sensitive tools for real-world road editing.
AI.method: RoadGIE introduces a novel interactive paradigm with connectivity-aware prompts (clicks and scribbles) that align with road network topology, integrates an expert-guided prompting strategy, and adapts skeleton-based recall loss for interactive scenarios to improve structural consistency.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in both segmentation accuracy and topological consistency on WorldRoadSeg-360K and other benchmarks, with efficient operation using only 3.7M parameters.
AI.conclusion: The combination of the large-scale diverse dataset WorldRoadSeg-360K and the interactive model RoadGIE establishes a new benchmark and paradigm for road extraction, effectively addressing connectivity and topology challenges in remote sensing.

title: WINDQuant: Weight-Informed Neural Decision-Making for Global Mixed-Precision LLM Quantization
summary: Quantization is an effective approach to reduce the memory footprint and inference cost of large language models (LLMs), yet maintaining performance in the ultra-low-bit regime remains challenging. Existing post-training methods often suffer from severe accuracy degradation, while quantization-aware training requires costly retraining and additional resources. Moreover, most mixed-precision strategies rely on coarse-grained or heuristic sensitivity analysis that overlooks fine-grained variations within weight matrices. We propose WINDQuant, a reinforcement-learning-based allocation controller for ultra-low-bit LLM quantization. Rather than introducing another low-level quantization operator, WINDQuant learns how to assign bit-widths and quantization treatments to fine-grained column chunks under a global storage budget. By operating at the column-chunk level, WINDQuant enables flexible and fine-grained precision assignment within layers under a global target bit-width. The implementation combines PPO with activation-aware calibration, lightweight per-unit quantizer fitting, and explicit effective-bit accounting of the learned mixed-precision plan. Experiments on LLaMA models demonstrate that WINDQuant achieves competitive performance in ultra-low-bit settings while reducing optimization overhead relative to retraining-based approaches, highlighting reinforcement learning as a practical controller for adaptive mixed-precision quantization.

AI.tldr: WINDQuant uses reinforcement learning (PPO) to assign fine-grained mixed-precision bit-widths at the column-chunk level for ultra-low-bit LLM quantization, achieving competitive performance with lower optimization overhead than retraining-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing ultra-low-bit quantization methods suffer from severe accuracy degradation (post-training) or require costly retraining. Mixed-precision strategies rely on coarse-grained/heuristic sensitivity analysis that ignores fine-grained variations within weight matrices.
AI.method: WINDQuant combines a reinforcement learning controller (PPO) with activation-aware calibration, lightweight per-unit quantizer fitting, and explicit effective-bit accounting to assign bit-widths and quantization treatments to fine-grained column chunks under a global storage budget.
AI.result: Experiments on LLaMA models show WINDQuant achieves competitive performance in ultra-low-bit settings while reducing optimization overhead relative to retraining-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Reinforcement learning is a practical and efficient controller for adaptive mixed-precision quantization, enabling fine-grained precision assignment without costly retraining.

title: Natural Human Motion Recovery by Aligning High-Order Temporal Dynamics from Monocular Videos
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures. Accepted as an Oral presentation and Best Paper Candidate at CVPR 2026. Project page: https://zju3dv.github.io/htd-refine/
summary: Human motion recovered from monocular videos often appears overly smooth or dynamically inconsistent, even when joint positions are numerically accurate.
