AI.method: 1) ATLAS: systematic lineage-aware search to trace RLVR datasets back to atomic sources. 2) Source-level Counterfactual Attribution (SCA): measures marginal utility of samples by comparing per-atomic-source RL checkpoints against a shared base model. 3) Composite dataset quality score Q derived from SCA signals.
AI.result: Over 99.7% of 1.45M instances attributed to just 20 atomic sources; most datasets are variants of shared upstream sources. DAPO++ consistently improves performance on held-out benchmarks, and Q strongly correlates with downstream RLVR training effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The RLVR data ecosystem is highly concentrated with limited genuinely new data. Lineage-aware curation (SCA) and the quality score Q enable more effective, decontaminated training datasets.

title: Generative Animations: A Multi-Model Pipeline for Prompt-Driven Motion Synthesis
summary: Animation elevates digital documents into immersive experiences, yet creating custom motion paths remains cumbersome, requiring designers to manually select presets, plot Bézier points, and configure timing properties. We introduce Generative Animations, a system that transforms natural language prompts into production-ready animations. By chaining Large Language Models (LLMs) for semantic parsing with the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for visual grounding, our pipeline automatically generates motion paths that respect scene geometry, handle depth-based occlusions, and honor 3D perspective transforms. We demonstrate the system through three use cases: contour-following trajectories, orbital animations with z-order awareness, and perspective-aligned motion on transformed objects.

AI.tldr: A system called Generative Animations that uses LLMs and SAM to automatically create motion path animations from natural language prompts, handling scene geometry, depth occlusions, and 3D perspective.
AI.motivation: Creating custom motion paths in animations is tedious and cumbersome, requiring designers to manually select presets, plot Bézier points, and configure timing properties.
AI.method: Chains Large Language Models (LLMs) for semantic parsing with the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for visual grounding to automatically generate motion paths.
AI.result: Demonstrated through three use cases: contour-following trajectories, orbital animations with z-order awareness, and perspective-aligned motion on transformed objects.
AI.conclusion: Generative Animations transforms natural language prompts into production-ready animations that respect scene geometry, handle depth-based occlusions, and honor 3D perspective transforms.

title: Convergence of Spectral Descent for Non-smooth Optimization
summary: The Muon optimizer has recently demonstrated remarkable empirical success in training large language models. However, the theoretical understanding of its mechanisms remains limited. Current convergence guarantees for Muon rely heavily on smoothness assumptions, leaving its non-smooth convergence behavior largely unexplored. In this work, we take a step toward bridging this gap by investigating Spectral Descent (SD), a simplified variant of Muon, together with its truncated counterpart, Truncated Spectral Descent (TSD). Under convexity, Lipschitz continuity, and sharpness conditions, we establish global linear convergence for both SD and TSD in non-smooth convex formulations. We also study regularized variants equipped with decoupled weight decay and derive sublinear convergence guarantees through their connection with Frank-Wolfe methods. Finally, we apply our theoretical framework to robust low-rank matrix recovery under mixed sparse and dense noise regimes and provide rigorous recovery guarantees. Numerical experiments support the theoretical findings and demonstrate the effectiveness of Muon-type methods for non-smooth optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes convergence guarantees for Spectral Descent (SD) and Truncated Spectral Descent (TSD), simplified variants of the Muon optimizer, under non-smooth convex optimization settings with sharpness conditions.
AI.motivation: Muon optimizer shows empirical success in LLM training but lacks theoretical understanding, especially in non-smooth settings where existing convergence guarantees rely on smoothness assumptions.
AI.method: The authors analyze Spectral Descent (SD) and Truncated Spectral Descent (TSD) under convexity, Lipschitz continuity, and sharpness conditions. They also study regularized variants with decoupled weight decay via connections to Frank-Wolfe methods.
AI.result: Global linear convergence for SD and TSD in non-smooth convex formulations; sublinear convergence for regularized variants with decoupled weight decay; rigorous recovery guarantees for robust low-rank matrix recovery under mixed noise regimes.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical findings bridge the gap between Muon's empirical success and its theoretical understanding, supported by numerical experiments demonstrating effectiveness for non-smooth optimization.

title: MRT: Masked Region Transformer for Layered Image Generation and Editing at Scale
summary: Layered image generation and editing is a fundamental capability that enables layer-wise reuse, editing, and composition of generated visual content, analogous to word-level editing in natural language. Despite its importance, this remains an underexplored area at scale. To address this gap, we present MRT, a 20B-parameter masked region diffusion model tailored for multi-layer transparent image generation and editing, trained on over 10M multilingual design samples spanning diverse aspect ratios and textual prompts. To fully leverage this scale, we make two key technical contributions. First, we unify three complementary tasks including text-to-layers, image-to-layers, and layers-to-layers within a shared masked region diffusion framework, where selective token masking enables flexible layer-wise generation and editing. Second, to enable overflow layer generation, we introduce an overflow-aware canvas layer that handles boundary inconsistencies and supports semi-transparent background synthesis, enabling complete editable layers extending beyond visible canvas boundaries. Additionally, we apply diffusion distillation to achieve 8-step, real-time multi-layer generation with minimal quality degradation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art approaches, including various commercial systems, across all three tasks, establishing a new benchmark for multi-layer transparent image generation. Notably, our model significantly outperforms the concurrent Qwen-Image-Layered model in image-to-layers quality according to user-study results, while achieving 10-100\times faster inference and reducing activation GPU memory consumption by 50-90\% during image-to-layer inference.

AI.tldr: MRT is a 20B-parameter masked region diffusion model for multi-layer transparent image generation and editing, unifying three tasks (text-to-layers, image-to-layers, layers-to-layers) with overflow-aware canvas and real-time 8-step inference.
AI.motivation: Layered image generation and editing is fundamentally important for layer-wise reuse and composition but remains underexplored at scale.
AI.method: 1) Unified masked region diffusion framework for three complementary tasks. 2) Overflow-aware canvas layer for boundary inconsistencies and semi-transparent backgrounds. 3) Diffusion distillation for 8-step real-time generation.
AI.result: Substantially outperforms prior SOTA and commercial systems across all three tasks; beats Qwen-Image-Layered in image-to-layers quality with 10-100× faster inference and 50-90% less GPU memory.
AI.conclusion: MRT establishes a new benchmark for multi-layer transparent image generation and editing, demonstrating that scale (20B params, 10M samples) combined with the unified masked region framework achieves superior performance and efficiency.

title: TED: Related Party Transaction guided Tax Evasion Detection on Heterogeneous Graph
comment: Accepted by Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery (DMKD25)
summary: Tax evasion causes severe losses of government revenues and disturbs the economic order of fair competition. To help alleviate this problem, the latest tax evasion detection solutions utilize expert knowledge to extract features and then train classifiers to determine whether a company is suspected of tax evasion. However, existing solutions mainly focus on the statistical features of the company, but fail to exploit the rich interactive information in tax scenarios, which affect the detection performance. In this paper, we first model the tax scenario as a heterogeneous graph and study the tax evasion detection problem under the heterogeneous graph model. To improve the performance of tax evasion detection, a novel graph neural network model is proposed to extract the comprehensive information of heterogeneous graphs. Specifically, we use heterogeneous and complex related party transaction groups to filter low-level noise information. Moreover, a hierarchical attention mechanism is designed to capture the deeper structure and semantic information hidden in the related party transaction group. We apply our method to the real risk management system of the tax bureau, and evaluate it on two human-labeled real-world tax datasets. The results demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art in the tax evasion detection task.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a graph neural network model with hierarchical attention for tax evasion detection, using heterogeneous graphs to model tax scenarios and related party transaction groups, achieving state-of-the-art performance on real-world tax datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing tax evasion detection solutions rely on statistical features and expert knowledge, failing to exploit rich interactive information in tax scenarios, which limits detection performance.
AI.method: (1) Models tax scenarios as heterogeneous graphs; (2) Uses heterogeneous and complex related party transaction groups to filter noise; (3) Designs a hierarchical attention mechanism to capture deeper structure and semantic information.
AI.result: Applied to a real tax bureau risk management system and evaluated on two human-labeled real-world datasets; significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed graph neural network effectively captures interactive information in tax scenarios, leading to improved tax evasion detection performance in practice.

title: Can Retrieval Heads See Images? Multimodal Retrieval Heads in Long-Context Vision-Language Models
summary: Large vision-language models increasingly rely on long-context modeling to reason over documents, hour-level videos, and long-horizon agent trajectories, requiring them to locate relevant evidence across interleaved text and images. Prior work has studied this behavior using retrieval heads in large language models, but its copy-based criterion does not directly apply when evidence appears in images. We introduce a multimodal retrieval head detection method that scores attention from question tokens to textual or visual evidence. With this method, we show that multimodal retrieval heads are sparse, intrinsic, and causally important: only 4.4-10.2% of attention heads account for 50% of the positive retrieval-score mass, and masking the top-5% selected heads drops MMLongBench-Doc from 48.2% to 5.7% and SlideVQA from 71.2% to 8.9%, while random-head masking is far less damaging. Further analysis shows that these heads are partly shared across modalities yet remain dynamic within each modality, with image retrieval heads changing more than text retrieval heads as context length and haystack modality change. Without further training, we find that these heads can also be used directly to rank visually rich documents: on MMDocIR, Qwen3-VL-8B selected-head scoring improves Recall@1 by 7.7/7.4 macro/micro points for page retrieval and 6.3/6.8 points for layout retrieval over the strongest reported baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method to detect multimodal retrieval heads in vision-language models that attend to both text and image evidence. These heads are sparse (4.4-10.2%), causally important (masking top-5% drops accuracy drastically), and can be used directly for document retrieval tasks without further training.
AI.motivation: Prior work on retrieval heads in LLMs used copy-based criteria that don't work when evidence appears in images. The authors aim to extend retrieval head analysis to multimodal settings where text and visual evidence are interleaved.
AI.method: The authors propose a multimodal retrieval head detection method that scores attention from question tokens to textual or visual evidence. They analyze the sparsity, causality, and modality-sharing patterns of these heads in large vision-language models.
AI.result: Only 4.4-10.2% of attention heads account for 50% of retrieval-score mass. Masking top-5% heads drops MMLongBench-Doc from 48.2% to 5.7% and SlideVQA from 71.2% to 8.9%. The heads improve Recall@1 by 7.7/7.4 macro/micro points on MMDocIR.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal retrieval heads are sparse, causally critical, partly shared across modalities yet dynamic, and can be directly applied to improve document retrieval tasks without additional training.

title: Probabilistic Recurrent Intention Switching Model
summary: Inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) recovers reward functions from observed behavior, yet traditional methods assume a single stationary reward that cannot capture goal switching within an episode. Recent multi-intention IRL methods address this by segmenting trajectories, but model intention transitions as either a memoryless Markov chain or via manual state augmentation with a fixed history window. We propose the Probabilistic Recurrent Intention Switching Model (PRISM), which replaces both mechanisms with a lightweight recurrent network that maps observation history to a per-step intention distribution. We prove that the resulting EM objective decomposes exactly into independent per-intention reward subproblems, each solvable in closed form, yielding an $\mathcal{O}(nK)$ E-step with no variational approximation. We evaluate PRISM on a non-Markovian gridworld, a mouse labyrinth, and BridgeData~V2 robotic manipulation, the first large-scale robotic application of multi-intention IRL. Across all settings PRISM achieves the highest held-out log-likelihood while recovering nameable, temporally coherent intentions from unlabeled demonstrations, suggesting that discrete goal switching is present in both biological and artificial agents.

AI.tldr: PRISM is a multi-intention IRL method that uses a lightweight recurrent network to model intention switching, achieving closed-form EM optimization and state-of-the-art results on gridworld, mouse labyrinth, and robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional IRL assumes a single stationary reward, failing to capture goal switching within episodes. Existing multi-intention methods rely on either memoryless Markov chains or manual state augmentation with fixed history windows, which are restrictive.
AI.method: PRISM replaces prior mechanisms with a lightweight recurrent network mapping observation history to per-step intention distributions. The EM objective decomposes into independent per-intention reward subproblems solvable in closed form, yielding an O(nK) E-step with no variational approximation.
AI.result: PRISM achieves the highest held-out log-likelihood across a non-Markovian gridworld, mouse labyrinth, and BridgeData V2 robotic manipulation, recovering nameable and temporally coherent intentions from unlabeled demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Discrete goal switching is present in both biological (mouse) and artificial (robot) agents, and PRISM effectively captures this behavior without restrictive modeling assumptions.

title: A Dynamic Programming Framework for Discovering Count and Values of Multilevel Image Thresholding
summary: Multilevel Image thresholding is an important preprocessing algorithm in computer vision applications nowadays. Since most common thresholding methods take the desired count of thresholds as input by the user, thresholding methods that automatically determines a suitable count of thresholds from the input image itself are advantageous. In this article, a novel thresholding method based on a dynamic programming algorithm and a modification of Minimum Error Thresholding (MET) criterion is thoroughly presented. An empirical statistical study is performed to pinpoint why this proposed method is superior. Moreover, an extended comparison between this proposed method and other state-of-the-art methods is performed on a comprehensive set of natural, satellite and medical test images. The numerical results show that the proposed MET-DP method takes much less time than traditional dynamic programming thresholding methods when the number of thresholds is high. The proposed method can detect a suitable count of thresholds for most of tested images of different types. However, traditional methods that take the count of thresholds as input produce thresholded images of higher structural similarity index measure (SSIM) and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) values than MET-DP. Source code can be found on https://w3id.org/met-dp/article1-code

AI.tldr: This paper proposes MET-DP, a multilevel image thresholding method that automatically determines the number of thresholds using dynamic programming and a modified Minimum Error Thresholding criterion. It is faster than traditional DP methods for high threshold counts but achieves lower SSIM and PSNR than methods that take threshold count as input.
AI.motivation: To develop an automatic thresholding method that can determine a suitable number of thresholds from the input image itself, eliminating the need for user input for threshold count.
AI.method: A dynamic programming algorithm combined with a modified Minimum Error Thresholding (MET) criterion (MET-DP).
AI.result: MET-DP is significantly faster than traditional dynamic programming methods for high threshold counts. It automatically detects suitable threshold counts for most test images. However, traditional methods produce higher SSIM and PSNR values.
AI.conclusion: MET-DP offers computational efficiency and automatic threshold count selection but sacrifices some image quality (SSIM, PSNR) compared to methods that require manual threshold count input.

title: Sampling Data with Chains of Forward-Backward Diffusion Steps
summary: Sampling from learned high-dimensional distributions is a foundational computational problem. We introduce U-turn chains: Markov chains obtained by iterating short forward-backward steps of a diffusion model, in which each step proposes a move that remains on the learned data manifold and, paired with a Metropolis-Hastings correction, samples from energy-modified targets. For synthetic languages, we show that minimal U-turn dynamics undergoes an ergodicity-breaking phase transition driven by fragmentation of the data manifold; ergodicity is restored at larger U-turn magnitude. In the non-ergodic regime, low-level features relax faster than high-level ones, an ordering that inverts only at sufficiently large U-turn magnitude. We test these predictions on natural language and natural images. In both modalities, minimal U-turns relax slowly, especially for high-level features approximated by deep representations in CNNs or LLMs. The layer-ordering inversion appears only at large noise when mixing is efficient -- signatures consistent with strongly constrained, weakly mixing local dynamics. We discuss the implications of these results for sampling with diffusion models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces U-turn chains (short forward-backward diffusion steps with Metropolis-Hastings correction) for sampling from high-dimensional distributions, and finds they undergo an ergodicity-breaking phase transition driven by data manifold fragmentation, with implications for diffusion model sampling efficiency across synthetic languages, natural language, and images.
AI.motivation: Sampling from learned high-dimensional distributions is a foundational computational problem, and existing methods face challenges with ergodicity and mixing efficiency, especially for high-level features.
AI.method: U-turn chains: Markov chains obtained by iterating short forward-backward steps of a diffusion model, where each step proposes moves on the learned data manifold paired with Metropolis-Hastings correction to sample from energy-modified targets.
AI.result: (1) Minimal U-turn dynamics undergo an ergodicity-breaking phase transition driven by data manifold fragmentation; (2) ergodicity is restored at larger U-turn magnitude; (3) In non-ergodic regime, low-level features relax faster than high-level ones, with inversion only at large U-turn magnitude; (4) Minimal U-turns show slow relaxation for high-level features in CNNs/LLMs, with layer-ordering inversion only at large noise.
AI.conclusion: The results indicate strongly constrained, weakly mixing local dynamics in both natural language and images, with implications for improving sampling efficiency in diffusion models.

title: Gemini Embedding 2: A Native Multimodal Embedding Model from Gemini
summary: We introduce Gemini Embedding 2, a native multimodal embedding model that allows embedding video, audio, image, and text modalities in a unified representation space. We leverage the multimodal capabilities of Gemini to produce embeddings for arbitrary combinations of interleaved inputs across all these modalities that generalize well across a wide variety of tasks. Applying large-scale contrastive learning in a multi-task multi-stage training setup, we achieve state-of-the-art performance on key embedding benchmarks including unimodal, cross-modal, and multimodal retrieval spanning a diverse set of tasks. We show that our embedding model demonstrates strong performance (with a score of 62.9 R@1 on MSCOCO, 68.8 NDCG@10 on Vatex, 69.9 on MTEB multilingual and 84.0 on MTEB Code) across a variety of tasks surpassing the performance of specialized models. These unified capabilities make Gemini Embedding 2 a promising candidate for downstream use cases such as RAG, recommendation and search. Furthermore, its robust zero-shot performance across distinct fields - from astronomy and bioscience to fine arts and the culinary arts - establishes it as a highly reliable, out-of-the-box representation even for specialized domains.

AI.tldr: Gemini Embedding 2 is a unified multimodal embedding model achieving state-of-the-art performance across video, audio, image, and text modalities on diverse benchmarks, surpassing specialized models.
AI.motivation: To create a single native multimodal embedding model that unifies video, audio, image, and text in a shared representation space, eliminating the need for separate specialized embedding models.
AI.method: Leverages Gemini's multimodal capabilities with large-scale contrastive learning in a multi-task, multi-stage training setup to produce embeddings for arbitrary interleaved multimodal inputs.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA scores: 62.9 R@1 on MSCOCO, 68.8 NDCG@10 on Vatex, 69.9 on MTEB multilingual, 84.0 on MTEB Code, surpassing specialized models across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gemini Embedding 2 is a promising unified solution for downstream use cases like RAG, recommendation, and search, with robust zero-shot performance even in specialized domains.

title: SCENT: Aligning Mass Spectra with Molecular Structure for Olfactory Perception
summary: Predicting human olfactory perception from molecular structure has seen remarkable progress, yet these approaches require explicit chemical structure at inference, which is not available in practical sensing settings. We address this gap by exploring direct electron ionization mass spectrometry (EI-MS), a sensing technique that acquires chemically informative fragmentation fingerprints in seconds, as an alternative input modality for olfactory prediction. We contribute Spectrum-to-Chemical Embedding alignmeNT (SCENT), a multi-modal contrastive learning framework that aligns EI-MS representations with pretrained chemical structure embeddings, while requiring only mass spectra at inference. On the multi-label odor descriptor prediction task, SCENT significantly outperforms MS-only baselines and achieves performance comparable to structure-based models, despite requiring no explicit molecular structure at test time. The learned representations also better approximate continuous human perceptual ratings and generalize to real-world lab-measured spectra, suggesting that cross-modal alignment is an effective strategy for grounding analytical spectra in chemical semantics.

AI.tldr: SCENT is a multi-modal contrastive learning framework that aligns electron ionization mass spectrometry data with chemical structure embeddings to predict human olfactory perception without requiring explicit molecular structure at inference time.
AI.motivation: Existing olfactory prediction methods require explicit chemical structure at inference, which is unavailable in practical sensing settings. The paper aims to bridge this gap by using EI-MS spectra—a fast and chemically informative sensing technique—as an alternative input modality.
AI.method: SCENT (Spectrum-to-Chemical Embedding alignmeNT) uses multi-modal contrastive learning to align EI-MS representations with pretrained chemical structure embeddings. Only mass spectra are needed at inference time.
AI.result: SCENT significantly outperforms MS-only baselines on multi-label odor descriptor prediction and achieves performance comparable to structure-based models. It also better approximates continuous human perceptual ratings and generalizes to real-world lab-measured spectra.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal alignment between analytical spectra and chemical semantics is an effective strategy for grounding olfactory perception prediction in practical sensing scenarios where chemical structure is unknown.

title: PlayClass: Automated Play Behaviour Classification in Poultry
summary: Automated monitoring of animal welfare has largely targeted negative indicators, leaving positive welfare behaviours such as play underexplored. To address this gap, we present PlayClass, a pipeline for play-behaviour classification in poultry from top-down pen video. The pipeline leverages long-duration tracking with SAM 3 via YOLO-guided chunk boundaries to minimise identity errors in point-based prompting, and frozen embeddings from image and video foundation models for play action classification. Although handcrafted motion features from tracked masks alone achieved competitive accuracy, V-JEPA 2.1 consistently outperformed all other backbones across model scales, reaching 77.0 macro-averaged F$_1$ when combined with handcrafted features. Despite this result, the dataset remains challenging due to play sub-types sharing similar kinematic profiles with non-play and inter-bird occlusion. Overall, our work provides encouraging evidence towards automated frameworks for play behaviour classification in poultry.

AI.tldr: PlayClass is a pipeline that uses SAM 3, YOLO, and V-JEPA 2.1 to classify play behaviour in poultry from top-down pen videos, achieving 77.0 macro-averaged F1.
AI.motivation: Automated welfare monitoring has focused on negative indicators (e.g., distress), leaving positive behaviours like play underexplored.
AI.method: Long-duration tracking via SAM 3 with YOLO-guided chunk boundaries for point-based prompting, plus frozen embeddings from image/video foundation models for play action classification.
AI.result: V-JEPA 2.1 outperformed all other backbones, achieving 77.0 macro-averaged F1 when combined with handcrafted features. Dataset remains challenging due to kinematic similarity and occlusion.
AI.conclusion: Provides encouraging evidence that automated frameworks can classify play behaviour in poultry, though challenges remain.

title: SQARL: A Size-Agnostic Reinforcement Learning approach for Circuit Allocation in Distributed Quantum Architectures
summary:
The scaling of quantum processors is currently limited by technical challenges such as decoherence and cross-talk. As the number of qubits grows, interference increases the computational noise. Distributed quantum computing addresses these limitations by interconnecting smaller, easier-to-handle quantum processors (cores), but it introduces the challenge of minimizing slow, error-prone inter-core communication. The task of distributing quantum circuits across cores while minimizing communication costs is known as the Qubit Allocation problem. This work focuses on developing a deep learning approach to this problem, emphasizing flexibility to quantum hardware topology and improving state-of-the-art performance.
  Heuristic and non-learning algorithms, such as the Hungarian Qubit Allocation (HQA), currently represent the state of the art. Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches leverage learned allocation policies but often lack flexibility, requiring retraining when hardware configurations change, and they fall short of the solution quality achieved by non-learning methods. However, learning mechanisms could outperform human-crafted heuristics.
  To overcome these limitations, this work proposes a flexible, transformer-based architecture that can handle arbitrary numbers of qubits and cores without retraining. Results show that the trained policy consistently outperforms the previous RL state of the art and narrows the gap between RL and HQA for the most common circuits. It achieves a 33% reduction in allocation cost relative to the HQA for the Cuccaro Adder and 25% on average for random circuits. These findings show that learning-based approaches can effectively match the performance of hand-crafted heuristics, a crucial step towards their application in real-world scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a flexible transformer-based deep learning approach for the Qubit Allocation problem in distributed quantum computing, achieving up to 33% cost reduction over state-of-the-art heuristics while handling arbitrary numbers of qubits and cores without retraining.
AI.motivation: Distributed quantum computing addresses scaling limitations by interconnecting smaller quantum cores, but introduces the challenge of minimizing slow, error-prone inter-core communication. Existing RL-based approaches lack flexibility and fall short of heuristic methods, motivating the need for a more flexible and high-performing learning-based solution.
AI.method: A transformer-based architecture that can handle arbitrary numbers of qubits and cores without retraining, trained via reinforcement learning to optimize the Qubit Allocation problem.
AI.result: Achieves 33% reduction in allocation cost relative to HQA for the Cuccaro Adder and 25% on average for random circuits; consistently outperforms previous RL state of the art and narrows the gap between RL and HQA.
AI.conclusion: Learning-based approaches can effectively match or surpass hand-crafted heuristics for qubit allocation, marking a crucial step toward real-world deployment of learned policies in distributed quantum computing.

title: How and What to Imagine? Visual Thinking in Unified Multimodal Models for Cross-View Spatial Reasoning
summary: Cross-view spatial reasoning remains a weak spot for vision-language models (VLMs): they often reason in language and lose the fine-grained geometry needed for the task. Thinking with images aims to address this by generating an intermediate thinking image, but recent work shows that models often ignore the visual evidence in these traces. We therefore ask how to make visual thinking matter, and what kind of visual thinking works best. We study these questions in unified multimodal models (UMMs), which natively support interleaved image-text generation. For the first question, we propose View Dropout (VDrop), a training-time intervention that hides parts of one input view from the answer span while keeping them visible to the thinking-image tokens. This encourages the model to use the thinking image when answering, instead of relying only on the input views. Once the thinking image is used for answer prediction, we study which type of visual thinking is most effective. We frame this as a learnability-informativeness tradeoff and compare three thinking-image variants: top-down, panoramic, and point-matching renderings. Trained on synthetic scenes and evaluated on five real-world out-of-domain benchmarks, panoramic visual thinking with VDrop is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable, and it achieves the best out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes View Dropout (VDrop), a training technique to make unified multimodal models use visual thinking images for cross-view spatial reasoning, and finds panoramic thinking images with VDrop achieve the best out-of-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: VLMs struggle with cross-view spatial reasoning because they rely on linguistic reasoning and ignore visual evidence in intermediate thinking images. The authors want to make visual thinking actually matter for the model's predictions.
AI.method: Proposes View Dropout (VDrop), a training-time intervention that hides parts of one input view from the answer span while keeping them visible to thinking-image tokens, forcing the model to use the thinking image. Then compares three thinking-image variants (top-down, panoramic, point-matching) under a learnability-informativeness tradeoff framework.
AI.result: Panoramic visual thinking with VDrop is the only configuration that is both informative and learnable, achieving the best out-of-domain generalization across five real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Making visual thinking matter requires explicit training interventions like VDrop, and panoramic renderings strike the best balance between informativeness and learnability for cross-view spatial reasoning tasks.

title: Less is More: Early Stopping Rollout for On-Policy Distillation
summary:
On-policy distillation has recently emerged as a promising alternative to standard sequence-level imitation, training a student by scoring its own rollouts with a teacher model. However, we observe ``Off-policy Teacher Decay'' problem in this paradigm: for the later tokens, with student's earlier trajectory as context that is off-policy to the teacher, the teacher's ability to produce a corrective score would decay, and may fall back to token-completion behavior learned in the pre-training stage. We empirically verify this problem, and we propose Early Stopping Rollout (ESR) to fix it: a simple yet effective distillation strategy that simply restricts the rollout generation to the first
  response tokens. We show that ESR both surpasses the full rollout OPD performance across model size, family, tasks and training regime, and exhibit much higher GPU efficiency and training stability, especially under cross model family scenarios. We further investigate the mechanism behind this surprising performance and discovered "Cascading Alignment" and "Sub-mode Commitment" effect of ESR that may explain why it works effectively and even sometimes exceeding the teacher model performance. Besides, we show that this position-based token selection strategy cannot be fully explainable by KL divergence and entropy signals.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies the "Off-policy Teacher Decay" problem in on-policy distillation for language models, where teacher scoring becomes less effective for later tokens due to off-policy context. The authors propose Early Stopping Rollout (ESR), which restricts rollout to the first K response tokens, demonstrating improved performance, efficiency, and stability across various settings.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the "Off-policy Teacher Decay" problem in on-policy distillation, where teacher models lose effectiveness in scoring later tokens due to off-policy context from student-generated earlier tokens.
AI.method: Early Stopping Rollout (ESR): a simple distillation strategy that restricts rollout generation to only the first K response tokens, preventing the teacher from being exposed to off-policy student contexts for later tokens.
AI.result: ESR surpasses full rollout OPD performance across model size, family, tasks, and training regimes, with higher GPU efficiency and training stability. It sometimes even exceeds teacher model performance, explained by "Cascading Alignment" and "Sub-mode Commitment" effects.
AI.conclusion: Early Stopping Rollout effectively solves the Off-policy Teacher Decay problem, outperforming full rollout strategies, and the position-based token selection strategy cannot be fully explained by KL divergence and entropy signals alone.

title: Q-GeoMem: Question-Guided Geometric Memory for Video Spatial Reasoning
summary: Video spatial reasoning requires accumulating viewpoint-dependent evidence over time while retaining information useful to the question being asked. Existing spatial video-language models improve geometric perception and long-range context modeling, but often treat memory as a generic temporal cache, which can introduce redundant or irrelevant geometry and weaken long-horizon reasoning. We propose \textbf{\ours}, a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning. \ours injects camera-conditioned geometry into visual tokens and maintains two complementary memories: a Fine-Grained Context Bank for recent dense features and camera states, and a Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank for compact long-range evidence. Each candidate frame is scored by the product of Q-Former-based question relevance and novelty with respect to the retained bank; this score is stored and reused during reading, while a capacity-based replacement rule keeps the bank compact. During reasoning, both memories are read before update and adaptively fused with the current frame representation. Experiments on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench show that \ours achieves state-of-the-art performance among evaluated spatial reasoning models, validating the effectiveness of question-guided geometric memory. Ablations further verify the contribution of the proposed evidence scoring mechanism.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes \ours, a question-guided geometric memory framework for video spatial reasoning that maintains two complementary memories (Fine-Grained Context Bank and Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank) with a scoring mechanism for relevance and novelty, achieving SOTA on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial video-language models treat memory as a generic temporal cache, which introduces redundant or irrelevant geometry and weakens long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: \ours injects camera-conditioned geometry into visual tokens, maintains two complementary memories (Fine-Grained Context Bank for recent dense features/camera states, Semantic-Geometric Evidence Bank for compact long-range evidence), scores candidate frames by Q-Former-based question relevance × novelty, and uses capacity-based replacement with adaptive fusion during reasoning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on VSI-Bench and VSTI-Bench among evaluated spatial reasoning models; ablations verify the contribution of the proposed evidence scoring mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Question-guided geometric memory effectively improves video spatial reasoning by maintaining focused, relevant geometric evidence over time.

title: Learning Dynamic Graph Representations through Timespan View Contrasts
summary: The rich information underlying graphs has inspired further investigation of unsupervised graph representation. Existing studies mainly depend on node features and topological properties within static graphs to create self-supervised signals, neglecting the temporal components carried by real-world graph data, such as timestamps of edges. To overcome this limitation, this paper explores how to model temporal evolution on dynamic graphs elegantly. Specifically, we introduce a new inductive bias, namely temporal translation invariance, which illustrates the tendency of the identical node to keep similar labels across different timespans. Based on this assumption, we develop a dynamic graph representation framework CLDG that encourages the node to maintain locally consistent temporal translation invariance through contrastive learning on different timespans. Except for standard CLDG which only considers explicit topological links, our further proposed CLDG++ additionally employs graph diffusion to uncover global contextual correlations between nodes, and designs a multi-scale contrastive learning objective composed of local-local, local-global, and global-global contrasts to enhance representation capabilities. Interestingly, by measuring the consistency between different timespans to shape anomaly indicators, CLDG and CLDG++ are seamlessly integrated with the task of spotting anomalies on dynamic graphs, which has broad applications in many high-impact domains, such as finance, cybersecurity, and healthcare. Experiments demonstrate that CLDG and CLDG++ both exhibit desirable performance in downstream tasks including node classification and dynamic graph anomaly detection. Moreover, CLDG significantly reduces time and space complexity by implicitly exploiting temporal cues instead of complicated sequence models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CLDG and CLDG++, dynamic graph representation learning frameworks that leverage temporal translation invariance (nodes tend to maintain similar labels across timespans) via contrastive learning on different timespans. CLDG uses explicit topological links, while CLDG++ adds graph diffusion for global correlations and multi-scale contrastive learning. Both methods integrate seamlessly with anomaly detection and outperform baselines on node classification and anomaly detection tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised graph representation learning methods rely on static graphs (node features + topology) and neglect temporal components (e.g., edge timestamps) in real-world dynamic graphs. This paper aims to model temporal evolution by introducing temporal translation invariance as an inductive bias.
AI.method: Introduces temporal translation invariance (nodes tend to keep similar labels across timespans). CLDG enforces this via contrastive learning across different timespans on explicit topological links. CLDG++ extends this by adding graph diffusion for global correlations and a multi-scale contrastive objective (local-local, local-global, global-global). Anomaly detection is integrated by measuring consistency between timespans.
AI.result: Both CLDG and CLDG++ achieve desirable performance on node classification and dynamic graph anomaly detection. CLDG significantly reduces time and space complexity by using implicit temporal cues instead of complex sequence models.
AI.conclusion: Modeling temporal evolution via temporal translation invariance and contrastive learning across timespans is effective for dynamic graph representation, enabling strong performance on downstream tasks while reducing computational complexity.

title: PARE: Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient Video Generation
summary: Video Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) generate high-quality videos but demand substantial compute due to wide blocks, deep architectures, and iterative sampling. Recent methods reduce cost by compressing width, depth, or sampling steps, but typically commit to a fixed architecture that cannot adapt to individual inputs or denoising stages. We propose PARE (Pruning and Adaptive Routing for Efficient video generation), which jointly compresses width and depth with structure-aware pruning and input-adaptive routing. For width, we observe that attention heads specialize into spatial and temporal roles, and design importance scoring that accounts for this distinction to prevent motion-critical temporal heads from being pruned prematurely. For depth, we train a lightweight router conditioned on denoising timestep and visual content to dynamically select which blocks to execute at each step, enabling per-input compute adaptation rather than static block removal. A progressive pipeline first recovers width-pruned quality via distillation, then jointly optimizes the student and router to decouple the two learning objectives. Experiments on Wan2.1-14B for both image-to-video and text-to-video generation show that PARE substantially reduces per-step computation while preserving quality across VBench dimensions, and composes with step distillation for further acceleration.

AI.tldr: PARE jointly compresses width (pruning spatial/temporal attention heads) and depth (adaptive block routing) of Video Diffusion Transformers, reducing per-step computation while maintaining video quality.
AI.motivation: Video DiTs are computationally expensive due to wide blocks, deep architectures, and iterative sampling. Existing compression methods use fixed architectures that cannot adapt to individual inputs or denoising stages.
AI.method: (1) Width compression: Structure-aware pruning with importance scoring that distinguishes spatial vs. temporal attention heads to protect motion-critical temporal heads. (2) Depth compression: Lightweight router conditioned on denoising timestep and visual content dynamically selects which blocks to execute per step. (3) Progressive pipeline: distillation for width recovery, then joint optimization of student and router.
AI.result: Applied to Wan2.1-14B for I2V and T2V generation; substantially reduces per-step computation while preserving quality across VBench dimensions; composes with step distillation.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, input-aware compression (both width and depth) is more effective than fixed architecture pruning for efficient video generation.

title: Learning to Orchestrate Agents under Uncertainty
summary: Adaptive orchestration of heterogeneous agents requires making sequential delegation decisions under uncertain and evolving agent behaviour, e.g., coordinating specialised AI models with varying reliability, cost, and response quality. While prior work on agent orchestration focuses on performance or cost, uncertainty in agent reliability and output distributions is typically not modelled explicitly at the orchestration level. In this work, we study the problem of adaptive orchestration of heterogeneous agents under uncertainty, where a meta-controller must decide when to delegate to an agent, accounting for reliability, cost, and uncertainty. We propose BOT-Orch, a lightweight framework that recasts orchestration as a bandit problem over agents, regularized by OT distances between agent output distributions and task-specific reference distributions. We show that the regularised orchestration enjoys $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{T})$ regret under standard assumptions, and provably induces preference ordering among agents with identical mean rewards but differing distributional alignment. Empirically, we demonstrate that BOT-Orch outperforms standard bandit and heuristic baselines in synthetic but adversarial task allocation settings with heterogeneous, non-i.i.d. agent behaviour.

AI.tldr: BOT-Orch is a lightweight framework that reframes adaptive orchestration of heterogeneous agents (e.g., AI models) as a bandit problem regularized by optimal transport distances, achieving theoretical regret guarantees and outperforming baselines in uncertain, adversarial settings.
AI.motivation: Existing agent orchestration methods focus on performance or cost but do not explicitly model uncertainty in agent reliability and output distributions at the orchestration level, limiting adaptive decision-making under evolving agent behavior.
AI.method: BOT-Orch recasts orchestration as a bandit problem over agents, regularized by OT (optimal transport) distances between agent output distributions and task-specific reference distributions.
AI.result: The regularized orchestration achieves O(√T) regret under standard assumptions, provably induces preference ordering among agents with identical mean rewards but differing distributional alignment, and empirically outperforms standard bandit and heuristic baselines in synthetic adversarial settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling uncertainty and distributional alignment in agent orchestration is beneficial both theoretically and empirically, and BOT-Orch provides a principled, lightweight solution.

title: Towards Controllable Image Generation through Representation-Conditioned Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation and editing, but guiding these models to produce specific outputs remains a challenge. Conventional approaches rely on conditioning mechanisms, such as text prompts or semantic maps, which require extensively annotated datasets. In this preliminary work, we explore diffusion models conditioned on representations from a pre-trained self-supervised model. The self-conditioning mechanism not only improves the quality of unconditional image generation, but also provides a representation space that can be used to control the generation. We explore this conditioning space by identifying directions of variations, and demonstrate promising properties in terms of smoothness and disentanglement.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using pre-trained self-supervised models as conditioning mechanisms for diffusion models, enabling quality improvement and controllable generation without requiring annotated datasets.
AI.motivation: Conventional conditioning mechanisms (text prompts, semantic maps) require extensively annotated datasets, which is costly and limits applicability.
AI.method: The authors condition diffusion models on representations from pre-trained self-supervised models, and explore the conditioning space by identifying variation directions.
AI.result: Self-conditioning improves unconditional image generation quality and provides a controllable representation space with smooth and disentangled properties.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised conditioning is a promising alternative to annotated-data-dependent conditioning, offering both quality gains and controllability.

title: Two Speeds of Learning: A Representation-Readout Decomposition of Grokking and Double Descent
summary: Training loss and accuracy are the standard signals used to monitor generalization during deep neural network training. Two well-documented phenomena complicate this picture: in grokking, train loss falls rapidly while test performance improves abruptly only after a long delay; in epoch-wise double descent, train loss decreases monotonically while test loss or error rises and falls. Existing accounts are often task-specific, and a task-agnostic analysis framework for diagnosing and explaining these phenomena across realistic tasks and architectures is missing. We address this challenge by analyzing two competing processes that underlie learning dynamics: representation learning in the encoder and readout calibration in the final classifier. Using tools from representational geometry, neural tangent kernels, and linear probing, we show that both processes are active throughout training, with the fluctuations of their relative speed giving rise to seemingly anomalous generalization dynamics. Applying the representation-readout decomposition to grokking across a wide range of tasks and architectures, we find that the readout is train-biased before grokking onset, and representation learning is gradual but not absent, contrary to the lazy-to-rich account. The framework further provides diagnostic signatures distinguishing spurious from genuine generalization: in a previously reported MNIST grokking example and an epoch-wise double descent example, apparent delayed or non-monotone generalization is shown to arise from representation degradation and readout misalignment induced by non-standard training recipes. Together, these results establish the representation-readout decomposition as a top-down framework for understanding learning dynamics and revealing underlying algorithms for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a representation-readout decomposition framework to explain anomalous generalization phenomena (grokking and epoch-wise double descent) by analyzing two competing processes: representation learning in the encoder and readout calibration in the classifier. The framework reveals that relative speed fluctuations between these processes drive seemingly unusual generalization dynamics.
AI.motivation: Two well-documented phenomena (grokking and epoch-wise double descent) complicate the standard view that training loss/accuracy signal generalization. Existing accounts are task-specific, and a task-agnostic analysis framework for diagnosing these phenomena across realistic tasks and architectures is missing.
AI.method: The authors decompose learning dynamics into representation learning (encoder) and readout calibration (final classifier), using tools from representational geometry, neural tangent kernels, and linear probing to analyze their relative contributions and speed fluctuations.
AI.result: The framework explains grokking across tasks/architectures, showing readout is train-biased before grokking onset and representation learning is gradual but not absent (contrary to the lazy-to-rich account). It also provides diagnostic signatures distinguishing spurious from genuine generalization in MNIST grokking and epoch-wise double descent cases.
AI.conclusion: The representation-readout decomposition serves as a top-down framework for understanding learning dynamics and revealing underlying algorithms for interpretability research.

title: When Eyes Betray AI: Social Gaze Consistency as a Semantic Cue for AI-Generated Image Detection
summary: Recent generative models have largely closed the gap on low-level artifacts - pixel fingerprints, frequency anomalies, upsampling traces - particularly in person-centric and partial-edit settings where the manipulated region is small and surrounded by photometrically authentic content. We introduce Social Gaze Consistency, a high-level semantic cue defined as the mutual coherence of gaze direction, head-eye alignment, and pupil placement between interacting individuals, and show that it constitutes a previously underutilized detection axis orthogonal to existing low-level paradigms. We instantiate this insight through three coupled mechanisms: (i) a controlled diagnostic dataset with region-specific perturbations of gaze-consistent imagery, where strict pair-level grouping forecloses generator-fingerprint memorization as an optimization-time shortcut rather than relying on augmentation; (ii) Block-Compositional Caption Supervision, which holds a single 5-block reasoning skeleton invariant across 1,250 macro-combined captions, decoupling reasoning consistency from surface diversity; (iii) Cross-architecture validation showing the same supervision improves a vision-language backbone (FakeVLM) by +3.7 pp on the COCOAI Interaction subset (balanced accuracy 67.8 -> 71.5) and +1.3 pp on the COCOAI Person subset (83.0 -> 84.3), with consistent gains on a vision-only backbone (Effort), evidencing a backbone-agnostic cue. Real- and fake-class recalls rise simultaneously, ruling out a "predict-all-fake" artifact. A four-step mechanistic account - paired-edit shortcut blocking, hard-to-easy difficulty transfer, CLIP prior preservation, and diffusion-family shared spectral weakness in periocular structure - explains why training on a single inpainter (FLUX.1-Fill) transfers to multi-generator suites. We will release the code upon acceptance to facilitate reproducibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Social Gaze Consistency—a high-level semantic cue based on mutual gaze coherence—as a detection method for AI-generated images, outperforming low-level artifact detectors across multiple architectures and generators.
AI.motivation: Existing generative models have nearly eliminated low-level artifacts (pixel fingerprints, frequency anomalies), especially in person-centric and partial-edit settings. The authors argue that high-level semantic cues like gaze consistency remain underutilized and offer a robust, orthogonal detection axis.
AI.method: Three mechanisms: (i) a controlled diagnostic dataset with region-specific gaze perturbations and strict pair-level grouping to prevent shortcut memorization; (ii) Block-Compositional Caption Supervision (5-block reasoning skeleton invariant across 1,250 captions); (iii) Cross-architecture validation on both vision-language (FakeVLM) and vision-only (Effort) backbones.
AI.result: FakeVLM improves by +3.7pp on COCOAI Interaction (67.8→71.5) and +1.3pp on COCOAI Person (83.0→84.3) in balanced accuracy. Effort shows consistent gains. Real and fake class recalls rise simultaneously. Training on a single inpainter (FLUX.1-Fill) transfers to multi-generator suites.
AI.conclusion: Social Gaze Consistency is a backbone-agnostic, transferable high-level cue for detecting AI-generated images, explained by a four-step mechanistic account (paired-edit shortcut blocking, hard-to-easy transfer, CLIP prior preservation, diffusion-family periocular weakness).

title: Feedforward 3D Editing Learns from Semantic-Part Transformation
summary: 3D editing is a fundamental capability for scalable 3D content creation. While image editing has rapidly evolved toward large-scale feedforward generative paradigms, 3D AI generation remains dominated by training-free editing pipelines. A central challenge of feedforward 3D editing lies in the lack of high-quality paired supervision. Editable 3D assets require simultaneous preservation of geometry, multi-view consistency, structural coherence, and localized edit controllability. Existing 3D editing datasets often rely on independently generated assets, image-mediated reconstruction or narrow edit taxonomies, leading to inaccurate localization, weak preservation, blurred edit boundaries, and limited semantic consistency. In this work, we introduce a new perspective: scalable feedforward 3D editing should be learned from semantic-part transformations. Based on this insight, we propose Pxform, a high-quality 3D editing dataset with over 100K consistent before/after editing pairs across seven edit types. Instead of treating objects as unstructured shapes, our pipeline grounds edits directly in semantic 3D parts. Built upon Pxform, we further propose PartFlow, a feedforward 3D editing network that injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D generative priors. PartFlow introduces mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision to jointly improve edit fidelity and source preservation, while requiring no 3D edit mask during inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that high-quality semantic-part supervision substantially improves scalable 3D editing, enabling PartFlow to achieve state-of-the-art performance on both geometric and appearance editing benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pxform (a 100K+ 3D editing dataset based on semantic-part transformations) and PartFlow (a feedforward 3D editing network using source-aware latent control), achieving state-of-the-art performance by grounding edits in semantic 3D parts rather than unstructured shapes.
AI.motivation: Feedforward 3D editing lacks high-quality paired supervision. Existing datasets rely on independently generated assets or narrow edit taxonomies, leading to inaccurate localization, weak preservation, and limited consistency.
AI.method: 1) Pxform dataset: 100K+ before/after editing pairs across 7 edit types, grounded in semantic 3D parts. 2) PartFlow network: injects source-aware latent control into pretrained 3D generative priors with mask-aware velocity preservation and render-space consistency supervision.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on both geometric and appearance editing benchmarks, demonstrating that semantic-part supervision substantially improves scalable 3D editing.
AI.conclusion: Scalable feedforward 3D editing should be learned from semantic-part transformations, and high-quality semantic-part supervision enables superior editing fidelity and source preservation.

title: ReMoE: Boosting Expert Reuse through Router Fine-Tuning in Memory-Constrained MoE LLM Inference
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary:
Fine-grained Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models sparsely activate only a subset of experts per token, reducing activated computation while maintaining high model capacity. However, in memory-constrained inference scenarios, only a small set of experts can be cached. Experts not in the cache must be fetched from slow external storage (e.g., UFS), leading to frequent evictions and substantial I/O overhead.
  We propose ReMoE, a router fine-tuning framework designed to boost token-wise expert reuse. ReMoE biases the router toward recently selected experts, producing temporally stable routing that better matches cache locality constraints. By increasing short-horizon expert reuse, ReMoE reduces expert fetches from storage without adding inference-time computation.
  Experiments on DeepSeek and Qwen models show that ReMoE improves expert reuse by 26% while maintaining downstream task performance. Real-system evaluations further confirm these benefits, improving output throughput by 8.4% under vLLM GPU-CPU expert offloading and reducing TPOT by 43.6-49.8% under llama.cpp on Jetson Orin NX, corresponding to a 1.77-1.99$\times$ decode speedup across diverse workloads. Checkpoints and usage instructions are available at https://github.com/BUAA-OSCAR/ReMoE.

AI.tldr: ReMoE fine-tunes MoE routers to favor recently selected experts, improving temporal expert reuse by 26%, reducing I/O overhead and boosting throughput by up to 8.4% (vLLM) and 1.77-1.99× decode speedup (llama.cpp on Jetson).
AI.motivation: Memory-constrained inference with MoE models suffers from frequent expert cache evictions and high I/O overhead because only a small subset of experts can be cached, and routing lacks temporal stability.
AI.method: ReMoE fine-tunes the router to bias it toward recently selected experts, producing temporally stable routing that aligns with cache locality constraints, reducing expert fetches without adding inference-time computation.
AI.result: Improves expert reuse by 26% while maintaining downstream task performance; boosts output throughput by 8.4% under vLLM GPU-CPU offloading; reduces TPOT by 43.6-49.8% on Jetson Orin NX (1.77-1.99× decode speedup).
AI.conclusion: Router fine-tuning for temporal expert reuse is an effective, lightweight approach to mitigate I/O bottlenecks in memory-constrained MoE inference, with practical speedups across real systems.

title: Adversarial Dual On-Policy Distillation from Expressive Flow-based Teacher
summary: Learning from demonstrations in embodied control is often cast as behavioral cloning, and recent diffusion or flow-matching policies improve this paradigm by modeling multi-modal expert actions. Yet these methods remain offline supervised learners: the policy is trained only on expert states and receives no corrective signal on the states it actually visits. On-policy distillation (OPD) offers a natural remedy, but standard OPD assumes a strong fixed teacher, which is unavailable in demonstration-only control. We propose \textbf{FA-OPD}, an \emph{adversarial dual on-policy distillation} method in which a Flow Matching (FM) teacher is learned from demonstrations and co-trained with a lightweight MLP student. The teacher provides two complementary signals on student rollouts. The reward channel learns an expert-likeness objective over state-action pairs and drives online exploration through long-horizon policy optimization. The action channel supplies dense local targets at student-visited states, stabilizing exploitation. FA-OPD couples them so that reward distillation enables generalization beyond point-wise demonstrations, while action distillation keeps exploration anchored near expert-like behavior. Across six robot navigation, manipulation, and locomotion benchmarks, FA-OPD beats strong baselines and shows much stronger robustness under noisy or limited demonstrations.

AI.tldr: FA-OPD is an adversarial dual on-policy distillation method with a Flow Matching teacher and MLP student that uses both reward and action channels to improve imitation learning from demonstrations, outperforming baselines across robot tasks.
AI.motivation: Behavioral cloning from demonstrations lacks corrective signals on states the policy actually visits, and on-policy distillation assumes a fixed strong teacher unavailable in demonstration-only settings.
AI.method: FA-OPD trains a Flow Matching teacher from demonstrations alongside an MLP student, providing two complementary signals: a reward channel (expert-likeness for exploration) and an action channel (dense local targets for exploitation), coupled for balanced generalization and stability.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines across six robot navigation, manipulation, and locomotion benchmarks, with stronger robustness under noisy or limited demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial dual on-policy distillation effectively bridges the gap between offline behavioral cloning and online policy improvement without requiring a pre-existing strong teacher.

title: SpatialBench: Is Your Spatial Foundation Model an All-Round Player?
summary: While spatial foundation models have demonstrated impressive performance on standard datasets, a critical question remains: are they truly all-round players capable of generalizing robustly across diverse downstream tasks, arbitrary viewpoints, shifting scene domains, varying input densities, and specific hardware constraints? Answering this overarching question requires a holistic assessment, yet current models are mainly evaluated on specific domains for which they were specifically designed or trained. Such evaluations are intrinsically limited by narrow paradigm coverage, limited scene domains, and arbitrary frame sampling, making it fundamentally difficult to assess their true generalization capabilities. To address this gap, we present SpatialBench, a cross-paradigm, domain-diverse benchmark for spatial foundation models with deterministic sampling. SpatialBench features unprecedented scale and rigorous deterministic design, comprising 19 datasets and 546 scenes across 5 diverse spatial domains. It comprehensively evaluates 41 models across 6 paradigms on 5 task suites under 4 different input density settings. Our extensive evaluation reveals that current models are not yet all-round players, and uncovers crucial insights for future advancement. Specifically, we demonstrate that full-context attention maximizes accuracy while bounded-memory strategies unlock long-sequence scalability. Moreover, our empirical evaluations in challenging embodied and egocentric tasks demonstrate that strict domain alignment and high data quality are far more critical to performance than simple dataset scaling. Furthermore, to address the largest data gap identified in our analysis, we go beyond evaluation by introducing a large-scale dataset, DA-Next-5M, and a strong baseline model, DA-Next, pushing the boundaries of spatial representation learning.

AI.tldr: SpatialBench is a large-scale, cross-paradigm benchmark (19 datasets, 546 scenes, 41 models, 6 paradigms) that reveals current spatial foundation models are not yet robust generalizers; it also introduces DA-Next-5M dataset and DA-Next model to push spatial representation learning forward.
AI.motivation: Existing spatial foundation models are evaluated only on narrow, task-specific domains they were designed for, making it impossible to assess true generalization across diverse downstream tasks, viewpoints, scene domains, input densities, and hardware constraints.
AI.method: SpatialBench is built with 19 datasets and 546 scenes across 5 spatial domains, evaluating 41 models from 6 paradigms on 5 task suites under 4 input density settings using deterministic sampling. Additionally, DA-Next-5M dataset and DA-Next baseline model are introduced.
AI.result: Current models are not yet all-round players. Full-context attention maximizes accuracy; bounded-memory strategies enable long-sequence scalability. Domain alignment and high data quality matter more than dataset scaling for embodied/egocentric tasks.
AI.conclusion: Spatial foundation models lack robust generalization; the paper provides insights for future progress and introduces new data/model to address identified gaps.

title: Mildly Overparameterized ReLU Networks on Orthogonal Data: Incremental Learning and Implicit Bias
summary: The successful training of neural networks hinges on the use of first order optimization methods, yet the theoretical characterization of these methods remains incomplete. This is especially true in settings with mild overparameterization. In this work, we study the gradient flow dynamics of two-layer ReLU networks from small initialization with orthogonal training data. We prove the limiting flow converges to a saddle-to-saddle jump process as the initialization scale tends to zero, revealing an incremental learning phenomenon in which a new neuron activates at each saddle. This analysis recovers the known result of Dana et al. (2025, arXiv:2502.16977) that the network interpolates the training data with high probability as soon as $m \gtrsim \log(n)$, where $m$ is the network width and $n$ is the number of training samples. This incremental process characterization also allows us to derive a novel implicit bias result: the learned interpolator has a squared $\ell_2$-norm scaling as $\sqrt{n}$, which is within a constant factor of the minimal $\ell_2$-norm interpolator. More broadly, our work provides the first rigorous proof of an incremental learning process for ReLU networks, whilst suggesting mildly overparameterized networks can converge to interpolating solutions whose complexity is of the same order as that of the optimal interpolator.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously analyzes gradient flow dynamics of two-layer ReLU networks from small initialization with orthogonal training data, proving convergence to a saddle-to-saddle jump process that reveals incremental learning, and deriving that the learned interpolator's squared ℓ2-norm scales as √n, close to the minimal ℓ2-norm interpolator.
AI.motivation: To theoretically characterize first-order optimization methods for neural networks, especially in mildly overparameterized settings, where existing theory remains incomplete.
AI.method: Studies gradient flow dynamics of two-layer ReLU networks from small initialization with orthogonal training data; proves limiting flow converges to a saddle-to-saddle jump process as initialization scale → 0.
AI.result: (1) Network interpolates training data with high probability when m ≳ log(n). (2) Learned interpolator's squared ℓ2-norm scales as √n, within a constant factor of the minimal ℓ2-norm interpolator.
AI.conclusion: Provides first rigorous proof of incremental learning process for ReLU networks, suggesting mildly overparameterized networks can converge to interpolating solutions whose complexity matches the optimal interpolator's order.

title: G3T Up! Gravity Aligned Coordinate Frames Simplify Pointmap Processing
summary: Modern feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods like VGGT predict pixel-aligned pointmaps in camera-centric coordinate frames. However, this choice of coordinate frame is not always optimal. We propose instead to predict pointmaps in upright, gravity-aligned frames that exploit strong structural cues present in many real-world scenes. Unlike camera-centric frames, gravity-aligned frames share a common vertical axis across viewpoints, reducing the rotational degrees of freedom needed to relate pointmaps to one another. To this end, we introduce the Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer (G3T), fine-tuned from existing models on gravity-aligned 3D data. G3T produces highly accurate gravity-aware predictions, including upright pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses. We further introduce G3T-Long, a submap-based incremental 3D reconstruction pipeline that leverages the reduced rotational degrees of freedom afforded by upright frames to achieve significantly improved reconstruction accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes G3T, a method that predicts 3D pointmaps in gravity-aligned coordinate frames instead of camera-centric frames, improving reconstruction accuracy by leveraging common vertical axes across viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Camera-centric coordinate frames are suboptimal for 3D reconstruction because they lack consistency across viewpoints. Gravity-aligned frames exploit structural cues in real-world scenes and share a common vertical axis, reducing rotational degrees of freedom.
AI.method: G3T (Gravity Grounded Geometry Transformer) is fine-tuned from existing models on gravity-aligned 3D data to predict upright pointmaps and camera-to-gravity poses. G3T-Long extends this with a submap-based incremental reconstruction pipeline.
AI.result: G3T produces highly accurate gravity-aware predictions, and G3T-Long achieves significantly improved reconstruction accuracy by leveraging reduced rotational degrees of freedom.
AI.conclusion: Predicting pointmaps in gravity-aligned frames is superior to camera-centric frames for real-world 3D reconstruction, and the proposed approach substantially improves accuracy.

title: High-Quality Synthetic Financial Time-Series using a GAN-Diffusion Framework
summary: In recent years, financial institutions and firms have increasingly adopted synthetic data to address data scarcity and to generate counterfactual market scenarios. However, reproducing all the statistical properties of financial time series, commonly known as stylized facts, remains an open challenge for many existing general-purpose architectures. In this paper, we present a quality-aware generative framework that combines two classes of generative methods, demonstrating how their integration addresses existing limitations while enhancing the realism of synthetic data. Specifically, we first introduce CoMeTS-GAN (Correlated Multivariate Time Series GAN), a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (C-GAN) designed to jointly generate mid-price and volume time-series for correlated stocks. We then show how our GAN architecture can be incorporated into state-of-the-art diffusion models to enhance the quality of generated correlation structures. Specifically, the GAN's Critic serves as a quality evaluation module that guides the diffusion process, enforcing learned correlation structures in the generated time-series. Our framework offers a lightweight and responsive solution for realistic stock market simulation, explicitly modeling inter-asset correlation structures. We experimentally validate our framework against leading generative architectures, showing that it more effectively captures the stylized facts of stock markets and models inter-asset correlations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a quality-aware generative framework combining CoMeTS-GAN (a C-GAN for correlated multivariate time series) with diffusion models, where the GAN's Critic acts as a quality evaluator to guide diffusion, achieving more realistic synthetic financial time series that better capture stylized facts and inter-asset correlations than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Financial institutions need synthetic data to address data scarcity and generate counterfactual market scenarios, but existing generative architectures struggle to reproduce all statistical properties (stylized facts) of financial time series.
AI.method: A hybrid framework combining CoMeTS-GAN (a Conditional GAN for generating mid-price and volume time-series of correlated stocks) with diffusion models. The GAN's Critic serves as a quality evaluation module to guide the diffusion process, enforcing learned correlation structures.
AI.result: The framework outperforms leading generative architectures in capturing stylized facts of stock markets and modeling inter-asset correlations, offering a lightweight and responsive solution.
AI.conclusion: Integrating GAN-based quality evaluation with diffusion models effectively addresses limitations of individual generative methods, producing more realistic synthetic financial time series with accurate correlation structures.

title: DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference for Quality-Diversity Search
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop Scalable Learning and Optimization for Efficient Multimodal AI Agents (SCALE)
summary: We present DEI: Diversity in Evolutionary Inference, a distributed Quality-Diversity (QD) search framework that assigns heterogeneous large language models (LLMs) as mutation operators across peer nodes communicating with non-blocking collective operations. Unlike homogeneous parallel search, which replicates a single model's inductive biases across all workers, DEI treats each LLM's distinct creative prior as a complementary source of behavioral novelty. Extending the Digital Red Queen framework with DEI, nodes share local optimal solutions at the end of each round to seed the next round's population. This creates cross-model adversarial pressure that drives robustness beyond intra-model self-play. Evaluated on the Core War domain, a competitive programming benchmark in which Redcode warrior programs battle inside a simulated machine, a four-node heterogeneous ensemble (GPT-5.4-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.2, and Claude Haiku 4.5) achieves 124 percent higher merged-archive QD-Score (45.90 vs. 20.46) and 28 percent higher coverage (80.6 percent vs. 63.0 percent of cells) than a single-node baseline at equal total LLM-call budget. The heterogeneous ensemble also outperforms an equally-budgeted homogeneous ensemble on QD-Score, coverage, and held-out solution generality across all four model families. These results provide the first empirical evidence that model diversity, not merely parallelism, is the key driver of gain in distributed LLM-based QD search.

AI.tldr: DEI is a distributed Quality-Diversity search framework that uses heterogeneous LLMs as mutation operators across peer nodes, achieving significantly higher QD-Score and coverage than homogeneous or single-node baselines on Core War benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing parallel QD search uses homogeneous models, replicating a single model's inductive biases across workers. The authors hypothesize that model diversity, not just parallelism, could better drive behavioral novelty and solution robustness in LLM-based evolutionary search.
AI.method: DEI extends the Digital Red Queen framework by having peer nodes with different LLMs (GPT-5.4-mini, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.2, Claude Haiku 4.5) share local optimal solutions after each round via non-blocking collective operations, creating cross-model adversarial pressure.
AI.result: Heterogeneous 4-node ensemble achieved 124% higher QD-Score (45.90 vs 20.46) and 28% higher coverage (80.6% vs 63.0%) over single-node baseline at equal LLM-call budget. It also outperformed homogeneous ensemble across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: Model diversity, not merely parallelism, is the key driver of gain in distributed LLM-based QD search, providing first empirical evidence for this finding.
